# RPCE Practice Questions (generated, RONR 12th-ed.-grounded)

2228 questions across 977 concepts. Types are varied so the same fact resurfaces in different shapes (cloze with hints, applied multiple choice, order-of-precedence ranking and ordering, and motion-characteristic questions). Every question is answerable from what it shows; the RONR (12th ed.) section is cited only in the answer.

Counts by type: Amendability (18), Applied multiple-choice (824), Cloze recall (967), Debatability (18), Precedence (ordering) (77), Precedence (ranking) (154), Precedence (select all) (74), Precedence (which is higher) (60), Second required (18), Vote required (18)

> Reproduce with `python pylib/tools/rpce_generate_questions.py`.

## Domain 1: Motions in General and Main Motions

**1.** `[K0049 · Vote required]` What vote does the motion to Main Motion require to be adopted?
   A. Majority vote
   B. Two-thirds vote
   C. No vote (chair rules / demand)
   D. Unanimous consent

   *Answer:* Majority vote. See RONR (12th ed.) §10:1 — “As explained in 3–6, a main motion is a motion whose introduction brings business before the assembly; such a motion can be made only while no other motion is pending.”

**2.** `[K0050 · Debatability]` Is the motion to Main Motion debatable?
   A. Debatable
   B. Not debatable

   *Answer:* Debatable. See RONR (12th ed.) §10:1 — “As explained in 3–6, a main motion is a motion whose introduction brings business before the assembly; such a motion can be made only while no other motion is pending.”

**3.** `[K0051 · Second required]` Does the motion to Main Motion require a second?
   A. Requires a second
   B. No second required

   *Answer:* Requires a second. See RONR (12th ed.) §10:1 — “As explained in 3–6, a main motion is a motion whose introduction brings business before the assembly; such a motion can be made only while no other motion is pending.”

**4.** `[K0052 · Amendability]` Can the motion to Main Motion be amended?
   A. Amendable
   B. Not amendable

   *Answer:* Amendable. See RONR (12th ed.) §10:1 — “As explained in 3–6, a main motion is a motion whose introduction brings business before the assembly; such a motion can be made only while no other motion is pending.”

**5.** `[C0157 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: A _____ is a motion whose introduction brings business before the assembly.

   *Answer:* main motion — “A main motion is a motion whose introduction brings business before the assembly.”. See RONR (12th ed.) §6:1 — “A main motion is a motion whose introduction brings business before the assembly.”

**6.** `[C0157 · Applied multiple-choice]` Fill in the blank: A _____ is a motion whose introduction brings business before the assembly.
   A. right
   B. select,
   C. main motion
   D. Roll-Call Vote

   *Answer:* C) main motion. See RONR (12th ed.) §6:1 — “A main motion is a motion whose introduction brings business before the assembly.”

**7.** `[C0158 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: It is usual to distinguish between original main motions and _____—which differ principally in the nature of their subject matter.

   *Answer:* incidental main motions — “It is usual to distinguish between original main motions and incidental main motions—which differ principally in the nature of their subject matter.”. See RONR (12th ed.) §6:2 — “It is usual to distinguish between original main motions and incidental main motions—which differ principally in the nature of their subject matter.”

**8.** `[C0158 · Applied multiple-choice]` Fill in the blank: It is usual to distinguish between original main motions and _____—which differ principally in the nature of their subject matter.
   A. Assembly
   B. incidental main motions
   C. of managers,
   D. strike out and insert

   *Answer:* B) incidental main motions. See RONR (12th ed.) §6:2 — “It is usual to distinguish between original main motions and incidental main motions—which differ principally in the nature of their subject matter.”

**9.** `[C0158 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: It should be noted that incidental main motions form a category completely separate from _____—the fourth general class of motions.

   *Answer:* incidental motions — “It should be noted that incidental main motions form a category completely separate from incidental motions—the fourth general class of motions.”. See RONR (12th ed.) §6:2 — “It should be noted that incidental main motions form a category completely separate from incidental motions—the fourth general class of motions.”

**10.** `[C0158 · Applied multiple-choice]` Fill in the blank: It should be noted that incidental main motions form a category completely separate from _____—the fourth general class of motions.
   A. rights
   B. incidental motions
   C. in some way
   D. disturb the assembly

   *Answer:* B) incidental motions. See RONR (12th ed.) §6:2 — “It should be noted that incidental main motions form a category completely separate from incidental motions—the fourth general class of motions.”

**11.** `[C0158 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: It is usual to distinguish between _____ and incidental main motions—which differ principally in the nature of their subject matter.

   *Answer:* original main motions — “It is usual to distinguish between original main motions and incidental main motions—which differ principally in the nature of their subject matter.”. See RONR (12th ed.) §6:2 — “It is usual to distinguish between original main motions and incidental main motions—which differ principally in the nature of their subject matter.”

**12.** `[C0159 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: _____ assist the assembly in treating or disposing of a main motion (and sometimes other motions).

   *Answer:* Subsidiary motions — “Subsidiary motions assist the assembly in treating or disposing of a main motion (and sometimes other motions).”. See RONR (12th ed.) §6:3 — “Subsidiary motions assist the assembly in treating or disposing of a main motion (and sometimes other motions).”

**13.** `[C0159 · Applied multiple-choice]` Fill in the blank: _____ assist the assembly in treating or disposing of a main motion (and sometimes other motions).
   A. precedence of motions
   B. takes precedence
   C. Robert’s Rules
   D. Subsidiary motions

   *Answer:* D) Subsidiary motions. See RONR (12th ed.) §6:3 — “Subsidiary motions assist the assembly in treating or disposing of a main motion (and sometimes other motions).”

**14.** `[C0160 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: In the case of the subsidiary motions only, their distinguishing characteristics as a class are explained _____ this description of the individual motions, since these characteristics will be more easily understood if the material is read in that order.

   *Answer:* after — “In the case of the subsidiary motions only, their distinguishing characteristics as a class are explained after this description of the individual motions, since these characteristics will be more easily understood if the material is read in that order.”. See RONR (12th ed.) §6:4 — “In the case of the subsidiary motions only, their distinguishing characteristics as a class are explained after this description of the individual motions, since these characteristics will be more easily understood if the material is read in that order.”

**15.** `[C0160 · Applied multiple-choice]` Fill in the blank: In the case of the subsidiary motions only, their distinguishing characteristics as a class are explained _____ this description of the individual motions, since these characteristics will be more easily understood if the material is read in that order.
   A. Standard
   B. after
   C. adoption
   D. parliamentary law

   *Answer:* B) after. See RONR (12th ed.) §6:4 — “In the case of the subsidiary motions only, their distinguishing characteristics as a class are explained after this description of the individual motions, since these characteristics will be more easily understood if the material is read in that order.”

**16.** `[C0162 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: (5) They are in order during the entire time that a motion to which they can be applied is pending, except as may be precluded by a previously adopted motion to Limit or Extend _____ or for the Previous Question that is in effect.

   *Answer:* Limits of Debate — “(5) They are in order during the entire time that a motion to which they can be applied is pending, except as may be precluded by a previously adopted motion to Limit or Extend Limits of Debate or for the Previous Question that is in effect.”. See RONR (12th ed.) §6:6 — “(5) They are in order during the entire time that a motion to which they can be applied is pending, except as may be precluded by a previously adopted motion to Limit or Extend Limits of Debate or for the Previous Question that is in effect.”

**17.** `[C0162 · Applied multiple-choice]` Fill in the blank: (5) They are in order during the entire time that a motion to which they can be applied is pending, except as may be precluded by a previously adopted motion to Limit or Extend _____ or for the Previous Question that is in effect.
   A. Limits of Debate
   B. Definitely
   C. plurality vote
   D. unanimous consent

   *Answer:* A) Limits of Debate. See RONR (12th ed.) §6:6 — “(5) They are in order during the entire time that a motion to which they can be applied is pending, except as may be precluded by a previously adopted motion to Limit or Extend Limits of Debate or for the Previous Question that is in effect.”

**18.** `[C0162 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: the adoption of one of them always _____ something to this other motion—that is, changes its status in some way—without adopting or expressly rejecting it.

   *Answer:* does — “the adoption of one of them always does something to this other motion—that is, changes its status in some way—without adopting or expressly rejecting it.”. See RONR (12th ed.) §6:6 — “the adoption of one of them always does something to this other motion—that is, changes its status in some way—without adopting or expressly rejecting it.”

**19.** `[C0162 · Applied multiple-choice]` Fill in the blank: the adoption of one of them always _____ something to this other motion—that is, changes its status in some way—without adopting or expressly rejecting it.
   A. adopt, accept,
   B. first paragraph
   C. officer
   D. does

   *Answer:* D) does. See RONR (12th ed.) §6:6 — “the adoption of one of them always does something to this other motion—that is, changes its status in some way—without adopting or expressly rejecting it.”

**20.** `[C0162 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: Subsidiary motions as a class are distinguished by having all five of the following characteristics: (1) They are always _____ to another motion while it is pending, to aid in treating or disposing of it;

   *Answer:* applied — “Subsidiary motions as a class are distinguished by having all five of the following characteristics: (1) They are always applied to another motion while it is pending, to aid in treating or disposing of it;”. See RONR (12th ed.) §6:6 — “Subsidiary motions as a class are distinguished by having all five of the following characteristics: (1) They are always applied to another motion while it is pending, to aid in treating or disposing of it;”

**21.** `[C0163 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: motions to Postpone Indefinitely, to Amend, and to _____, on the other hand, may have become pending before the motion to Postpone Definitely was moved, but none of these three motions can now be made—except that it is in order to move to amend the motion to Postpone, while it is immediately pending.

   *Answer:* Commit — “motions to Postpone Indefinitely, to Amend, and to Commit, on the other hand, may have become pending before the motion to Postpone Definitely was moved, but none of these three motions can now be made—except that it is in order to move to amend the motion to Postpone, while it is immediately pending.”. See RONR (12th ed.) §6:7 — “motions to Postpone Indefinitely, to Amend, and to Commit, on the other hand, may have become pending before the motion to Postpone Definitely was moved, but none of these three motions can now be made—except that it is in order to move to amend the motion to Postpone, while it is immediately pending.”

**22.** `[C0163 · Applied multiple-choice]` Fill in the blank: motions to Postpone Indefinitely, to Amend, and to _____, on the other hand, may have become pending before the motion to Postpone Definitely was moved, but none of these three motions can now be made—except that it is in order to move to amend the motion to Postpone, while it is immediately pending.
   A. Commit
   B. Example
   C. no more than a
   D. Nominations by the Chair,

   *Answer:* A) Commit. See RONR (12th ed.) §6:7 — “motions to Postpone Indefinitely, to Amend, and to Commit, on the other hand, may have become pending before the motion to Postpone Definitely was moved, but none of these three motions can now be made—except that it is in order to move to amend the motion to Postpone, while it is immediately pending.”

**23.** `[C0163 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: All of the subsidiary motions can be amended except Postpone _____, the Previous Question, and Lay on the Table (which, by the nature of what they propose, do not lend themselves to amendment).

   *Answer:* Indefinitely — “All of the subsidiary motions can be amended except Postpone Indefinitely, the Previous Question, and Lay on the Table (which, by the nature of what they propose, do not lend themselves to amendment).”. See RONR (12th ed.) §6:7 — “All of the subsidiary motions can be amended except Postpone Indefinitely, the Previous Question, and Lay on the Table (which, by the nature of what they propose, do not lend themselves to amendment).”

**24.** `[C0163 · Applied multiple-choice]` Fill in the blank: All of the subsidiary motions can be amended except Postpone _____, the Previous Question, and Lay on the Table (which, by the nature of what they propose, do not lend themselves to amendment).
   A. Indefinitely
   B. opinion
   C. ordinary committees
   D. Rescind and Expunge from the Minutes

   *Answer:* A) Indefinitely. See RONR (12th ed.) §6:7 — “All of the subsidiary motions can be amended except Postpone Indefinitely, the Previous Question, and Lay on the Table (which, by the nature of what they propose, do not lend themselves to amendment).”

**25.** `[C0163 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: The subsidiary motion to _____ is applicable to many other motions in addition to the main motion.

   *Answer:* Amend — “The subsidiary motion to Amend is applicable to many other motions in addition to the main motion.”. See RONR (12th ed.) §6:7 — “The subsidiary motion to Amend is applicable to many other motions in addition to the main motion.”

**26.** `[C0164 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: From these rules, it follows that the motion to Limit or Extend Limits of Debate can be applied to any of the four subsidiary motions of lower rank (but not to the two that rank above it), while the _____ can be applied to any of the five subsidiary motions that rank below it (but not to the one subsidiary motion that ranks above it).

   *Answer:* Previous Question — “From these rules, it follows that the motion to Limit or Extend Limits of Debate can be applied to any of the four subsidiary motions of lower rank (but not to the two that rank above it), while the Previous Question can be applied to any of the five subsidiary motions that rank below it (but not to the one subsidiary motion that ranks above it).”. See RONR (12th ed.) §6:8 — “From these rules, it follows that the motion to Limit or Extend Limits of Debate can be applied to any of the four subsidiary motions of lower rank (but not to the two that rank above it), while the Previous Question can be applied to any of the five subsidiary motions that rank below it (but not to the one subsidiary motion that ranks above it).”

**27.** `[C0164 · Applied multiple-choice]` Fill in the blank: From these rules, it follows that the motion to Limit or Extend Limits of Debate can be applied to any of the four subsidiary motions of lower rank (but not to the two that rank above it), while the _____ can be applied to any of the five subsidiary motions that rank below it (but not to the one subsidiary motion that ranks above it).
   A. Previous Question
   B. Unfinished Business and General Orders
   C. Refer
   D. part

   *Answer:* A) Previous Question. See RONR (12th ed.) §6:8 — “From these rules, it follows that the motion to Limit or Extend Limits of Debate can be applied to any of the four subsidiary motions of lower rank (but not to the two that rank above it), while the Previous Question can be applied to any of the five subsidiary motions that rank below it (but not to the one subsidiary motion that ranks above it).”

**28.** `[C0164 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: From these rules, it follows that the motion to _____ can be applied to any of the four subsidiary motions of lower rank (but not to the two that rank above it), while the Previous Question can be applied to any of the five subsidiary motions that rank below it (but not to the one subsidiary motion that ranks above it).

   *Answer:* Limit or Extend Limits of Debate — “From these rules, it follows that the motion to Limit or Extend Limits of Debate can be applied to any of the four subsidiary motions of lower rank (but not to the two that rank above it), while the Previous Question can be applied to any of the five subsidiary motions that rank below it (but not to the one subsidiary motion that ranks above it).”. See RONR (12th ed.) §6:8 — “From these rules, it follows that the motion to Limit or Extend Limits of Debate can be applied to any of the four subsidiary motions of lower rank (but not to the two that rank above it), while the Previous Question can be applied to any of the five subsidiary motions that rank below it (but not to the one subsidiary motion that ranks above it).”

**29.** `[C0164 · Applied multiple-choice]` Fill in the blank: From these rules, it follows that the motion to _____ can be applied to any of the four subsidiary motions of lower rank (but not to the two that rank above it), while the Previous Question can be applied to any of the five subsidiary motions that rank below it (but not to the one subsidiary motion that ranks above it).
   A. secretary
   B. by paragraph or
   C. Limit or Extend Limits of Debate
   D. control of the assembly

   *Answer:* C) Limit or Extend Limits of Debate. See RONR (12th ed.) §6:8 — “From these rules, it follows that the motion to Limit or Extend Limits of Debate can be applied to any of the four subsidiary motions of lower rank (but not to the two that rank above it), while the Previous Question can be applied to any of the five subsidiary motions that rank below it (but not to the one subsidiary motion that ranks above it).”

**30.** `[C0164 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: The four lowest-ranking subsidiary motions can be debated (except that _____ is undebatable when it is applied to an undebatable motion).

   *Answer:* Amend — “The four lowest-ranking subsidiary motions can be debated (except that Amend is undebatable when it is applied to an undebatable motion).”. See RONR (12th ed.) §6:8 — “The four lowest-ranking subsidiary motions can be debated (except that Amend is undebatable when it is applied to an undebatable motion).”

**31.** `[C0166 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: Unlike subsidiary or incidental motions, _____ do not relate to the pending business, but have to do with special matters of immediate and overriding importance which, without debate, should be allowed to interrupt the consideration of anything else.

   *Answer:* privileged motions — “Unlike subsidiary or incidental motions, privileged motions do not relate to the pending business, but have to do with special matters of immediate and overriding importance which, without debate, should be allowed to interrupt the consideration of anything else.”. See RONR (12th ed.) §6:11 — “Unlike subsidiary or incidental motions, privileged motions do not relate to the pending business, but have to do with special matters of immediate and overriding importance which, without debate, should be allowed to interrupt the consideration of anything else.”

**32.** `[C0166 · Applied multiple-choice]` Fill in the blank: Unlike subsidiary or incidental motions, _____ do not relate to the pending business, but have to do with special matters of immediate and overriding importance which, without debate, should be allowed to interrupt the consideration of anything else.
   A. delegates
   B. Fix the Time to Which to
   C. privileged motions
   D. first paragraph

   *Answer:* C) privileged motions. See RONR (12th ed.) §6:11 — “Unlike subsidiary or incidental motions, privileged motions do not relate to the pending business, but have to do with special matters of immediate and overriding importance which, without debate, should be allowed to interrupt the consideration of anything else.”

**39.** `[C0176 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: While a series consisting of a main motion and a number of subsidiary or privileged motions is being considered, it is possible for some of the incidental motions—such as a Point of Order, an _____, or a Division of the Assembly—to arise more than once, in connection with different motions in the series.

   *Answer:* Appeal — “While a series consisting of a main motion and a number of subsidiary or privileged motions is being considered, it is possible for some of the incidental motions—such as a Point of Order, an Appeal, or a Division of the Assembly—to arise more than once, in connection with different motions in the series.”. See RONR (12th ed.) §6:22 — “While a series consisting of a main motion and a number of subsidiary or privileged motions is being considered, it is possible for some of the incidental motions—such as a Point of Order, an Appeal, or a Division of the Assembly—to arise more than once, in connection with different motions in the series.”

   B. Appeal

   *Answer:* B) Appeal. **50.** `[C0237 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: The division of main motions into _____ and incidental main motions was mentioned in 6 and is discussed below.

   *Answer:* original — “The division of main motions into original and incidental main motions was mentioned in 6 and is discussed below.”. See RONR (12th ed.) §10:2 — “The division of main motions into original and incidental main motions was mentioned in 6 and is discussed below.”

**51.** `[C0237 · Applied multiple-choice]` Fill in the blank: The division of main motions into _____ and incidental main motions was mentioned in 6 and is discussed below.
   A. original
   B. Robert’s Rules
   C. Insert, or Add
   D. secondary amendment to strike out

   *Answer:* A) original. See RONR (12th ed.) §10:2 — “The division of main motions into original and incidental main motions was mentioned in 6 and is discussed below.”

**52.** `[C0237 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: The division of main motions into original and _____ main motions was mentioned in 6 and is discussed below.

   *Answer:* incidental — “The division of main motions into original and incidental main motions was mentioned in 6 and is discussed below.”. See RONR (12th ed.) §10:2 — “The division of main motions into original and incidental main motions was mentioned in 6 and is discussed below.”

**53.** `[C0237 · Applied multiple-choice]` Fill in the blank: The division of main motions into original and _____ main motions was mentioned in 6 and is discussed below.
   A. not pending
   B. yields to
   C. incidental
   D. Adjourn

   *Answer:* C) incidental. See RONR (12th ed.) §10:2 — “The division of main motions into original and incidental main motions was mentioned in 6 and is discussed below.”

**54.** `[C0238 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: or “… to adopt the following resolution: ‘_____, That the Northridge Improvement Association oppose a municipal tax increase at this time.’” It may be more suitable for an original main motion to be made orally, or to be submitted in writing, depending on its length, complexity, or importance.

   *Answer:* Resolved — “or “… to adopt the following resolution: ‘Resolved, That the Northridge Improvement Association oppose a municipal tax increase at this time.’” It may be more suitable for an original main motion to be made orally, or to be submitted in writing, depending on its length, complexity, or importance.”. See RONR (12th ed.) §10:3 — “or “… to adopt the following resolution: ‘Resolved, That the Northridge Improvement Association oppose a municipal tax increase at this time.’” It may be more suitable for an original main motion to be made orally, or to be submitted in writing, depending on its length, complexity, or importance.”

**55.** `[C0238 · Applied multiple-choice]` Fill in the blank: or “… to adopt the following resolution: ‘_____, That the Northridge Improvement Association oppose a municipal tax increase at this time.’” It may be more suitable for an original main motion to be made orally, or to be submitted in writing, depending on its length, complexity, or importance.
   A. Resolved
   B. and
   C. preferential voting
   D. immediately

   *Answer:* A) Resolved. See RONR (12th ed.) §10:3 — “or “… to adopt the following resolution: ‘Resolved, That the Northridge Improvement Association oppose a municipal tax increase at this time.’” It may be more suitable for an original main motion to be made orally, or to be submitted in writing, depending on its length, complexity, or importance.”

**56.** `[C0238 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: An _____ is a main motion that introduces a substantive question as a new subject.

   *Answer:* original main motion — “An original main motion is a main motion that introduces a substantive question as a new subject.”. See RONR (12th ed.) §10:3 — “An original main motion is a main motion that introduces a substantive question as a new subject.”

**57.** `[C0238 · Applied multiple-choice]` Fill in the blank: An _____ is a main motion that introduces a substantive question as a new subject.
   A. recess
   B. original main motion
   C. Naming Members to a Special
   D. unfinished business,

   *Answer:* B) original main motion. See RONR (12th ed.) §10:3 — “An original main motion is a main motion that introduces a substantive question as a new subject.”

**58.** `[C0239 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: An _____ is a main motion that is incidental to or relates to the business of the assembly, or its past or future action.

   *Answer:* incidental main motion — “An incidental main motion is a main motion that is incidental to or relates to the business of the assembly, or its past or future action.”. See RONR (12th ed.) §10:4 — “An incidental main motion is a main motion that is incidental to or relates to the business of the assembly, or its past or future action.”

**59.** `[C0239 · Applied multiple-choice]` Fill in the blank: An _____ is a main motion that is incidental to or relates to the business of the assembly, or its past or future action.
   A. mass meeting,
   B. incidental main motion
   C. Robert’s Rules of
   D. within

   *Answer:* B) incidental main motion. See RONR (12th ed.) §10:4 — “An incidental main motion is a main motion that is incidental to or relates to the business of the assembly, or its past or future action.”

**60.** `[C0240 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: An incidental main motion involving a subject already entered into might be a motion to adopt recommendations which a committee has prepared upon instructions (not relating to a referred motion), or a motion to _____ action taken at a meeting when no quorum was present.

   *Answer:* ratify — “An incidental main motion involving a subject already entered into might be a motion to adopt recommendations which a committee has prepared upon instructions (not relating to a referred motion), or a motion to ratify action taken at a meeting when no quorum was present.”. See RONR (12th ed.) §10:5 — “An incidental main motion involving a subject already entered into might be a motion to adopt recommendations which a committee has prepared upon instructions (not relating to a referred motion), or a motion to ratify action taken at a meeting when no quorum was present.”

**61.** `[C0240 · Applied multiple-choice]` Fill in the blank: An incidental main motion involving a subject already entered into might be a motion to adopt recommendations which a committee has prepared upon instructions (not relating to a referred motion), or a motion to _____ action taken at a meeting when no quorum was present.
   A. from the primary amendment
   B. Crestwood
   C. ratify
   D. Definitely

   *Answer:* C) ratify. See RONR (12th ed.) §10:5 — “An incidental main motion involving a subject already entered into might be a motion to adopt recommendations which a committee has prepared upon instructions (not relating to a referred motion), or a motion to ratify action taken at a meeting when no quorum was present.”

**62.** `[C0240 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: An incidental main motion involving a subject already entered into might be a motion to _____ recommendations which a committee has prepared upon instructions (not relating to a referred motion), or a motion to ratify action taken at a meeting when no quorum was present.

   *Answer:* adopt — “An incidental main motion involving a subject already entered into might be a motion to adopt recommendations which a committee has prepared upon instructions (not relating to a referred motion), or a motion to ratify action taken at a meeting when no quorum was present.”. See RONR (12th ed.) §10:5 — “An incidental main motion involving a subject already entered into might be a motion to adopt recommendations which a committee has prepared upon instructions (not relating to a referred motion), or a motion to ratify action taken at a meeting when no quorum was present.”

**63.** `[C0240 · Applied multiple-choice]` Fill in the blank: An incidental main motion involving a subject already entered into might be a motion to _____ recommendations which a committee has prepared upon instructions (not relating to a referred motion), or a motion to ratify action taken at a meeting when no quorum was present.

**64.** `[C0240 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: An example of an incidental main motion relating to procedure without reference to an item of business would be a motion to take a _____, made when no business is pending, or a motion to place a special limit on the length of speeches throughout a meeting.

   *Answer:* recess — “An example of an incidental main motion relating to procedure without reference to an item of business would be a motion to take a recess, made when no business is pending, or a motion to place a special limit on the length of speeches throughout a meeting.”. See RONR (12th ed.) §10:5 — “An example of an incidental main motion relating to procedure without reference to an item of business would be a motion to take a recess, made when no business is pending, or a motion to place a special limit on the length of speeches throughout a meeting.”

Referring to the Table of Rules Relating to Motions on pages t6–t33, most of the motions listed as “main”—with the exception of No.”. Referring to the Table of Rules Relating to Motions on pages t6–t33, most of the motions listed as “main”—with the exception of No.”

**74.** `[C0245 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: A motion whose only effect is to propose that the assembly _____ from doing something should not be offered if the same result can be accomplished by offering no motion at all.

   *Answer:* refrain — “A motion whose only effect is to propose that the assembly refrain from doing something should not be offered if the same result can be accomplished by offering no motion at all.”. See RONR (12th ed.) §10:11 — “A motion whose only effect is to propose that the assembly refrain from doing something should not be offered if the same result can be accomplished by offering no motion at all.”

**75.** `[C0245 · Applied multiple-choice]` Fill in the blank: A motion whose only effect is to propose that the assembly _____ from doing something should not be offered if the same result can be accomplished by offering no motion at all.
   A. incidental motions
   B. Definitely
   C. refrain
   D. originating in the committee

   *Answer:* C) refrain. See RONR (12th ed.) §10:11 — “A motion whose only effect is to propose that the assembly refrain from doing something should not be offered if the same result can be accomplished by offering no motion at all.”

**76.** `[C0247 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: As previously stated, a main motion—particularly an original main motion—is frequently offered as a _____, either because of its importance or because of its length or complexity.

   *Answer:* resolution — “As previously stated, a main motion—particularly an original main motion—is frequently offered as a resolution, either because of its importance or because of its length or complexity.”. See RONR (12th ed.) §10:13 — “As previously stated, a main motion—particularly an original main motion—is frequently offered as a resolution, either because of its importance or because of its length or complexity.”

**77.** `[C0247 · Applied multiple-choice]` Fill in the blank: As previously stated, a main motion—particularly an original main motion—is frequently offered as a _____, either because of its importance or because of its length or complexity.

   *Answer:* C) resolution. See RONR (12th ed.) §10:13 — “As previously stated, a main motion—particularly an original main motion—is frequently offered as a resolution, either because of its importance or because of its length or complexity.”

**78.** `[C0247 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: for example, “That the Merchants’ Association sponsor an essay contest open to high school students of the city, to be conducted according to the following specifications:.…” If put in the form of a resolution, the preceding example would be written, “Resolved, That the Merchants’ Association sponsor an essay contest.…” In a resolution, the name of the adopting organization can also be made a part of the enacting words, as in “_____ International Benevolent Association in convention assembled, That.…”

   *Answer:* Resolved by the — “for example, “That the Merchants’ Association sponsor an essay contest open to high school students of the city, to be conducted according to the following specifications:.…” If put in the form of a resolution, the preceding example would be written, “Resolved, That the Merchants’ Association sponsor an essay contest.…” In a resolution, the name of the adopting organization can also be made a part of the enacting words, as in “Resolved by the International Benevolent Association in convention assembled, That.…””. See RONR (12th ed.) §10:13 — “for example, “That the Merchants’ Association sponsor an essay contest open to high school students of the city, to be conducted according to the following specifications:.…” If put in the form of a resolution, the preceding example would be written, “Resolved, That the Merchants’ Association sponsor an essay contest.…” In a resolution, the name of the adopting organization can also be made a part of the enacting words, as in “Resolved by the International Benevolent Association in convention assembled, That.…””

**81.** `[C0249 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: If the resolution is offered in a mass meeting (or in any meeting where there is no established organization whose act the adopted resolution would become), it may begin, “_____, That it is the sense of this meeting that…” A resolution can consist of more than one resolving clause, as in the following example:

   *Answer:* Resolved — “If the resolution is offered in a mass meeting (or in any meeting where there is no established organization whose act the adopted resolution would become), it may begin, “Resolved, That it is the sense of this meeting that…” A resolution can consist of more than one resolving clause, as in the following example:”. **83.** `[C0250 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: A preamble consists of one or more clauses beginning “Whereas,.…” _____ rule nor custom requires a resolution to have a preamble, and one should not be used merely for the sake of form.

   *Answer:* It should be emphasized that neither — “A preamble consists of one or more clauses beginning “Whereas,.…” It should be emphasized that neither rule nor custom requires a resolution to have a preamble, and one should not be used merely for the sake of form.”. See RONR (12th ed.) §10:16 — “A preamble consists of one or more clauses beginning “Whereas,.…” It should be emphasized that neither rule nor custom requires a resolution to have a preamble, and one should not be used merely for the sake of form.”

   D. in some way

**87.** `[C0252 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: When the Previous _____ (16) is ordered on the resolution before the preamble has been considered for amendment, however, the Previous Question does not apply to the preamble, which becomes open to debate and amendment unless the Previous Question is then separately ordered on it also.

See RONR (12th ed.) §10:19 — “When the Previous Question (16) is ordered on the resolution before the preamble has been considered for amendment, however, the Previous Question does not apply to the preamble, which becomes open to debate and amendment unless the Previous Question is then separately ordered on it also.”
