   D. constitution

   *Answer:* A) Resolved. See RONR (12th ed.) §10:22 — “The word “Resolved” is underlined or printed in italics, and is followed by a comma and the word “That”—which begins with a capital “T.” If there is more than one resolving clause, each of them should be a separate paragraph.”

**96.** `[C0256 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: In organizations with employees, the assembly or the board can give instructions to an employee in the form of an order, which is written just as a resolution except that the word “_____” is used in place of the word “Resolved.” An example would be: “Ordered, That the steward obtain impoundment of all unauthorized vehicles found parked on the club premises.”

   *Answer:* Ordered — “In organizations with employees, the assembly or the board can give instructions to an employee in the form of an order, which is written just as a resolution except that the word “Ordered” is used in place of the word “Resolved.” An example would be: “Ordered, That the steward obtain impoundment of all unauthorized vehicles found parked on the club premises.””. See RONR (12th ed.) §10:24 — “In organizations with employees, the assembly or the board can give instructions to an employee in the form of an order, which is written just as a resolution except that the word “Ordered” is used in place of the word “Resolved.” An example would be: “Ordered, That the steward obtain impoundment of all unauthorized vehicles found parked on the club premises.””

**97.** `[C0256 · Applied multiple-choice]` Fill in the blank: In organizations with employees, the assembly or the board can give instructions to an employee in the form of an order, which is written just as a resolution except that the word “_____” is used in place of the word “Resolved.” An example would be: “Ordered, That the steward obtain impoundment of all unauthorized vehicles found parked on the club premises.”
   A. assembly
   B. receive
   C. Ordered
   D. germane

   *Answer:* C) Ordered. See RONR (12th ed.) §10:24 — “In organizations with employees, the assembly or the board can give instructions to an employee in the form of an order, which is written just as a resolution except that the word “Ordered” is used in place of the word “Resolved.” An example would be: “Ordered, That the steward obtain impoundment of all unauthorized vehicles found parked on the club premises.””

**98.** `[C0256 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: In organizations with employees, the assembly or the board can give instructions to an employee in the form of an order, which is written just as a resolution except that the word “_____” is used in place of the word “Resolved.” An example would be: “Ordered, That the steward obtain impoundment of all unauthorized vehicles found parked on the club premises.”

   *Answer:* Ordered — “In organizations with employees, the assembly or the board can give instructions to an employee in the form of an order, which is written just as a resolution except that the word “Ordered” is used in place of the word “Resolved.” An example would be: “Ordered, That the steward obtain impoundment of all unauthorized vehicles found parked on the club premises.””. See RONR (12th ed.) §10:24 — “In organizations with employees, the assembly or the board can give instructions to an employee in the form of an order, which is written just as a resolution except that the word “Ordered” is used in place of the word “Resolved.” An example would be: “Ordered, That the steward obtain impoundment of all unauthorized vehicles found parked on the club premises.””

**99.** `[C0256 · Applied multiple-choice]` Fill in the blank: In organizations with employees, the assembly or the board can give instructions to an employee in the form of an order, which is written just as a resolution except that the word “_____” is used in place of the word “Resolved.” An example would be: “Ordered, That the steward obtain impoundment of all unauthorized vehicles found parked on the club premises.”
   A. Ordered
   B. Resolved,
   C. For Permission (or Leave) to
   D. deliberative

   *Answer:* A) Ordered. See RONR (12th ed.) §10:24 — “In organizations with employees, the assembly or the board can give instructions to an employee in the form of an order, which is written just as a resolution except that the word “Ordered” is used in place of the word “Resolved.” An example would be: “Ordered, That the steward obtain impoundment of all unauthorized vehicles found parked on the club premises.””

**100.** `[C0256 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: In organizations with employees, the assembly or the board can give instructions to an employee in the form of an _____, which is written just as a resolution except that the word “Ordered” is used in place of the word “Resolved.” An example would be: “Ordered, That the steward obtain impoundment of all unauthorized vehicles found parked on the club premises.”

   *Answer:* order — “In organizations with employees, the assembly or the board can give instructions to an employee in the form of an order, which is written just as a resolution except that the word “Ordered” is used in place of the word “Resolved.” An example would be: “Ordered, That the steward obtain impoundment of all unauthorized vehicles found parked on the club premises.””. See RONR (12th ed.) §10:24 — “In organizations with employees, the assembly or the board can give instructions to an employee in the form of an order, which is written just as a resolution except that the word “Ordered” is used in place of the word “Resolved.” An example would be: “Ordered, That the steward obtain impoundment of all unauthorized vehicles found parked on the club premises.””

**101.** `[C0257 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: In the case of a series of resolutions relating to a single subject, if members desire one or more of the resolutions to be considered separately, the motion for _____ (27) must be made and adopted by a majority vote.

   *Answer:* Division of a Question — “In the case of a series of resolutions relating to a single subject, if members desire one or more of the resolutions to be considered separately, the motion for Division of a Question (27) must be made and adopted by a majority vote.”. See RONR (12th ed.) §10:25 — “In the case of a series of resolutions relating to a single subject, if members desire one or more of the resolutions to be considered separately, the motion for Division of a Question (27) must be made and adopted by a majority vote.”

**102.** `[C0257 · Applied multiple-choice]` Fill in the blank: In the case of a series of resolutions relating to a single subject, if members desire one or more of the resolutions to be considered separately, the motion for _____ (27) must be made and adopted by a majority vote.
   A. Resolved,
   B. primary amendment
   C. takes precedence
   D. Division of a Question

   *Answer:* D) Division of a Question. See RONR (12th ed.) §10:25 — “In the case of a series of resolutions relating to a single subject, if members desire one or more of the resolutions to be considered separately, the motion for Division of a Question (27) must be made and adopted by a majority vote.”

**103.** `[C0259 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: The rules relating to the motions to Suspend the Rules (25), Take from the Table (34), Rescind or Amend Something Previously Adopted (35), _____ Committee (36), and Reconsider (37), and the rules relating to the renewal of motions (38), should be read in connection with the application of these methods.

   *Answer:* Discharge a — “The rules relating to the motions to Suspend the Rules (25), Take from the Table (34), Rescind or Amend Something Previously Adopted (35), Discharge a Committee (36), and Reconsider (37), and the rules relating to the renewal of motions (38), should be read in connection with the application of these methods.”. See RONR (12th ed.) §10:27 — “The rules relating to the motions to Suspend the Rules (25), Take from the Table (34), Rescind or Amend Something Previously Adopted (35), Discharge a Committee (36), and Reconsider (37), and the rules relating to the renewal of motions (38), should be read in connection with the application of these methods.”

**104.** `[C0259 · Applied multiple-choice]` Fill in the blank: The rules relating to the motions to Suspend the Rules (25), Take from the Table (34), Rescind or Amend Something Previously Adopted (35), _____ Committee (36), and Reconsider (37), and the rules relating to the renewal of motions (38), should be read in connection with the application of these methods.
   A. Discharge a
   B. on the Table
   C. Postpone
   D. Principles Governing the

   *Answer:* A) Discharge a. See RONR (12th ed.) §10:27 — “The rules relating to the motions to Suspend the Rules (25), Take from the Table (34), Rescind or Amend Something Previously Adopted (35), Discharge a Committee (36), and Reconsider (37), and the rules relating to the renewal of motions (38), should be read in connection with the application of these methods.”

**105.** `[C0259 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: The rules relating to the motions to Suspend the Rules (25), Take from the Table (34), Rescind or Amend Something Previously Adopted (35), Discharge a Committee (36), and _____ (37), and the rules relating to the renewal of motions (38), should be read in connection with the application of these methods.

   *Answer:* Reconsider — “The rules relating to the motions to Suspend the Rules (25), Take from the Table (34), Rescind or Amend Something Previously Adopted (35), Discharge a Committee (36), and Reconsider (37), and the rules relating to the renewal of motions (38), should be read in connection with the application of these methods.”. See RONR (12th ed.) §10:27 — “The rules relating to the motions to Suspend the Rules (25), Take from the Table (34), Rescind or Amend Something Previously Adopted (35), Discharge a Committee (36), and Reconsider (37), and the rules relating to the renewal of motions (38), should be read in connection with the application of these methods.”

**106.** `[C0259 · Applied multiple-choice]` Fill in the blank: The rules relating to the motions to Suspend the Rules (25), Take from the Table (34), Rescind or Amend Something Previously Adopted (35), Discharge a Committee (36), and _____ (37), and the rules relating to the renewal of motions (38), should be read in connection with the application of these methods.
   A. Reconsider
   B. secondary amendment
   C. amendment of the first degree
   D. Ordered

   *Answer:* A) Reconsider. See RONR (12th ed.) §10:27 — “The rules relating to the motions to Suspend the Rules (25), Take from the Table (34), Rescind or Amend Something Previously Adopted (35), Discharge a Committee (36), and Reconsider (37), and the rules relating to the renewal of motions (38), should be read in connection with the application of these methods.”

**107.** `[C0259 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: The rules relating to the motions to Suspend the Rules (25), Take from the Table (34), Rescind or _____ (35), Discharge a Committee (36), and Reconsider (37), and the rules relating to the renewal of motions (38), should be read in connection with the application of these methods.

   *Answer:* Amend Something Previously Adopted — “The rules relating to the motions to Suspend the Rules (25), Take from the Table (34), Rescind or Amend Something Previously Adopted (35), Discharge a Committee (36), and Reconsider (37), and the rules relating to the renewal of motions (38), should be read in connection with the application of these methods.”. See RONR (12th ed.) §10:27 — “The rules relating to the motions to Suspend the Rules (25), Take from the Table (34), Rescind or Amend Something Previously Adopted (35), Discharge a Committee (36), and Reconsider (37), and the rules relating to the renewal of motions (38), should be read in connection with the application of these methods.”

**108.** `[C0267 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: A subsidiary or incidental motion _____ to a main question if it is related to the main question in such a way that—once introduced—it must be decided before the main question can be decided.

   *Answer:* adhere — “A subsidiary or incidental motion adhere to a main question if it is related to the main question in such a way that—once introduced—it must be decided before the main question can be decided.”. See RONR (12th ed.) §10:35 — “A subsidiary or incidental motion adhere to a main question if it is related to the main question in such a way that—once introduced—it must be decided before the main question can be decided.”

**109.** `[C0267 · Applied multiple-choice]` Fill in the blank: A subsidiary or incidental motion _____ to a main question if it is related to the main question in such a way that—once introduced—it must be decided before the main question can be decided.
   A. Call of the House,
   B. house of delegates
   C. Question
   D. adhere

   *Answer:* D) adhere. See RONR (12th ed.) §10:35 — “A subsidiary or incidental motion adhere to a main question if it is related to the main question in such a way that—once introduced—it must be decided before the main question can be decided.”

**110.** `[C0268 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: Main Motions Brought Up by Means of a _____ or by Raising a Question of Privilege.

   *Answer:* Call for the Orders of the Day — “Main Motions Brought Up by Means of a Call for the Orders of the Day or by Raising a Question of Privilege.”. See RONR (12th ed.) §10:36 — “Main Motions Brought Up by Means of a Call for the Orders of the Day or by Raising a Question of Privilege.”

**111.** `[C0268 · Applied multiple-choice]` Fill in the blank: Main Motions Brought Up by Means of a _____ or by Raising a Question of Privilege.
   A. standing rule of
   B. legal
   C. Call for the Orders of the Day
   D. question is pending

   *Answer:* C) Call for the Orders of the Day. See RONR (12th ed.) §10:36 — “Main Motions Brought Up by Means of a Call for the Orders of the Day or by Raising a Question of Privilege.”

**112.** `[C0268 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: Certain questions may come before the assembly with the status of main motions after having interrupted other business, however, if they are brought up by means of either of the two lowest-ranking _____ motions.

   *Answer:* privileged — “Certain questions may come before the assembly with the status of main motions after having interrupted other business, however, if they are brought up by means of either of the two lowest-ranking privileged motions.”. See RONR (12th ed.) §10:36 — “Certain questions may come before the assembly with the status of main motions after having interrupted other business, however, if they are brought up by means of either of the two lowest-ranking privileged motions.”

**113.** `[C0268 · Applied multiple-choice]` Fill in the blank: Certain questions may come before the assembly with the status of main motions after having interrupted other business, however, if they are brought up by means of either of the two lowest-ranking _____ motions.
   A. extending
   B. undebatable
   C. clerk,
   D. privileged

   *Answer:* D) privileged. See RONR (12th ed.) §10:36 — “Certain questions may come before the assembly with the status of main motions after having interrupted other business, however, if they are brought up by means of either of the two lowest-ranking privileged motions.”

**114.** `[C0268 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: As a result of the application of one of these devices to interrupt a pending question, an order of the day (that is, another question previously set as due to come up automatically at the time) may be taken up, or an urgent motion relating to the privileges of the assembly or of a member may be admitted as a _____ to be entertained immediately.

   *Answer:* question of privilege — “As a result of the application of one of these devices to interrupt a pending question, an order of the day (that is, another question previously set as due to come up automatically at the time) may be taken up, or an urgent motion relating to the privileges of the assembly or of a member may be admitted as a question of privilege to be entertained immediately.”. See RONR (12th ed.) §10:36 — “As a result of the application of one of these devices to interrupt a pending question, an order of the day (that is, another question previously set as due to come up automatically at the time) may be taken up, or an urgent motion relating to the privileges of the assembly or of a member may be admitted as a question of privilege to be entertained immediately.”

**115.** `[C0269 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: Below for the main motion, and in each of the succeeding sections covering the different motions of parliamentary law (11–37), is a subsection headed _____ giving a script showing the words to be spoken by a member making the motion and by the chair in stating and putting the question on it.

   *Answer:* Form and Example — “Below for the main motion, and in each of the succeeding sections covering the different motions of parliamentary law (11–37), is a subsection headed Form and Example giving a script showing the words to be spoken by a member making the motion and by the chair in stating and putting the question on it.”. See RONR (12th ed.) §10:37 — “Below for the main motion, and in each of the succeeding sections covering the different motions of parliamentary law (11–37), is a subsection headed Form and Example giving a script showing the words to be spoken by a member making the motion and by the chair in stating and putting the question on it.”

**116.** `[C0269 · Applied multiple-choice]` Fill in the blank: Below for the main motion, and in each of the succeeding sections covering the different motions of parliamentary law (11–37), is a subsection headed _____ giving a script showing the words to be spoken by a member making the motion and by the chair in stating and putting the question on it.
   A. Form and Example
   B. Platform Committee
   C. Commit
   D. Discharge a Committee

   *Answer:* A) Form and Example. See RONR (12th ed.) §10:37 — “Below for the main motion, and in each of the succeeding sections covering the different motions of parliamentary law (11–37), is a subsection headed Form and Example giving a script showing the words to be spoken by a member making the motion and by the chair in stating and putting the question on it.”

**117.** `[C0275 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: The term _____ (or notice), as applied to necessary conditions for the adoption of certain motions, has a particular meaning in parliamentary law.

   *Answer:* previous notice — “The term previous notice (or notice), as applied to necessary conditions for the adoption of certain motions, has a particular meaning in parliamentary law.”. See RONR (12th ed.) §10:44 — “The term previous notice (or notice), as applied to necessary conditions for the adoption of certain motions, has a particular meaning in parliamentary law.”

**118.** `[C0275 · Applied multiple-choice]` Fill in the blank: The term _____ (or notice), as applied to necessary conditions for the adoption of certain motions, has a particular meaning in parliamentary law.
   A. the one
   B. Robert’s Rules of Order Newly Revised
   C. previous notice
   D. house of delegates

   *Answer:* C) previous notice. See RONR (12th ed.) §10:44 — “The term previous notice (or notice), as applied to necessary conditions for the adoption of certain motions, has a particular meaning in parliamentary law.”

**119.** `[C0275 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: The term previous _____ (or notice), as applied to necessary conditions for the adoption of certain motions, has a particular meaning in parliamentary law.

   *Answer:* notice — “The term previous notice (or notice), as applied to necessary conditions for the adoption of certain motions, has a particular meaning in parliamentary law.”. See RONR (12th ed.) §10:44 — “The term previous notice (or notice), as applied to necessary conditions for the adoption of certain motions, has a particular meaning in parliamentary law.”

**120.** `[C0275 · Applied multiple-choice]` Fill in the blank: The term previous _____ (or notice), as applied to necessary conditions for the adoption of certain motions, has a particular meaning in parliamentary law.
   A. secretary
   B. those present,
   C. Suspend
   D. notice

   *Answer:* D) notice. See RONR (12th ed.) §10:44 — “The term previous notice (or notice), as applied to necessary conditions for the adoption of certain motions, has a particular meaning in parliamentary law.”

**121.** `[C0276 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: Motions that have the effect of changing or nullifying previous action of the assembly—such as the motion to _____ or to Amend Something Previously Adopted (35), the motion to Discharge a Committee (36), or a motion to postpone an event already scheduled—require previous notice if they are to be adopted by only a majority vote.

   *Answer:* Rescind — “Motions that have the effect of changing or nullifying previous action of the assembly—such as the motion to Rescind or to Amend Something Previously Adopted (35), the motion to Discharge a Committee (36), or a motion to postpone an event already scheduled—require previous notice if they are to be adopted by only a majority vote.”. See RONR (12th ed.) §10:45 — “Motions that have the effect of changing or nullifying previous action of the assembly—such as the motion to Rescind or to Amend Something Previously Adopted (35), the motion to Discharge a Committee (36), or a motion to postpone an event already scheduled—require previous notice if they are to be adopted by only a majority vote.”

**122.** `[C0276 · Applied multiple-choice]` Fill in the blank: Motions that have the effect of changing or nullifying previous action of the assembly—such as the motion to _____ or to Amend Something Previously Adopted (35), the motion to Discharge a Committee (36), or a motion to postpone an event already scheduled—require previous notice if they are to be adopted by only a majority vote.
   A. or local law
   B. amend
   C. Rescind
   D. vote in the negative

   *Answer:* C) Rescind. See RONR (12th ed.) §10:45 — “Motions that have the effect of changing or nullifying previous action of the assembly—such as the motion to Rescind or to Amend Something Previously Adopted (35), the motion to Discharge a Committee (36), or a motion to postpone an event already scheduled—require previous notice if they are to be adopted by only a majority vote.”

**123.** `[C0276 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: The adoption or amendment of special rules of order requires either (a) previous notice _____ a two-thirds vote or (b) a vote of a majority of the entire membership—as does the amendment of bylaws if they do not prescribe the procedure for their amendment, which they should do (see also Table of Rules Relating to Motions, pages t6–t33).

   *Answer:* and — “The adoption or amendment of special rules of order requires either (a) previous notice and a two-thirds vote or (b) a vote of a majority of the entire membership—as does the amendment of bylaws if they do not prescribe the procedure for their amendment, which they should do (see also Table of Rules Relating to Motions, pages t6–t33).”. See RONR (12th ed.) §10:45 — “The adoption or amendment of special rules of order requires either (a) previous notice and a two-thirds vote or (b) a vote of a majority of the entire membership—as does the amendment of bylaws if they do not prescribe the procedure for their amendment, which they should do (see also Table of Rules Relating to Motions, pages t6–t33).”

**124.** `[C0276 · Applied multiple-choice]` Fill in the blank: The adoption or amendment of special rules of order requires either (a) previous notice _____ a two-thirds vote or (b) a vote of a majority of the entire membership—as does the amendment of bylaws if they do not prescribe the procedure for their amendment, which they should do (see also Table of Rules Relating to Motions, pages t6–t33).
   A. and
   B. ad hoc
   C. To Read Papers
   D. standing rule

   *Answer:* A) and. See RONR (12th ed.) §10:45 — “The adoption or amendment of special rules of order requires either (a) previous notice and a two-thirds vote or (b) a vote of a majority of the entire membership—as does the amendment of bylaws if they do not prescribe the procedure for their amendment, which they should do (see also Table of Rules Relating to Motions, pages t6–t33).”

**125.** `[C0276 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: Motions that have the effect of changing or nullifying previous action of the assembly—such as the motion to Rescind or to Amend Something Previously Adopted (35), the motion to _____ (36), or a motion to postpone an event already scheduled—require previous notice if they are to be adopted by only a majority vote.

   *Answer:* Discharge a Committee — “Motions that have the effect of changing or nullifying previous action of the assembly—such as the motion to Rescind or to Amend Something Previously Adopted (35), the motion to Discharge a Committee (36), or a motion to postpone an event already scheduled—require previous notice if they are to be adopted by only a majority vote.”. See RONR (12th ed.) §10:45 — “Motions that have the effect of changing or nullifying previous action of the assembly—such as the motion to Rescind or to Amend Something Previously Adopted (35), the motion to Discharge a Committee (36), or a motion to postpone an event already scheduled—require previous notice if they are to be adopted by only a majority vote.”

**126.** `[C0278 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: If previous notice is given _____, it can be given orally unless the rules of the organization require it to be in writing—which is often the case with notice of amendments to bylaws.

   *Answer:* at a meeting — “If previous notice is given at a meeting, it can be given orally unless the rules of the organization require it to be in writing—which is often the case with notice of amendments to bylaws.”. See RONR (12th ed.) §10:47 — “If previous notice is given at a meeting, it can be given orally unless the rules of the organization require it to be in writing—which is often the case with notice of amendments to bylaws.”

**127.** `[C0278 · Applied multiple-choice]` Fill in the blank: If previous notice is given _____, it can be given orally unless the rules of the organization require it to be in writing—which is often the case with notice of amendments to bylaws.
   A. at a meeting
   B. The Framing of
   C. of Order
   D. immediately pending question

   *Answer:* A) at a meeting. See RONR (12th ed.) §10:47 — “If previous notice is given at a meeting, it can be given orally unless the rules of the organization require it to be in writing—which is often the case with notice of amendments to bylaws.”

**128.** `[C0282 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: A motion to _____ (or accept or agree to) an officer’s or a committee’s report or recommendations which the assembly (by means of a main motion) directed the officer or committee to prepare is an incidental main motion.

   *Answer:* adopt — “A motion to adopt (or accept or agree to) an officer’s or a committee’s report or recommendations which the assembly (by means of a main motion) directed the officer or committee to prepare is an incidental main motion.”. See RONR (12th ed.) §10:52 — “A motion to adopt (or accept or agree to) an officer’s or a committee’s report or recommendations which the assembly (by means of a main motion) directed the officer or committee to prepare is an incidental main motion.”

**129.** `[C0282 · Applied multiple-choice]` Fill in the blank: A motion to _____ (or accept or agree to) an officer’s or a committee’s report or recommendations which the assembly (by means of a main motion) directed the officer or committee to prepare is an incidental main motion.
   A. adopt
   B. ceases to be
   C. rising
   D. expressly

   *Answer:* A) adopt. See RONR (12th ed.) §10:52 — “A motion to adopt (or accept or agree to) an officer’s or a committee’s report or recommendations which the assembly (by means of a main motion) directed the officer or committee to prepare is an incidental main motion.”

**130.** `[C0282 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: A motion to adopt (or _____ or agree to) an officer’s or a committee’s report or recommendations which the assembly (by means of a main motion) directed the officer or committee to prepare is an incidental main motion.

   *Answer:* accept — “A motion to adopt (or accept or agree to) an officer’s or a committee’s report or recommendations which the assembly (by means of a main motion) directed the officer or committee to prepare is an incidental main motion.”. See RONR (12th ed.) §10:52 — “A motion to adopt (or accept or agree to) an officer’s or a committee’s report or recommendations which the assembly (by means of a main motion) directed the officer or committee to prepare is an incidental main motion.”

**131.** `[C0282 · Applied multiple-choice]` Fill in the blank: A motion to adopt (or _____ or agree to) an officer’s or a committee’s report or recommendations which the assembly (by means of a main motion) directed the officer or committee to prepare is an incidental main motion.
   A. secret
   B. adoption
   C. accept
   D. yields to

   *Answer:* C) accept. See RONR (12th ed.) §10:52 — “A motion to adopt (or accept or agree to) an officer’s or a committee’s report or recommendations which the assembly (by means of a main motion) directed the officer or committee to prepare is an incidental main motion.”

**132.** `[C0282 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: A motion to adopt (or accept or _____) an officer’s or a committee’s report or recommendations which the assembly (by means of a main motion) directed the officer or committee to prepare is an incidental main motion.

   *Answer:* agree to — “A motion to adopt (or accept or agree to) an officer’s or a committee’s report or recommendations which the assembly (by means of a main motion) directed the officer or committee to prepare is an incidental main motion.”. See RONR (12th ed.) §10:52 — “A motion to adopt (or accept or agree to) an officer’s or a committee’s report or recommendations which the assembly (by means of a main motion) directed the officer or committee to prepare is an incidental main motion.”

**133.** `[C0284 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: The motion to ratify (also called _____ or confirm) is an incidental main motion that is used to confirm or make valid an action already taken that cannot become valid until approved by the assembly.

   *Answer:* approve — “The motion to ratify (also called approve or confirm) is an incidental main motion that is used to confirm or make valid an action already taken that cannot become valid until approved by the assembly.”. See RONR (12th ed.) §10:54 — “The motion to ratify (also called approve or confirm) is an incidental main motion that is used to confirm or make valid an action already taken that cannot become valid until approved by the assembly.”

**134.** `[C0284 · Applied multiple-choice]` Fill in the blank: The motion to ratify (also called _____ or confirm) is an incidental main motion that is used to confirm or make valid an action already taken that cannot become valid until approved by the assembly.
   A. body of people
   B. approve
   C. Consider by Paragraph
   D. Limit or Extend

   *Answer:* B) approve. See RONR (12th ed.) §10:54 — “The motion to ratify (also called approve or confirm) is an incidental main motion that is used to confirm or make valid an action already taken that cannot become valid until approved by the assembly.”

**135.** `[C0284 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: The motion to ratify (also called approve or _____) is an incidental main motion that is used to confirm or make valid an action already taken that cannot become valid until approved by the assembly.

   *Answer:* confirm — “The motion to ratify (also called approve or confirm) is an incidental main motion that is used to confirm or make valid an action already taken that cannot become valid until approved by the assembly.”. See RONR (12th ed.) §10:54 — “The motion to ratify (also called approve or confirm) is an incidental main motion that is used to confirm or make valid an action already taken that cannot become valid until approved by the assembly.”

**136.** `[C0284 · Applied multiple-choice]` Fill in the blank: The motion to ratify (also called approve or _____) is an incidental main motion that is used to confirm or make valid an action already taken that cannot become valid until approved by the assembly.
   A. a Question
   B. the Rules
   C. confirm
   D. sustaining

   *Answer:* C) confirm. See RONR (12th ed.) §10:54 — “The motion to ratify (also called approve or confirm) is an incidental main motion that is used to confirm or make valid an action already taken that cannot become valid until approved by the assembly.”

**137.** `[C0284 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: The motion to _____ (also called approve or confirm) is an incidental main motion that is used to confirm or make valid an action already taken that cannot become valid until approved by the assembly.

   *Answer:* ratify — “The motion to ratify (also called approve or confirm) is an incidental main motion that is used to confirm or make valid an action already taken that cannot become valid until approved by the assembly.”. See RONR (12th ed.) §10:54 — “The motion to ratify (also called approve or confirm) is an incidental main motion that is used to confirm or make valid an action already taken that cannot become valid until approved by the assembly.”

---

## Domain 2: Subsidiary and Privileged Motions

**1.** `[K0001 · Vote required]` What vote does the motion to Fix the Time to Which to Adjourn require to be adopted?
   A. Majority vote
   B. Two-thirds vote
   C. No vote (chair rules / demand)
   D. Unanimous consent

   *Answer:* Majority vote. See RONR (12th ed.) §22:1 — “The object of the motion to Fix the Time to Which to Adjourn (also referred to as the motion to “fix the time for an adjourned meeting”) is to set the time, and sometimes the place, for another meeting to continue business of the session, with no effect on when the present meeting will adjourn.”

**2.** `[K0002 · Debatability]` Is the motion to Fix the Time to Which to Adjourn debatable?
   A. Debatable
   B. Not debatable

   *Answer:* Not debatable. See RONR (12th ed.) §22:1 — “The object of the motion to Fix the Time to Which to Adjourn (also referred to as the motion to “fix the time for an adjourned meeting”) is to set the time, and sometimes the place, for another meeting to continue business of the session, with no effect on when the present meeting will adjourn.”

**3.** `[K0003 · Second required]` Does the motion to Fix the Time to Which to Adjourn require a second?
   A. Requires a second
   B. No second required

   *Answer:* Requires a second. See RONR (12th ed.) §22:1 — “The object of the motion to Fix the Time to Which to Adjourn (also referred to as the motion to “fix the time for an adjourned meeting”) is to set the time, and sometimes the place, for another meeting to continue business of the session, with no effect on when the present meeting will adjourn.”

**4.** `[K0004 · Amendability]` Can the motion to Fix the Time to Which to Adjourn be amended?
   A. Amendable
   B. Not amendable

   *Answer:* Amendable. See RONR (12th ed.) §22:1 — “The object of the motion to Fix the Time to Which to Adjourn (also referred to as the motion to “fix the time for an adjourned meeting”) is to set the time, and sometimes the place, for another meeting to continue business of the session, with no effect on when the present meeting will adjourn.”

**5.** `[K0005 · Vote required]` What vote does the motion to Adjourn require to be adopted?
   A. Majority vote
   B. Two-thirds vote
   C. No vote (chair rules / demand)
   D. Unanimous consent

   *Answer:* Majority vote. See RONR (12th ed.) §21:1 — “A motion to adjourn may be a privileged or a main motion depending on a number of conditions.”

**6.** `[K0006 · Debatability]` Is the motion to Adjourn debatable?
   A. Debatable
   B. Not debatable

   *Answer:* Not debatable. See RONR (12th ed.) §21:1 — “A motion to adjourn may be a privileged or a main motion depending on a number of conditions.”

**7.** `[K0007 · Second required]` Does the motion to Adjourn require a second?
   A. Requires a second
   B. No second required

   *Answer:* Requires a second. See RONR (12th ed.) §21:1 — “A motion to adjourn may be a privileged or a main motion depending on a number of conditions.”

**8.** `[K0008 · Amendability]` Can the motion to Adjourn be amended?
   A. Amendable
   B. Not amendable

   *Answer:* Not amendable. See RONR (12th ed.) §21:1 — “A motion to adjourn may be a privileged or a main motion depending on a number of conditions.”

**9.** `[K0009 · Vote required]` What vote does the motion to Recess require to be adopted?
   A. Majority vote
   B. Two-thirds vote
   C. No vote (chair rules / demand)
   D. Unanimous consent

   *Answer:* Majority vote. See RONR (12th ed.) §20:1 — “A recess is a short intermission in the assembly’s proceedings, commonly of only a few minutes, which does not close the meeting and after which business will immediately be resumed at exactly the point where it was interrupted.”

**10.** `[K0010 · Debatability]` Is the motion to Recess debatable?
   A. Debatable
   B. Not debatable

   *Answer:* Not debatable. See RONR (12th ed.) §20:1 — “A recess is a short intermission in the assembly’s proceedings, commonly of only a few minutes, which does not close the meeting and after which business will immediately be resumed at exactly the point where it was interrupted.”

**11.** `[K0011 · Second required]` Does the motion to Recess require a second?
   A. Requires a second
   B. No second required

   *Answer:* Requires a second. See RONR (12th ed.) §20:1 — “A recess is a short intermission in the assembly’s proceedings, commonly of only a few minutes, which does not close the meeting and after which business will immediately be resumed at exactly the point where it was interrupted.”

**12.** `[K0012 · Amendability]` Can the motion to Recess be amended?
   A. Amendable
   B. Not amendable

   *Answer:* Amendable. See RONR (12th ed.) §20:1 — “A recess is a short intermission in the assembly’s proceedings, commonly of only a few minutes, which does not close the meeting and after which business will immediately be resumed at exactly the point where it was interrupted.”

**13.** `[K0013 · Vote required]` What vote does the motion to Raise a Question of Privilege require to be adopted?
   A. Majority vote
   B. Two-thirds vote
   C. No vote (chair rules / demand)
   D. Unanimous consent

   *Answer:* No vote (chair rules / demand). See RONR (12th ed.) §19:1 — “To Raise a Question of Privilege is a device that permits a request or main motion relating to the rights and privileges of the assembly or any of its members to be brought up for possible immediate consideration because of its urgency, while business is pending and the request or motion would…”

**14.** `[K0014 · Debatability]` Is the motion to Raise a Question of Privilege debatable?
   A. Debatable
   B. Not debatable

   *Answer:* Not debatable. See RONR (12th ed.) §19:1 — “To Raise a Question of Privilege is a device that permits a request or main motion relating to the rights and privileges of the assembly or any of its members to be brought up for possible immediate consideration because of its urgency, while business is pending and the request or motion would…”

**15.** `[K0015 · Second required]` Does the motion to Raise a Question of Privilege require a second?
   A. Requires a second
   B. No second required

   *Answer:* No second required. See RONR (12th ed.) §19:1 — “To Raise a Question of Privilege is a device that permits a request or main motion relating to the rights and privileges of the assembly or any of its members to be brought up for possible immediate consideration because of its urgency, while business is pending and the request or motion would…”

**16.** `[K0016 · Amendability]` Can the motion to Raise a Question of Privilege be amended?
   A. Amendable
   B. Not amendable

   *Answer:* Not amendable. See RONR (12th ed.) §19:1 — “To Raise a Question of Privilege is a device that permits a request or main motion relating to the rights and privileges of the assembly or any of its members to be brought up for possible immediate consideration because of its urgency, while business is pending and the request or motion would…”

**17.** `[K0017 · Vote required]` What vote does the motion to Call for the Orders of the Day require to be adopted?
   A. Majority vote
   B. Two-thirds vote
   C. No vote (chair rules / demand)
   D. Unanimous consent

   *Answer:* No vote (chair rules / demand). See RONR (12th ed.) §18:1 — “A Call for the Orders of the Day is a privileged motion by which a member can require the assembly to conform to its agenda, program, or order of business, or to take up a general or special order that is due to come up at the time (14, 41), unless two thirds of those voting wish to do otherwise.”

**18.** `[K0018 · Debatability]` Is the motion to Call for the Orders of the Day debatable?
   A. Debatable
   B. Not debatable

   *Answer:* Not debatable. See RONR (12th ed.) §18:1 — “A Call for the Orders of the Day is a privileged motion by which a member can require the assembly to conform to its agenda, program, or order of business, or to take up a general or special order that is due to come up at the time (14, 41), unless two thirds of those voting wish to do otherwise.”

**19.** `[K0019 · Second required]` Does the motion to Call for the Orders of the Day require a second?
   A. Requires a second
   B. No second required

   *Answer:* No second required. See RONR (12th ed.) §18:1 — “A Call for the Orders of the Day is a privileged motion by which a member can require the assembly to conform to its agenda, program, or order of business, or to take up a general or special order that is due to come up at the time (14, 41), unless two thirds of those voting wish to do otherwise.”

**20.** `[K0020 · Amendability]` Can the motion to Call for the Orders of the Day be amended?
   A. Amendable
   B. Not amendable

   *Answer:* Not amendable. See RONR (12th ed.) §18:1 — “A Call for the Orders of the Day is a privileged motion by which a member can require the assembly to conform to its agenda, program, or order of business, or to take up a general or special order that is due to come up at the time (14, 41), unless two thirds of those voting wish to do otherwise.”

**21.** `[K0021 · Vote required]` What vote does the motion to Lay on the Table require to be adopted?
   A. Majority vote
   B. Two-thirds vote
   C. No vote (chair rules / demand)
   D. Unanimous consent

   *Answer:* Majority vote. See RONR (12th ed.) §17:1 — “The motion to Lay on the Table enables the assembly to lay the pending question aside temporarily when something else of immediate urgency has arisen or when something else needs to be addressed before consideration of the pending question is resumed, in such a way that:”

**22.** `[K0022 · Debatability]` Is the motion to Lay on the Table debatable?
   A. Debatable
   B. Not debatable

   *Answer:* Not debatable. See RONR (12th ed.) §17:1 — “The motion to Lay on the Table enables the assembly to lay the pending question aside temporarily when something else of immediate urgency has arisen or when something else needs to be addressed before consideration of the pending question is resumed, in such a way that:”

**23.** `[K0023 · Second required]` Does the motion to Lay on the Table require a second?
   A. Requires a second
   B. No second required

   *Answer:* Requires a second. See RONR (12th ed.) §17:1 — “The motion to Lay on the Table enables the assembly to lay the pending question aside temporarily when something else of immediate urgency has arisen or when something else needs to be addressed before consideration of the pending question is resumed, in such a way that:”

**24.** `[K0024 · Amendability]` Can the motion to Lay on the Table be amended?
   A. Amendable
   B. Not amendable

   *Answer:* Not amendable. See RONR (12th ed.) §17:1 — “The motion to Lay on the Table enables the assembly to lay the pending question aside temporarily when something else of immediate urgency has arisen or when something else needs to be addressed before consideration of the pending question is resumed, in such a way that:”

**25.** `[K0025 · Vote required]` What vote does the motion to Previous Question require to be adopted?
   A. Majority vote
   B. Two-thirds vote
   C. No vote (chair rules / demand)
   D. Unanimous consent

   *Answer:* Two-thirds vote. See RONR (12th ed.) §16:1 — “The Previous Question is the motion used to bring the assembly to an immediate vote on one or more pending questions; its adoption does this with certain exceptions.”

**26.** `[K0026 · Debatability]` Is the motion to Previous Question debatable?
   A. Debatable
   B. Not debatable

   *Answer:* Not debatable. See RONR (12th ed.) §16:1 — “The Previous Question is the motion used to bring the assembly to an immediate vote on one or more pending questions; its adoption does this with certain exceptions.”

**27.** `[K0027 · Second required]` Does the motion to Previous Question require a second?
   A. Requires a second
   B. No second required

   *Answer:* Requires a second. See RONR (12th ed.) §16:1 — “The Previous Question is the motion used to bring the assembly to an immediate vote on one or more pending questions; its adoption does this with certain exceptions.”

**28.** `[K0028 · Amendability]` Can the motion to Previous Question be amended?
   A. Amendable
   B. Not amendable

   *Answer:* Not amendable. See RONR (12th ed.) §16:1 — “The Previous Question is the motion used to bring the assembly to an immediate vote on one or more pending questions; its adoption does this with certain exceptions.”

**29.** `[K0029 · Vote required]` What vote does the motion to Limit or Extend Limits of Debate require to be adopted?
   A. Majority vote
   B. Two-thirds vote
   C. No vote (chair rules / demand)
   D. Unanimous consent

   *Answer:* Two-thirds vote. See RONR (12th ed.) §15:1 — “The subsidiary motion to Limit or Extend Limits of Debate is one of the two motions by means of which an assembly can exercise special control over debate on a pending question or on a series of pending questions.”

**30.** `[K0030 · Debatability]` Is the motion to Limit or Extend Limits of Debate debatable?
   A. Debatable
   B. Not debatable

   *Answer:* Not debatable. See RONR (12th ed.) §15:1 — “The subsidiary motion to Limit or Extend Limits of Debate is one of the two motions by means of which an assembly can exercise special control over debate on a pending question or on a series of pending questions.”

**31.** `[K0031 · Second required]` Does the motion to Limit or Extend Limits of Debate require a second?
   A. Requires a second
   B. No second required

   *Answer:* Requires a second. See RONR (12th ed.) §15:1 — “The subsidiary motion to Limit or Extend Limits of Debate is one of the two motions by means of which an assembly can exercise special control over debate on a pending question or on a series of pending questions.”

**32.** `[K0032 · Amendability]` Can the motion to Limit or Extend Limits of Debate be amended?
   A. Amendable
   B. Not amendable

   *Answer:* Amendable. See RONR (12th ed.) §15:1 — “The subsidiary motion to Limit or Extend Limits of Debate is one of the two motions by means of which an assembly can exercise special control over debate on a pending question or on a series of pending questions.”

**33.** `[K0033 · Vote required]` What vote does the motion to Postpone to a Certain Time require to be adopted?
   A. Majority vote
   B. Two-thirds vote
   C. No vote (chair rules / demand)
   D. Unanimous consent

   *Answer:* Majority vote. See RONR (12th ed.) §14:1 — “The subsidiary motion to Postpone to a Certain Time (or Postpone Definitely, or Postpone) is the motion by which action on a pending question can be put off, within limits, to a definite session, day, meeting, or hour, or until after a certain event.”

**34.** `[K0034 · Debatability]` Is the motion to Postpone to a Certain Time debatable?
   A. Debatable
   B. Not debatable

   *Answer:* Debatable. See RONR (12th ed.) §14:1 — “The subsidiary motion to Postpone to a Certain Time (or Postpone Definitely, or Postpone) is the motion by which action on a pending question can be put off, within limits, to a definite session, day, meeting, or hour, or until after a certain event.”

**35.** `[K0035 · Second required]` Does the motion to Postpone to a Certain Time require a second?
   A. Requires a second
   B. No second required

   *Answer:* Requires a second. See RONR (12th ed.) §14:1 — “The subsidiary motion to Postpone to a Certain Time (or Postpone Definitely, or Postpone) is the motion by which action on a pending question can be put off, within limits, to a definite session, day, meeting, or hour, or until after a certain event.”

**36.** `[K0036 · Amendability]` Can the motion to Postpone to a Certain Time be amended?
   A. Amendable
   B. Not amendable

   *Answer:* Amendable. See RONR (12th ed.) §14:1 — “The subsidiary motion to Postpone to a Certain Time (or Postpone Definitely, or Postpone) is the motion by which action on a pending question can be put off, within limits, to a definite session, day, meeting, or hour, or until after a certain event.”

**37.** `[K0037 · Vote required]` What vote does the motion to Commit or Refer require to be adopted?
   A. Majority vote
   B. Two-thirds vote
   C. No vote (chair rules / demand)
   D. Unanimous consent

   *Answer:* Majority vote. See RONR (12th ed.) §13:1 — “The subsidiary motion to Commit or Refer is generally used to send a pending question to a relatively small group of selected persons—a committee—so that the question may be carefully investigated and put into better condition for the assembly to consider.”

**38.** `[K0038 · Debatability]` Is the motion to Commit or Refer debatable?
   A. Debatable
   B. Not debatable

   *Answer:* Debatable. See RONR (12th ed.) §13:1 — “The subsidiary motion to Commit or Refer is generally used to send a pending question to a relatively small group of selected persons—a committee—so that the question may be carefully investigated and put into better condition for the assembly to consider.”

**39.** `[K0039 · Second required]` Does the motion to Commit or Refer require a second?
   A. Requires a second
   B. No second required

   *Answer:* Requires a second. See RONR (12th ed.) §13:1 — “The subsidiary motion to Commit or Refer is generally used to send a pending question to a relatively small group of selected persons—a committee—so that the question may be carefully investigated and put into better condition for the assembly to consider.”

**40.** `[K0040 · Amendability]` Can the motion to Commit or Refer be amended?
   A. Amendable
   B. Not amendable

   *Answer:* Amendable. See RONR (12th ed.) §13:1 — “The subsidiary motion to Commit or Refer is generally used to send a pending question to a relatively small group of selected persons—a committee—so that the question may be carefully investigated and put into better condition for the assembly to consider.”

**41.** `[K0041 · Vote required]` What vote does the motion to Amend require to be adopted?
   A. Majority vote
   B. Two-thirds vote
   C. No vote (chair rules / demand)
   D. Unanimous consent

   *Answer:* Majority vote. See RONR (12th ed.) §12:1 — “The subsidiary motion to Amend is a motion to modify the wording—and within certain limits the meaning—of a pending motion before the pending motion itself is acted upon.”

**42.** `[K0042 · Debatability]` Is the motion to Amend debatable?
   A. Debatable
   B. Not debatable

   *Answer:* Debatable. See RONR (12th ed.) §12:1 — “The subsidiary motion to Amend is a motion to modify the wording—and within certain limits the meaning—of a pending motion before the pending motion itself is acted upon.”

**43.** `[K0043 · Second required]` Does the motion to Amend require a second?
   A. Requires a second
   B. No second required

   *Answer:* Requires a second. See RONR (12th ed.) §12:1 — “The subsidiary motion to Amend is a motion to modify the wording—and within certain limits the meaning—of a pending motion before the pending motion itself is acted upon.”

**44.** `[K0044 · Amendability]` Can the motion to Amend be amended?
   A. Amendable
   B. Not amendable

   *Answer:* Amendable. See RONR (12th ed.) §12:1 — “The subsidiary motion to Amend is a motion to modify the wording—and within certain limits the meaning—of a pending motion before the pending motion itself is acted upon.”

**45.** `[K0045 · Vote required]` What vote does the motion to Postpone Indefinitely require to be adopted?
   A. Majority vote
   B. Two-thirds vote
   C. No vote (chair rules / demand)
   D. Unanimous consent

   *Answer:* Majority vote. See RONR (12th ed.) §11:1 — “Postpone Indefinitely is a motion that the assembly decline to take a position on the main question.”

**46.** `[K0046 · Debatability]` Is the motion to Postpone Indefinitely debatable?
   A. Debatable
   B. Not debatable

   *Answer:* Debatable. See RONR (12th ed.) §11:1 — “Postpone Indefinitely is a motion that the assembly decline to take a position on the main question.”

**47.** `[K0047 · Second required]` Does the motion to Postpone Indefinitely require a second?
   A. Requires a second
   B. No second required

   *Answer:* Requires a second. See RONR (12th ed.) §11:1 — “Postpone Indefinitely is a motion that the assembly decline to take a position on the main question.”

**48.** `[K0048 · Amendability]` Can the motion to Postpone Indefinitely be amended?
   A. Amendable
   B. Not amendable

   *Answer:* Not amendable. See RONR (12th ed.) §11:1 — “Postpone Indefinitely is a motion that the assembly decline to take a position on the main question.”

**49.** `[K0073 · Precedence (ranking)]` Which of these motions has the highest precedence?
   A. Adjourn
   B. Recess
   C. Fix the Time to Which to Adjourn

   *Answer:* Fix the Time to Which to Adjourn. See RONR (12th ed.) §22:1 — “The object of the motion to Fix the Time to Which to Adjourn (also referred to as the motion to “fix the time for an adjourned meeting”) is to set the time, and sometimes the place, for another meeting to continue business of the session, with no effect on when the present meeting will adjourn.”

**50.** `[K0074 · Precedence (ranking)]` Which of these motions has the lowest precedence?
   A. Fix the Time to Which to Adjourn
   B. Adjourn
   C. Recess

   *Answer:* Recess. See RONR (12th ed.) §20:1 — “A recess is a short intermission in the assembly’s proceedings, commonly of only a few minutes, which does not close the meeting and after which business will immediately be resumed at exactly the point where it was interrupted.”

**51.** `[K0075 · Precedence (ordering)]` Put these motions in order of precedence.

   *Answer:* Fix the Time to Which to Adjourn → Adjourn → Recess. See RONR (12th ed.) §22:1 — “The object of the motion to Fix the Time to Which to Adjourn (also referred to as the motion to “fix the time for an adjourned meeting”) is to set the time, and sometimes the place, for another meeting to continue business of the session, with no effect on when the present meeting will adjourn.”

**52.** `[K0076 · Precedence (ranking)]` Which of these motions has the highest precedence?
   A. Adjourn
   B. Recess
   C. Raise a Question of Privilege

   *Answer:* Adjourn. See RONR (12th ed.) §21:1 — “A motion to adjourn may be a privileged or a main motion depending on a number of conditions.”

   A. Adjourn

See RONR (12th ed.) §19:1 — “To Raise a Question of Privilege is a device that permits a request or main motion relating to the rights and privileges of the assembly or any of its members to be brought up for possible immediate consideration because of its urgency, while business is pending and the request or motion would…”

**54.** `[K0078 · Precedence (ordering)]` Put these motions in order of precedence.

   *Answer:* Adjourn → Recess → Raise a Question of Privilege. See RONR (12th ed.) §21:1 — “A motion to adjourn may be a privileged or a main motion depending on a number of conditions.”

**65.** `[K0089 · Precedence (ranking)]` Which of these motions has the lowest precedence?
   A. Previous Question
   B. Limit or Extend Limits of Debate
   C. Lay on the Table

   *Answer:* Limit or Extend Limits of Debate. See RONR (12th ed.) §15:1 — “The subsidiary motion to Limit or Extend Limits of Debate is one of the two motions by means of which an assembly can exercise special control over debate on a pending question or on a series of pending questions.”

**66.** `[K0090 · Precedence (ordering)]` Put these motions in order of precedence.

   *Answer:* Lay on the Table → Previous Question → Limit or Extend Limits of Debate. See RONR (12th ed.) §17:1 — “The motion to Lay on the Table enables the assembly to lay the pending question aside temporarily when something else of immediate urgency has arisen or when something else needs to be addressed before consideration of the pending question is resumed, in such a way that:”

**67.** `[K0091 · Precedence (ranking)]` Which of these motions has the highest precedence?
   A. Postpone to a Certain Time
   B. Limit or Extend Limits of Debate
   C. Previous Question

   *Answer:* Previous Question. See RONR (12th ed.) §16:1 — “The Previous Question is the motion used to bring the assembly to an immediate vote on one or more pending questions; its adoption does this with certain exceptions.”

**68.** `[K0092 · Precedence (ranking)]` Which of these motions has the lowest precedence?
   A. Limit or Extend Limits of Debate
   B. Previous Question
   C. Postpone to a Certain Time

   *Answer:* Postpone to a Certain Time. See RONR (12th ed.) §14:1 — “The subsidiary motion to Postpone to a Certain Time (or Postpone Definitely, or Postpone) is the motion by which action on a pending question can be put off, within limits, to a definite session, day, meeting, or hour, or until after a certain event.”

**69.** `[K0093 · Precedence (ordering)]` Put these motions in order of precedence.

   *Answer:* Previous Question → Limit or Extend Limits of Debate → Postpone to a Certain Time. See RONR (12th ed.) §16:1 — “The Previous Question is the motion used to bring the assembly to an immediate vote on one or more pending questions; its adoption does this with certain exceptions.”

**70.** `[K0094 · Precedence (ranking)]` Which of these motions has the highest precedence?
   A. Commit or Refer
   B. Postpone to a Certain Time
   C. Limit or Extend Limits of Debate

   *Answer:* Limit or Extend Limits of Debate. See RONR (12th ed.) §15:1 — “The subsidiary motion to Limit or Extend Limits of Debate is one of the two motions by means of which an assembly can exercise special control over debate on a pending question or on a series of pending questions.”

**71.** `[K0095 · Precedence (ranking)]` Which of these motions has the lowest precedence?
   A. Limit or Extend Limits of Debate
   B. Commit or Refer
   C. Postpone to a Certain Time

   *Answer:* Commit or Refer. See RONR (12th ed.) §13:1 — “The subsidiary motion to Commit or Refer is generally used to send a pending question to a relatively small group of selected persons—a committee—so that the question may be carefully investigated and put into better condition for the assembly to consider.”

**72.** `[K0096 · Precedence (ordering)]` Put these motions in order of precedence.

   *Answer:* Limit or Extend Limits of Debate → Postpone to a Certain Time → Commit or Refer. See RONR (12th ed.) §15:1 — “The subsidiary motion to Limit or Extend Limits of Debate is one of the two motions by means of which an assembly can exercise special control over debate on a pending question or on a series of pending questions.”

**73.** `[K0097 · Precedence (ranking)]` Which of these motions has the highest precedence?
   A. Commit or Refer
   B. Amend
   C. Postpone to a Certain Time

   *Answer:* Postpone to a Certain Time. See RONR (12th ed.) §14:1 — “The subsidiary motion to Postpone to a Certain Time (or Postpone Definitely, or Postpone) is the motion by which action on a pending question can be put off, within limits, to a definite session, day, meeting, or hour, or until after a certain event.”

**74.** `[K0098 · Precedence (ranking)]` Which of these motions has the lowest precedence?
   A. Commit or Refer
   B. Amend
   C. Postpone to a Certain Time

   *Answer:* Amend. See RONR (12th ed.) §12:1 — “The subsidiary motion to Amend is a motion to modify the wording—and within certain limits the meaning—of a pending motion before the pending motion itself is acted upon.”

**75.** `[K0099 · Precedence (ordering)]` Put these motions in order of precedence.

   *Answer:* Postpone to a Certain Time → Commit or Refer → Amend. See RONR (12th ed.) §14:1 — “The subsidiary motion to Postpone to a Certain Time (or Postpone Definitely, or Postpone) is the motion by which action on a pending question can be put off, within limits, to a definite session, day, meeting, or hour, or until after a certain event.”

**76.** `[K0100 · Precedence (ranking)]` Which of these motions has the highest precedence?
   A. Amend
   B. Postpone Indefinitely
   C. Commit or Refer

   *Answer:* Commit or Refer. See RONR (12th ed.) §13:1 — “The subsidiary motion to Commit or Refer is generally used to send a pending question to a relatively small group of selected persons—a committee—so that the question may be carefully investigated and put into better condition for the assembly to consider.”

**77.** `[K0101 · Precedence (ranking)]` Which of these motions has the lowest precedence?
   A. Postpone Indefinitely
   B. Amend
   C. Commit or Refer

   *Answer:* Postpone Indefinitely. See RONR (12th ed.) §11:1 — “Postpone Indefinitely is a motion that the assembly decline to take a position on the main question.”

**78.** `[K0102 · Precedence (ordering)]` Put these motions in order of precedence.

   *Answer:* Commit or Refer → Amend → Postpone Indefinitely. See RONR (12th ed.) §13:1 — “The subsidiary motion to Commit or Refer is generally used to send a pending question to a relatively small group of selected persons—a committee—so that the question may be carefully investigated and put into better condition for the assembly to consider.”

**79.** `[K0103 · Precedence (ranking)]` Which of these motions has the highest precedence?
   A. Fix the Time to Which to Adjourn
   B. Raise a Question of Privilege
   C. Recess
   D. Adjourn

   *Answer:* Fix the Time to Which to Adjourn. See RONR (12th ed.) §22:1 — “The object of the motion to Fix the Time to Which to Adjourn (also referred to as the motion to “fix the time for an adjourned meeting”) is to set the time, and sometimes the place, for another meeting to continue business of the session, with no effect on when the present meeting will adjourn.”

**80.** `[K0104 · Precedence (ranking)]` Which of these motions has the lowest precedence?
   A. Raise a Question of Privilege
   B. Fix the Time to Which to Adjourn
   C. Adjourn
   D. Recess

   *Answer:* Raise a Question of Privilege. See RONR (12th ed.) §19:1 — “To Raise a Question of Privilege is a device that permits a request or main motion relating to the rights and privileges of the assembly or any of its members to be brought up for possible immediate consideration because of its urgency, while business is pending and the request or motion would…”

**81.** `[K0105 · Precedence (ordering)]` Put these motions in order of precedence.

   *Answer:* Fix the Time to Which to Adjourn → Adjourn → Recess → Raise a Question of Privilege. See RONR (12th ed.) §22:1 — “The object of the motion to Fix the Time to Which to Adjourn (also referred to as the motion to “fix the time for an adjourned meeting”) is to set the time, and sometimes the place, for another meeting to continue business of the session, with no effect on when the present meeting will adjourn.”

**82.** `[K0106 · Precedence (ranking)]` Which of these motions has the highest precedence?
   A. Raise a Question of Privilege
   B. Adjourn
   C. Call for the Orders of the Day
   D. Recess

   *Answer:* Adjourn. See RONR (12th ed.) §21:1 — “A motion to adjourn may be a privileged or a main motion depending on a number of conditions.”

**83.** `[K0107 · Precedence (ranking)]` Which of these motions has the lowest precedence?
   A. Adjourn
   B. Raise a Question of Privilege
   C. Recess
   D. Call for the Orders of the Day

   *Answer:* Call for the Orders of the Day. See RONR (12th ed.) §18:1 — “A Call for the Orders of the Day is a privileged motion by which a member can require the assembly to conform to its agenda, program, or order of business, or to take up a general or special order that is due to come up at the time (14, 41), unless two thirds of those voting wish to do otherwise.”

**84.** `[K0108 · Precedence (ordering)]` Put these motions in order of precedence.

   *Answer:* Adjourn → Recess → Raise a Question of Privilege → Call for the Orders of the Day. See RONR (12th ed.) §21:1 — “A motion to adjourn may be a privileged or a main motion depending on a number of conditions.”

**85.** `[K0109 · Precedence (ranking)]` Which of these motions has the highest precedence?
   A. Call for the Orders of the Day
   B. Raise a Question of Privilege
   C. Lay on the Table
   D. Recess

   *Answer:* Recess. See RONR (12th ed.) §20:1 — “A recess is a short intermission in the assembly’s proceedings, commonly of only a few minutes, which does not close the meeting and after which business will immediately be resumed at exactly the point where it was interrupted.”

**86.** `[K0110 · Precedence (ranking)]` Which of these motions has the lowest precedence?
   A. Lay on the Table
   B. Recess
   C. Raise a Question of Privilege
   D. Call for the Orders of the Day

   *Answer:* Lay on the Table. See RONR (12th ed.) §17:1 — “The motion to Lay on the Table enables the assembly to lay the pending question aside temporarily when something else of immediate urgency has arisen or when something else needs to be addressed before consideration of the pending question is resumed, in such a way that:”

**87.** `[K0111 · Precedence (ordering)]` Put these motions in order of precedence.

   *Answer:* Recess → Raise a Question of Privilege → Call for the Orders of the Day → Lay on the Table. See RONR (12th ed.) §20:1 — “A recess is a short intermission in the assembly’s proceedings, commonly of only a few minutes, which does not close the meeting and after which business will immediately be resumed at exactly the point where it was interrupted.”

**126.** `[K0150 · Precedence (ordering)]` Put these motions in order of precedence.

   *Answer:* Previous Question → Limit or Extend Limits of Debate → Postpone to a Certain Time → Commit or Refer → Amend. See RONR (12th ed.) §16:1 — “The Previous Question is the motion used to bring the assembly to an immediate vote on one or more pending questions; its adoption does this with certain exceptions.”

**127.** `[K0151 · Precedence (ranking)]` Which of these motions has the highest precedence?
   A. Postpone to a Certain Time
   B. Amend
   C. Commit or Refer
   D. Limit or Extend Limits of Debate

   *Answer:* Limit or Extend Limits of Debate. See RONR (12th ed.) §15:1 — “The subsidiary motion to Limit or Extend Limits of Debate is one of the two motions by means of which an assembly can exercise special control over debate on a pending question or on a series of pending questions.”

**128.** `[K0152 · Precedence (ranking)]` Which of these motions has the lowest precedence?
   A. Amend
   B. Postpone Indefinitely
   C. Commit or Refer
   D. Limit or Extend Limits of Debate

   *Answer:* Postpone Indefinitely. See RONR (12th ed.) §11:1 — “Postpone Indefinitely is a motion that the assembly decline to take a position on the main question.”

**129.** `[K0153 · Precedence (ordering)]` Put these motions in order of precedence.

   *Answer:* Limit or Extend Limits of Debate → Postpone to a Certain Time → Commit or Refer → Amend → Postpone Indefinitely. See RONR (12th ed.) §15:1 — “The subsidiary motion to Limit or Extend Limits of Debate is one of the two motions by means of which an assembly can exercise special control over debate on a pending question or on a series of pending questions.”

**130.** `[K0154 · Precedence (ranking)]` Which of these motions has the highest precedence?
   A. Commit or Refer
   B. Adjourn
   C. Fix the Time to Which to Adjourn
   D. Recess

   *Answer:* Fix the Time to Which to Adjourn. See RONR (12th ed.) §22:1 — “The object of the motion to Fix the Time to Which to Adjourn (also referred to as the motion to “fix the time for an adjourned meeting”) is to set the time, and sometimes the place, for another meeting to continue business of the session, with no effect on when the present meeting will adjourn.”

**131.** `[K0155 · Precedence (ranking)]` Which of these motions has the lowest precedence?
   A. Commit or Refer
   B. Fix the Time to Which to Adjourn
   C. Adjourn
   D. Recess

   *Answer:* Commit or Refer. See RONR (12th ed.) §13:1 — “The subsidiary motion to Commit or Refer is generally used to send a pending question to a relatively small group of selected persons—a committee—so that the question may be carefully investigated and put into better condition for the assembly to consider.”

**132.** `[K0156 · Precedence (ordering)]` Put these motions in order of precedence.

   *Answer:* Fix the Time to Which to Adjourn → Adjourn → Recess → Commit or Refer. See RONR (12th ed.) §22:1 — “The object of the motion to Fix the Time to Which to Adjourn (also referred to as the motion to “fix the time for an adjourned meeting”) is to set the time, and sometimes the place, for another meeting to continue business of the session, with no effect on when the present meeting will adjourn.”

**133.** `[K0157 · Precedence (ranking)]` Which of these motions has the highest precedence?
   A. Call for the Orders of the Day
   B. Previous Question
   C. Fix the Time to Which to Adjourn
   D. Adjourn

   *Answer:* Fix the Time to Which to Adjourn. See RONR (12th ed.) §22:1 — “The object of the motion to Fix the Time to Which to Adjourn (also referred to as the motion to “fix the time for an adjourned meeting”) is to set the time, and sometimes the place, for another meeting to continue business of the session, with no effect on when the present meeting will adjourn.”

**134.** `[K0158 · Precedence (ranking)]` Which of these motions has the lowest precedence?
   A. Previous Question
   B. Adjourn
   C. Fix the Time to Which to Adjourn
   D. Raise a Question of Privilege

   *Answer:* Previous Question. See RONR (12th ed.) §16:1 — “The Previous Question is the motion used to bring the assembly to an immediate vote on one or more pending questions; its adoption does this with certain exceptions.”

**135.** `[K0159 · Precedence (ordering)]` Put these motions in order of precedence.

   *Answer:* Fix the Time to Which to Adjourn → Adjourn → Raise a Question of Privilege → Call for the Orders of the Day → Previous Question. See RONR (12th ed.) §22:1 — “The object of the motion to Fix the Time to Which to Adjourn (also referred to as the motion to “fix the time for an adjourned meeting”) is to set the time, and sometimes the place, for another meeting to continue business of the session, with no effect on when the present meeting will adjourn.”

**136.** `[K0160 · Precedence (ranking)]` Which of these motions has the highest precedence?
   A. Limit or Extend Limits of Debate
   B. Recess
   C. Amend
   D. Postpone Indefinitely

   *Answer:* Recess. See RONR (12th ed.) §20:1 — “A recess is a short intermission in the assembly’s proceedings, commonly of only a few minutes, which does not close the meeting and after which business will immediately be resumed at exactly the point where it was interrupted.”

**137.** `[K0161 · Precedence (ranking)]` Which of these motions has the lowest precedence?
   A. Postpone Indefinitely
   B. Recess
   C. Limit or Extend Limits of Debate
   D. Amend

   *Answer:* Postpone Indefinitely. See RONR (12th ed.) §11:1 — “Postpone Indefinitely is a motion that the assembly decline to take a position on the main question.”

**138.** `[K0162 · Precedence (ordering)]` Put these motions in order of precedence.

   *Answer:* Recess → Raise a Question of Privilege → Limit or Extend Limits of Debate → Amend → Postpone Indefinitely. See RONR (12th ed.) §20:1 — “A recess is a short intermission in the assembly’s proceedings, commonly of only a few minutes, which does not close the meeting and after which business will immediately be resumed at exactly the point where it was interrupted.”

**139.** `[K0163 · Precedence (ranking)]` Which of these motions has the highest precedence?
   A. Limit or Extend Limits of Debate
   B. Raise a Question of Privilege
   C. Previous Question
   D. Recess

   *Answer:* Recess. See RONR (12th ed.) §20:1 — “A recess is a short intermission in the assembly’s proceedings, commonly of only a few minutes, which does not close the meeting and after which business will immediately be resumed at exactly the point where it was interrupted.”

**140.** `[K0164 · Precedence (ranking)]` Which of these motions has the lowest precedence?
   A. Previous Question
   B. Limit or Extend Limits of Debate
   C. Recess
   D. Postpone to a Certain Time

   *Answer:* Postpone to a Certain Time. See RONR (12th ed.) §14:1 — “The subsidiary motion to Postpone to a Certain Time (or Postpone Definitely, or Postpone) is the motion by which action on a pending question can be put off, within limits, to a definite session, day, meeting, or hour, or until after a certain event.”

**141.** `[K0165 · Precedence (ordering)]` Put these motions in order of precedence.

   *Answer:* Recess → Raise a Question of Privilege → Previous Question → Limit or Extend Limits of Debate → Postpone to a Certain Time. See RONR (12th ed.) §20:1 — “A recess is a short intermission in the assembly’s proceedings, commonly of only a few minutes, which does not close the meeting and after which business will immediately be resumed at exactly the point where it was interrupted.”

**142.** `[K0166 · Precedence (ranking)]` Which of these motions has the highest precedence?
   A. Call for the Orders of the Day
   B. Fix the Time to Which to Adjourn
   C. Previous Question
   D. Adjourn

   *Answer:* Fix the Time to Which to Adjourn. See RONR (12th ed.) §22:1 — “The object of the motion to Fix the Time to Which to Adjourn (also referred to as the motion to “fix the time for an adjourned meeting”) is to set the time, and sometimes the place, for another meeting to continue business of the session, with no effect on when the present meeting will adjourn.”

**143.** `[K0167 · Precedence (ranking)]` Which of these motions has the lowest precedence?
   A. Adjourn
   B. Previous Question
   C. Call for the Orders of the Day
   D. Fix the Time to Which to Adjourn

   *Answer:* Limit or Extend Limits of Debate. See RONR (12th ed.) §15:1 — “The subsidiary motion to Limit or Extend Limits of Debate is one of the two motions by means of which an assembly can exercise special control over debate on a pending question or on a series of pending questions.”

**144.** `[K0168 · Precedence (ordering)]` Put these motions in order of precedence.

   *Answer:* Fix the Time to Which to Adjourn → Adjourn → Call for the Orders of the Day → Previous Question → Limit or Extend Limits of Debate. See RONR (12th ed.) §22:1 — “The object of the motion to Fix the Time to Which to Adjourn (also referred to as the motion to “fix the time for an adjourned meeting”) is to set the time, and sometimes the place, for another meeting to continue business of the session, with no effect on when the present meeting will adjourn.”

**145.** `[K0169 · Precedence (ranking)]` Which of these motions has the highest precedence?
   A. Adjourn
   B. Recess
   C. Call for the Orders of the Day

   *Answer:* Adjourn. See RONR (12th ed.) §21:1 — “A motion to adjourn may be a privileged or a main motion depending on a number of conditions.”

**146.** `[K0170 · Precedence (ranking)]` Which of these motions has the lowest precedence?
   A. Recess
   B. Adjourn
   C. Call for the Orders of the Day

   *Answer:* Call for the Orders of the Day. See RONR (12th ed.) §18:1 — “A Call for the Orders of the Day is a privileged motion by which a member can require the assembly to conform to its agenda, program, or order of business, or to take up a general or special order that is due to come up at the time (14, 41), unless two thirds of those voting wish to do otherwise.”

**147.** `[K0171 · Precedence (ordering)]` Put these motions in order of precedence.

   *Answer:* Adjourn → Recess → Call for the Orders of the Day. See RONR (12th ed.) §21:1 — “A motion to adjourn may be a privileged or a main motion depending on a number of conditions.”

**148.** `[K0172 · Precedence (ranking)]` Which of these motions has the highest precedence?
   A. Recess
   B. Raise a Question of Privilege
   C. Postpone Indefinitely

   *Answer:* Recess. See RONR (12th ed.) §20:1 — “A recess is a short intermission in the assembly’s proceedings, commonly of only a few minutes, which does not close the meeting and after which business will immediately be resumed at exactly the point where it was interrupted.”

   C. Postpone Indefinitely

   *Answer:* Postpone Indefinitely. See RONR (12th ed.) §11:1 — “Postpone Indefinitely is a motion that the assembly decline to take a position on the main question.”

   *Answer:* Recess → Raise a Question of Privilege → Postpone Indefinitely. See RONR (12th ed.) §20:1 — “A recess is a short intermission in the assembly’s proceedings, commonly of only a few minutes, which does not close the meeting and after which business will immediately be resumed at exactly the point where it was interrupted.”

See RONR (12th ed.) §22:1 — “The object of the motion to Fix the Time to Which to Adjourn (also referred to as the motion to “fix the time for an adjourned meeting”) is to set the time, and sometimes the place, for another meeting to continue business of the session, with no effect on when the present meeting will adjourn.”

See RONR (12th ed.) §22:1 — “The object of the motion to Fix the Time to Which to Adjourn (also referred to as the motion to “fix the time for an adjourned meeting”) is to set the time, and sometimes the place, for another meeting to continue business of the session, with no effect on when the present meeting will adjourn.”

   D. Commit or Refer

See RONR (12th ed.) §13:1 — “The subsidiary motion to Commit or Refer is generally used to send a pending question to a relatively small group of selected persons—a committee—so that the question may be carefully investigated and put into better condition for the assembly to consider.”
