   *Answer:* Fix the Time to Which to Adjourn → Recess → Lay on the Table → Postpone to a Certain Time → Commit or Refer. See RONR (12th ed.) §22:1 — “The object of the motion to Fix the Time to Which to Adjourn (also referred to as the motion to “fix the time for an adjourned meeting”) is to set the time, and sometimes the place, for another meeting to continue business of the session, with no effect on when the present meeting will adjourn.”

   A. Amend
   B. Postpone to a Certain Time
   C. Adjourn

   *Answer:* Adjourn. See RONR (12th ed.) §21:1 — “A motion to adjourn may be a privileged or a main motion depending on a number of conditions.”

**158.** `[K0182 · Precedence (ranking)]` Which of these motions has the lowest precedence?
   A. Postpone to a Certain Time
   B. Amend
   C. Adjourn

   *Answer:* Amend. See RONR (12th ed.) §12:1 — “The subsidiary motion to Amend is a motion to modify the wording—and within certain limits the meaning—of a pending motion before the pending motion itself is acted upon.”

   *Answer:* Adjourn → Postpone to a Certain Time → Amend. See RONR (12th ed.) §21:1 — “A motion to adjourn may be a privileged or a main motion depending on a number of conditions.”

**184.** `[K0208 · Precedence (ranking)]` Which of these motions has the highest precedence?

   C. Lay on the Table
   D. Postpone to a Certain Time

   *Answer:* Raise a Question of Privilege. See RONR (12th ed.) §19:1 — “To Raise a Question of Privilege is a device that permits a request or main motion relating to the rights and privileges of the assembly or any of its members to be brought up for possible immediate consideration because of its urgency, while business is pending and the request or motion would…”

   *Answer:* Raise a Question of Privilege. See RONR (12th ed.) §19:1 — “To Raise a Question of Privilege is a device that permits a request or main motion relating to the rights and privileges of the assembly or any of its members to be brought up for possible immediate consideration because of its urgency, while business is pending and the request or motion would…”

**188.** `[K0212 · Precedence (ranking)]` Which of these motions has the lowest precedence?
   A. Call for the Orders of the Day
   B. Lay on the Table
   C. Raise a Question of Privilege

   *Answer:* Lay on the Table. See RONR (12th ed.) §17:1 — “The motion to Lay on the Table enables the assembly to lay the pending question aside temporarily when something else of immediate urgency has arisen or when something else needs to be addressed before consideration of the pending question is resumed, in such a way that:”

**189.** `[K0213 · Precedence (ordering)]` Put these motions in order of precedence.

   *Answer:* Raise a Question of Privilege → Call for the Orders of the Day → Lay on the Table. See RONR (12th ed.) §19:1 — “To Raise a Question of Privilege is a device that permits a request or main motion relating to the rights and privileges of the assembly or any of its members to be brought up for possible immediate consideration because of its urgency, while business is pending and the request or motion would…”

**190.** `[K0214 · Precedence (ranking)]` Which of these motions has the highest precedence?
   A. Postpone to a Certain Time
   B. Adjourn
   C. Raise a Question of Privilege
   D. Amend

   *Answer:* Adjourn. See RONR (12th ed.) §21:1 — “A motion to adjourn may be a privileged or a main motion depending on a number of conditions.”

**191.** `[K0215 · Precedence (ranking)]` Which of these motions has the lowest precedence?
   A. Adjourn
   B. Raise a Question of Privilege
   C. Amend
   D. Postpone to a Certain Time

   *Answer:* Amend. See RONR (12th ed.) §12:1 — “The subsidiary motion to Amend is a motion to modify the wording—and within certain limits the meaning—of a pending motion before the pending motion itself is acted upon.”

**192.** `[K0216 · Precedence (ordering)]` Put these motions in order of precedence.

   *Answer:* Adjourn → Raise a Question of Privilege → Previous Question → Postpone to a Certain Time → Amend. See RONR (12th ed.) §21:1 — “A motion to adjourn may be a privileged or a main motion depending on a number of conditions.”

**193.** `[K0217 · Precedence (ranking)]` Which of these motions has the highest precedence?
   A. Adjourn
   B. Recess
   C. Commit or Refer
   D. Raise a Question of Privilege

   *Answer:* Adjourn. See RONR (12th ed.) §21:1 — “A motion to adjourn may be a privileged or a main motion depending on a number of conditions.”

**194.** `[K0218 · Precedence (ranking)]` Which of these motions has the lowest precedence?
   A. Postpone to a Certain Time
   B. Raise a Question of Privilege
   C. Recess
   D. Adjourn

   *Answer:* Commit or Refer. See RONR (12th ed.) §13:1 — “The subsidiary motion to Commit or Refer is generally used to send a pending question to a relatively small group of selected persons—a committee—so that the question may be carefully investigated and put into better condition for the assembly to consider.”

**195.** `[K0219 · Precedence (ordering)]` Put these motions in order of precedence.

   *Answer:* Adjourn → Recess → Raise a Question of Privilege → Postpone to a Certain Time → Commit or Refer. See RONR (12th ed.) §21:1 — “A motion to adjourn may be a privileged or a main motion depending on a number of conditions.”

**196.** `[K0220 · Precedence (ranking)]` Which of these motions has the highest precedence?
   A. Adjourn
   B. Call for the Orders of the Day
   C. Postpone Indefinitely

   *Answer:* Adjourn. See RONR (12th ed.) §21:1 — “A motion to adjourn may be a privileged or a main motion depending on a number of conditions.”

**197.** `[K0221 · Precedence (ranking)]` Which of these motions has the lowest precedence?
   A. Postpone Indefinitely
   B. Adjourn
   C. Call for the Orders of the Day

   *Answer:* Postpone Indefinitely. See RONR (12th ed.) §11:1 — “Postpone Indefinitely is a motion that the assembly decline to take a position on the main question.”

**198.** `[K0222 · Precedence (ordering)]` Put these motions in order of precedence.

   *Answer:* Adjourn → Call for the Orders of the Day → Postpone Indefinitely. See RONR (12th ed.) §21:1 — “A motion to adjourn may be a privileged or a main motion depending on a number of conditions.”

**199.** `[K0223 · Precedence (ranking)]` Which of these motions has the highest precedence?
   A. Amend
   B. Previous Question
   C. Postpone to a Certain Time

   *Answer:* Previous Question. See RONR (12th ed.) §16:1 — “The Previous Question is the motion used to bring the assembly to an immediate vote on one or more pending questions; its adoption does this with certain exceptions.”

**200.** `[K0224 · Precedence (ranking)]` Which of these motions has the lowest precedence?
   A. Amend
   B. Previous Question
   C. Postpone to a Certain Time

   *Answer:* Amend. See RONR (12th ed.) §12:1 — “The subsidiary motion to Amend is a motion to modify the wording—and within certain limits the meaning—of a pending motion before the pending motion itself is acted upon.”

**201.** `[K0225 · Precedence (ordering)]` Put these motions in order of precedence.

   *Answer:* Previous Question → Postpone to a Certain Time → Amend. See RONR (12th ed.) §16:1 — “The Previous Question is the motion used to bring the assembly to an immediate vote on one or more pending questions; its adoption does this with certain exceptions.”

**202.** `[K0226 · Precedence (ranking)]` Which of these motions has the highest precedence?
   A. Commit or Refer
   B. Previous Question
   C. Lay on the Table

   *Answer:* Lay on the Table. See RONR (12th ed.) §17:1 — “The motion to Lay on the Table enables the assembly to lay the pending question aside temporarily when something else of immediate urgency has arisen or when something else needs to be addressed before consideration of the pending question is resumed, in such a way that:”

**203.** `[K0227 · Precedence (ranking)]` Which of these motions has the lowest precedence?
   A. Lay on the Table
   B. Commit or Refer
   C. Previous Question

   *Answer:* Commit or Refer. See RONR (12th ed.) §13:1 — “The subsidiary motion to Commit or Refer is generally used to send a pending question to a relatively small group of selected persons—a committee—so that the question may be carefully investigated and put into better condition for the assembly to consider.”

**204.** `[K0228 · Precedence (ordering)]` Put these motions in order of precedence.

   *Answer:* Lay on the Table → Previous Question → Commit or Refer. See RONR (12th ed.) §17:1 — “The motion to Lay on the Table enables the assembly to lay the pending question aside temporarily when something else of immediate urgency has arisen or when something else needs to be addressed before consideration of the pending question is resumed, in such a way that:”

   *Answer:* Lay on the Table. See RONR (12th ed.) §17:1 — “The motion to Lay on the Table enables the assembly to lay the pending question aside temporarily when something else of immediate urgency has arisen or when something else needs to be addressed before consideration of the pending question is resumed, in such a way that:”

**206.** `[K0230 · Precedence (ranking)]` Which of these motions has the lowest precedence?
   A. Postpone to a Certain Time
   B. Postpone Indefinitely
   C. Lay on the Table

   *Answer:* Postpone Indefinitely. See RONR (12th ed.) §11:1 — “Postpone Indefinitely is a motion that the assembly decline to take a position on the main question.”

**207.** `[K0231 · Precedence (ordering)]` Put these motions in order of precedence.

   *Answer:* Lay on the Table → Postpone to a Certain Time → Postpone Indefinitely. See RONR (12th ed.) §17:1 — “The motion to Lay on the Table enables the assembly to lay the pending question aside temporarily when something else of immediate urgency has arisen or when something else needs to be addressed before consideration of the pending question is resumed, in such a way that:”

**208.** `[K0232 · Precedence (ranking)]` Which of these motions has the highest precedence?
   A. Lay on the Table
   B. Recess
   C. Amend
   D. Commit or Refer

   *Answer:* Recess. See RONR (12th ed.) §20:1 — “A recess is a short intermission in the assembly’s proceedings, commonly of only a few minutes, which does not close the meeting and after which business will immediately be resumed at exactly the point where it was interrupted.”

**209.** `[K0233 · Precedence (ranking)]` Which of these motions has the lowest precedence?
   A. Amend
   B. Commit or Refer
   C. Recess
   D. Raise a Question of Privilege

   *Answer:* Amend. See RONR (12th ed.) §12:1 — “The subsidiary motion to Amend is a motion to modify the wording—and within certain limits the meaning—of a pending motion before the pending motion itself is acted upon.”

**210.** `[K0234 · Precedence (ordering)]` Put these motions in order of precedence.

   *Answer:* Recess → Raise a Question of Privilege → Lay on the Table → Commit or Refer → Amend. See RONR (12th ed.) §20:1 — “A recess is a short intermission in the assembly’s proceedings, commonly of only a few minutes, which does not close the meeting and after which business will immediately be resumed at exactly the point where it was interrupted.”

**211.** `[K0235 · Precedence (ranking)]` Which of these motions has the highest precedence?
   A. Limit or Extend Limits of Debate
   B. Postpone Indefinitely
   C. Commit or Refer
   D. Call for the Orders of the Day

   *Answer:* Fix the Time to Which to Adjourn. See RONR (12th ed.) §22:1 — “The object of the motion to Fix the Time to Which to Adjourn (also referred to as the motion to “fix the time for an adjourned meeting”) is to set the time, and sometimes the place, for another meeting to continue business of the session, with no effect on when the present meeting will adjourn.”

**212.** `[K0236 · Precedence (ranking)]` Which of these motions has the lowest precedence?
   A. Call for the Orders of the Day
   B. Limit or Extend Limits of Debate
   C. Fix the Time to Which to Adjourn
   D. Commit or Refer

   *Answer:* Postpone Indefinitely. See RONR (12th ed.) §11:1 — “Postpone Indefinitely is a motion that the assembly decline to take a position on the main question.”

**213.** `[K0237 · Precedence (ordering)]` Put these motions in order of precedence.

   *Answer:* Fix the Time to Which to Adjourn → Call for the Orders of the Day → Limit or Extend Limits of Debate → Commit or Refer → Postpone Indefinitely. See RONR (12th ed.) §22:1 — “The object of the motion to Fix the Time to Which to Adjourn (also referred to as the motion to “fix the time for an adjourned meeting”) is to set the time, and sometimes the place, for another meeting to continue business of the session, with no effect on when the present meeting will adjourn.”

   A. Previous Question
   B. Call for the Orders of the Day

   D. Postpone to a Certain Time

   *Answer:* Fix the Time to Which to Adjourn. See RONR (12th ed.) §22:1 — “The object of the motion to Fix the Time to Which to Adjourn (also referred to as the motion to “fix the time for an adjourned meeting”) is to set the time, and sometimes the place, for another meeting to continue business of the session, with no effect on when the present meeting will adjourn.”

   A. Limit or Extend Limits of Debate
   B. Fix the Time to Which to Adjourn
   C. Previous Question
   D. Call for the Orders of the Day

   *Answer:* Postpone to a Certain Time. See RONR (12th ed.) §14:1 — “The subsidiary motion to Postpone to a Certain Time (or Postpone Definitely, or Postpone) is the motion by which action on a pending question can be put off, within limits, to a definite session, day, meeting, or hour, or until after a certain event.”

See RONR (12th ed.) §22:1 — “The object of the motion to Fix the Time to Which to Adjourn (also referred to as the motion to “fix the time for an adjourned meeting”) is to set the time, and sometimes the place, for another meeting to continue business of the session, with no effect on when the present meeting will adjourn.”

**224.** `[K0248 · Precedence (ranking)]` Which of these motions has the lowest precedence?
   A. Fix the Time to Which to Adjourn
   B. Recess
   C. Raise a Question of Privilege
   D. Call for the Orders of the Day

   *Answer:* Call for the Orders of the Day. See RONR (12th ed.) §18:1 — “A Call for the Orders of the Day is a privileged motion by which a member can require the assembly to conform to its agenda, program, or order of business, or to take up a general or special order that is due to come up at the time (14, 41), unless two thirds of those voting wish to do otherwise.”

   *Answer:* Commit or Refer. See RONR (12th ed.) §13:1 — “The subsidiary motion to Commit or Refer is generally used to send a pending question to a relatively small group of selected persons—a committee—so that the question may be carefully investigated and put into better condition for the assembly to consider.”

**249.** `[K0273 · Precedence (ordering)]` Put these motions in order of precedence.

   *Answer:* Fix the Time to Which to Adjourn → Lay on the Table → Limit or Extend Limits of Debate → Commit or Refer. See RONR (12th ed.) §22:1 — “The object of the motion to Fix the Time to Which to Adjourn (also referred to as the motion to “fix the time for an adjourned meeting”) is to set the time, and sometimes the place, for another meeting to continue business of the session, with no effect on when the present meeting will adjourn.”

**250.** `[K0274 · Precedence (ranking)]` Which of these motions has the highest precedence?
   A. Adjourn
   B. Previous Question
   C. Postpone to a Certain Time
   D. Limit or Extend Limits of Debate

   *Answer:* Fix the Time to Which to Adjourn. See RONR (12th ed.) §22:1 — “The object of the motion to Fix the Time to Which to Adjourn (also referred to as the motion to “fix the time for an adjourned meeting”) is to set the time, and sometimes the place, for another meeting to continue business of the session, with no effect on when the present meeting will adjourn.”

**251.** `[K0275 · Precedence (ranking)]` Which of these motions has the lowest precedence?
   A. Adjourn
   B. Previous Question
   C. Postpone to a Certain Time
   D. Fix the Time to Which to Adjourn

   *Answer:* Postpone to a Certain Time. See RONR (12th ed.) §14:1 — “The subsidiary motion to Postpone to a Certain Time (or Postpone Definitely, or Postpone) is the motion by which action on a pending question can be put off, within limits, to a definite session, day, meeting, or hour, or until after a certain event.”

**252.** `[K0276 · Precedence (ordering)]` Put these motions in order of precedence.

   *Answer:* Fix the Time to Which to Adjourn → Adjourn → Previous Question → Limit or Extend Limits of Debate → Postpone to a Certain Time. See RONR (12th ed.) §22:1 — “The object of the motion to Fix the Time to Which to Adjourn (also referred to as the motion to “fix the time for an adjourned meeting”) is to set the time, and sometimes the place, for another meeting to continue business of the session, with no effect on when the present meeting will adjourn.”

**253.** `[K0277 · Precedence (ranking)]` Which of these motions has the highest precedence?
   A. Call for the Orders of the Day
   B. Postpone Indefinitely
   C. Adjourn
   D. Raise a Question of Privilege

   *Answer:* Fix the Time to Which to Adjourn. See RONR (12th ed.) §22:1 — “The object of the motion to Fix the Time to Which to Adjourn (also referred to as the motion to “fix the time for an adjourned meeting”) is to set the time, and sometimes the place, for another meeting to continue business of the session, with no effect on when the present meeting will adjourn.”

**254.** `[K0278 · Precedence (ranking)]` Which of these motions has the lowest precedence?
   A. Postpone Indefinitely
   B. Call for the Orders of the Day
   C. Raise a Question of Privilege
   D. Fix the Time to Which to Adjourn

   *Answer:* Postpone Indefinitely. See RONR (12th ed.) §11:1 — “Postpone Indefinitely is a motion that the assembly decline to take a position on the main question.”

**255.** `[K0279 · Precedence (ordering)]` Put these motions in order of precedence.

   *Answer:* Fix the Time to Which to Adjourn → Adjourn → Raise a Question of Privilege → Call for the Orders of the Day → Postpone Indefinitely. See RONR (12th ed.) §22:1 — “The object of the motion to Fix the Time to Which to Adjourn (also referred to as the motion to “fix the time for an adjourned meeting”) is to set the time, and sometimes the place, for another meeting to continue business of the session, with no effect on when the present meeting will adjourn.”

**256.** `[K0280 · Precedence (ranking)]` Which of these motions has the highest precedence?
   A. Call for the Orders of the Day
   B. Postpone Indefinitely
   C. Recess
   D. Commit or Refer

   *Answer:* Recess. See RONR (12th ed.) §20:1 — “A recess is a short intermission in the assembly’s proceedings, commonly of only a few minutes, which does not close the meeting and after which business will immediately be resumed at exactly the point where it was interrupted.”

**257.** `[K0281 · Precedence (ranking)]` Which of these motions has the lowest precedence?
   A. Postpone Indefinitely
   B. Recess
   C. Postpone to a Certain Time
   D. Commit or Refer

   *Answer:* Postpone Indefinitely. See RONR (12th ed.) §11:1 — “Postpone Indefinitely is a motion that the assembly decline to take a position on the main question.”

**258.** `[K0282 · Precedence (ordering)]` Put these motions in order of precedence.

   *Answer:* Recess → Call for the Orders of the Day → Postpone to a Certain Time → Commit or Refer → Postpone Indefinitely. See RONR (12th ed.) §20:1 — “A recess is a short intermission in the assembly’s proceedings, commonly of only a few minutes, which does not close the meeting and after which business will immediately be resumed at exactly the point where it was interrupted.”

**259.** `[K0283 · Precedence (ranking)]` Which of these motions has the highest precedence?
   A. Postpone Indefinitely
   B. Adjourn
   C. Postpone to a Certain Time
   D. Amend

   *Answer:* Adjourn. See RONR (12th ed.) §21:1 — “A motion to adjourn may be a privileged or a main motion depending on a number of conditions.”

**260.** `[K0284 · Precedence (ranking)]` Which of these motions has the lowest precedence?
   A. Amend
   B. Adjourn
   C. Recess
   D. Postpone Indefinitely

   *Answer:* Postpone Indefinitely. See RONR (12th ed.) §11:1 — “Postpone Indefinitely is a motion that the assembly decline to take a position on the main question.”

**261.** `[K0285 · Precedence (ordering)]` Put these motions in order of precedence.

   *Answer:* Adjourn → Recess → Postpone to a Certain Time → Amend → Postpone Indefinitely. See RONR (12th ed.) §21:1 — “A motion to adjourn may be a privileged or a main motion depending on a number of conditions.”

   C. Adjourn
   D. Postpone to a Certain Time

   *Answer:* Adjourn. See RONR (12th ed.) §21:1 — “A motion to adjourn may be a privileged or a main motion depending on a number of conditions.”

   *Answer:* Adjourn → Raise a Question of Privilege → Call for the Orders of the Day → Postpone to a Certain Time. See RONR (12th ed.) §21:1 — “A motion to adjourn may be a privileged or a main motion depending on a number of conditions.”

   A. Postpone Indefinitely

   C. Amend

   *Answer:* Postpone Indefinitely. See RONR (12th ed.) §11:1 — “Postpone Indefinitely is a motion that the assembly decline to take a position on the main question.”

**270.** `[K0294 · Precedence (ordering)]` Put these motions in order of precedence.

   *Answer:* Fix the Time to Which to Adjourn → Amend → Postpone Indefinitely. See RONR (12th ed.) §22:1 — “The object of the motion to Fix the Time to Which to Adjourn (also referred to as the motion to “fix the time for an adjourned meeting”) is to set the time, and sometimes the place, for another meeting to continue business of the session, with no effect on when the present meeting will adjourn.”

**271.** `[K0295 · Precedence (ranking)]` Which of these motions has the highest precedence?
   A. Limit or Extend Limits of Debate

   C. Lay on the Table
   D. Postpone to a Certain Time

   *Answer:* Raise a Question of Privilege. See RONR (12th ed.) §19:1 — “To Raise a Question of Privilege is a device that permits a request or main motion relating to the rights and privileges of the assembly or any of its members to be brought up for possible immediate consideration because of its urgency, while business is pending and the request or motion would…”

**275.** `[K0299 · Precedence (ranking)]` Which of these motions has the lowest precedence?
   A. Raise a Question of Privilege
   B. Adjourn
   C. Fix the Time to Which to Adjourn
   D. Previous Question

   *Answer:* Amend. See RONR (12th ed.) §12:1 — “The subsidiary motion to Amend is a motion to modify the wording—and within certain limits the meaning—of a pending motion before the pending motion itself is acted upon.”

**276.** `[K0300 · Precedence (ordering)]` Put these motions in order of precedence.

   *Answer:* Fix the Time to Which to Adjourn → Adjourn → Raise a Question of Privilege → Previous Question → Amend. See RONR (12th ed.) §22:1 — “The object of the motion to Fix the Time to Which to Adjourn (also referred to as the motion to “fix the time for an adjourned meeting”) is to set the time, and sometimes the place, for another meeting to continue business of the session, with no effect on when the present meeting will adjourn.”

**280.** `[K0304 · Precedence (which is higher)]` Which motion takes precedence — Commit or Refer or Recess?
   A. Commit or Refer
   B. Recess

   *Answer:* Recess. See RONR (12th ed.) §20:1 — “A recess is a short intermission in the assembly’s proceedings, commonly of only a few minutes, which does not close the meeting and after which business will immediately be resumed at exactly the point where it was interrupted.”

**281.** `[K0305 · Precedence (which is higher)]` Which motion takes precedence — Recess or Lay on the Table?
   A. Recess
   B. Lay on the Table

   *Answer:* Recess. See RONR (12th ed.) §20:1 — “A recess is a short intermission in the assembly’s proceedings, commonly of only a few minutes, which does not close the meeting and after which business will immediately be resumed at exactly the point where it was interrupted.”

**282.** `[K0306 · Precedence (which is higher)]` Which motion takes precedence — Previous Question or Adjourn?
   A. Previous Question
   B. Adjourn

   *Answer:* Adjourn. See RONR (12th ed.) §21:1 — “A motion to adjourn may be a privileged or a main motion depending on a number of conditions.”

**283.** `[K0307 · Precedence (which is higher)]` Which motion takes precedence — Postpone Indefinitely or Raise a Question of Privilege?
   A. Postpone Indefinitely
   B. Raise a Question of Privilege

See RONR (12th ed.) §19:1 — “To Raise a Question of Privilege is a device that permits a request or main motion relating to the rights and privileges of the assembly or any of its members to be brought up for possible immediate consideration because of its urgency, while business is pending and the request or motion would…”

**284.** `[K0308 · Precedence (which is higher)]` Which motion takes precedence — Commit or Refer or Previous Question?
   A. Commit or Refer
   B. Previous Question

   *Answer:* Previous Question. See RONR (12th ed.) §16:1 — “The Previous Question is the motion used to bring the assembly to an immediate vote on one or more pending questions; its adoption does this with certain exceptions.”

**285.** `[K0309 · Precedence (which is higher)]` Which motion takes precedence — Postpone to a Certain Time or Limit or Extend Limits of Debate?
   A. Postpone to a Certain Time
   B. Limit or Extend Limits of Debate

   *Answer:* Limit or Extend Limits of Debate. See RONR (12th ed.) §15:1 — “The subsidiary motion to Limit or Extend Limits of Debate is one of the two motions by means of which an assembly can exercise special control over debate on a pending question or on a series of pending questions.”

**286.** `[K0310 · Precedence (which is higher)]` Which motion takes precedence — Amend or Postpone to a Certain Time?
   A. Amend
   B. Postpone to a Certain Time

   *Answer:* Postpone to a Certain Time. See RONR (12th ed.) §14:1 — “The subsidiary motion to Postpone to a Certain Time (or Postpone Definitely, or Postpone) is the motion by which action on a pending question can be put off, within limits, to a definite session, day, meeting, or hour, or until after a certain event.”

   A. Fix the Time to Which to Adjourn

   *Answer:* Fix the Time to Which to Adjourn. See RONR (12th ed.) §22:1 — “The object of the motion to Fix the Time to Which to Adjourn (also referred to as the motion to “fix the time for an adjourned meeting”) is to set the time, and sometimes the place, for another meeting to continue business of the session, with no effect on when the present meeting will adjourn.”

**289.** `[K0313 · Precedence (which is higher)]` Which motion takes precedence — Recess or Limit or Extend Limits of Debate?
   A. Recess
   B. Limit or Extend Limits of Debate

   *Answer:* Recess. See RONR (12th ed.) §20:1 — “A recess is a short intermission in the assembly’s proceedings, commonly of only a few minutes, which does not close the meeting and after which business will immediately be resumed at exactly the point where it was interrupted.”

**290.** `[K0314 · Precedence (which is higher)]` Which motion takes precedence — Lay on the Table or Previous Question?

**299.** `[K0323 · Precedence (which is higher)]` Which motion takes precedence — Call for the Orders of the Day or Postpone to a Certain Time?

   B. Postpone to a Certain Time

   *Answer:* Call for the Orders of the Day. See RONR (12th ed.) §18:1 — “A Call for the Orders of the Day is a privileged motion by which a member can require the assembly to conform to its agenda, program, or order of business, or to take up a general or special order that is due to come up at the time (14, 41), unless two thirds of those voting wish to do otherwise.”

See RONR (12th ed.) §22:1 — “The object of the motion to Fix the Time to Which to Adjourn (also referred to as the motion to “fix the time for an adjourned meeting”) is to set the time, and sometimes the place, for another meeting to continue business of the session, with no effect on when the present meeting will adjourn.”

**308.** `[K0332 · Precedence (which is higher)]` Which motion takes precedence — Limit or Extend Limits of Debate or Commit or Refer?
   A. Limit or Extend Limits of Debate
   B. Commit or Refer

   *Answer:* Limit or Extend Limits of Debate. See RONR (12th ed.) §15:1 — “The subsidiary motion to Limit or Extend Limits of Debate is one of the two motions by means of which an assembly can exercise special control over debate on a pending question or on a series of pending questions.”

**309.** `[K0333 · Precedence (which is higher)]` Which motion takes precedence — Amend or Previous Question?
   A. Amend
   B. Previous Question

   *Answer:* Previous Question. See RONR (12th ed.) §16:1 — “The Previous Question is the motion used to bring the assembly to an immediate vote on one or more pending questions; its adoption does this with certain exceptions.”

**310.** `[K0334 · Precedence (which is higher)]` Which motion takes precedence — Adjourn or Postpone to a Certain Time?
   A. Adjourn
   B. Postpone to a Certain Time

   *Answer:* Adjourn. See RONR (12th ed.) §21:1 — “A motion to adjourn may be a privileged or a main motion depending on a number of conditions.”

**311.** `[K0335 · Precedence (which is higher)]` Which motion takes precedence — Limit or Extend Limits of Debate or Call for the Orders of the Day?
   A. Limit or Extend Limits of Debate
   B. Call for the Orders of the Day

   *Answer:* Call for the Orders of the Day. See RONR (12th ed.) §18:1 — “A Call for the Orders of the Day is a privileged motion by which a member can require the assembly to conform to its agenda, program, or order of business, or to take up a general or special order that is due to come up at the time (14, 41), unless two thirds of those voting wish to do otherwise.”

**312.** `[K0336 · Precedence (which is higher)]` Which motion takes precedence — Limit or Extend Limits of Debate or Call for the Orders of the Day?
   A. Limit or Extend Limits of Debate
   B. Call for the Orders of the Day

   *Answer:* Call for the Orders of the Day. See RONR (12th ed.) §18:1 — “A Call for the Orders of the Day is a privileged motion by which a member can require the assembly to conform to its agenda, program, or order of business, or to take up a general or special order that is due to come up at the time (14, 41), unless two thirds of those voting wish to do otherwise.”

**313.** `[K0337 · Precedence (which is higher)]` Which motion takes precedence — Recess or Limit or Extend Limits of Debate?
   A. Recess
   B. Limit or Extend Limits of Debate

   *Answer:* Recess. See RONR (12th ed.) §20:1 — “A recess is a short intermission in the assembly’s proceedings, commonly of only a few minutes, which does not close the meeting and after which business will immediately be resumed at exactly the point where it was interrupted.”

**314.** `[K0338 · Precedence (which is higher)]` Which motion takes precedence — Lay on the Table or Adjourn?
   A. Lay on the Table
   B. Adjourn

   *Answer:* Adjourn. See RONR (12th ed.) §21:1 — “A motion to adjourn may be a privileged or a main motion depending on a number of conditions.”

**315.** `[K0339 · Precedence (which is higher)]` Which motion takes precedence — Call for the Orders of the Day or Postpone to a Certain Time?
   A. Call for the Orders of the Day
   B. Postpone to a Certain Time

   *Answer:* Call for the Orders of the Day. See RONR (12th ed.) §18:1 — “A Call for the Orders of the Day is a privileged motion by which a member can require the assembly to conform to its agenda, program, or order of business, or to take up a general or special order that is due to come up at the time (14, 41), unless two thirds of those voting wish to do otherwise.”

**316.** `[K0340 · Precedence (which is higher)]` Which motion takes precedence — Amend or Adjourn?
   A. Amend
   B. Adjourn

   *Answer:* Adjourn. See RONR (12th ed.) §21:1 — “A motion to adjourn may be a privileged or a main motion depending on a number of conditions.”

**317.** `[K0341 · Precedence (which is higher)]` Which motion takes precedence — Adjourn or Call for the Orders of the Day?
   A. Adjourn
   B. Call for the Orders of the Day

   *Answer:* Adjourn. See RONR (12th ed.) §21:1 — “A motion to adjourn may be a privileged or a main motion depending on a number of conditions.”

**318.** `[K0342 · Precedence (which is higher)]` Which motion takes precedence — Raise a Question of Privilege or Lay on the Table?
   A. Raise a Question of Privilege
   B. Lay on the Table

   *Answer:* Raise a Question of Privilege. See RONR (12th ed.) §19:1 — “To Raise a Question of Privilege is a device that permits a request or main motion relating to the rights and privileges of the assembly or any of its members to be brought up for possible immediate consideration because of its urgency, while business is pending and the request or motion would…”

**319.** `[K0343 · Precedence (which is higher)]` Which motion takes precedence — Commit or Refer or Postpone to a Certain Time?
   A. Commit or Refer
   B. Postpone to a Certain Time

   *Answer:* Postpone to a Certain Time. See RONR (12th ed.) §14:1 — “The subsidiary motion to Postpone to a Certain Time (or Postpone Definitely, or Postpone) is the motion by which action on a pending question can be put off, within limits, to a definite session, day, meeting, or hour, or until after a certain event.”

**320.** `[K0344 · Precedence (which is higher)]` Which motion takes precedence — Recess or Raise a Question of Privilege?
   A. Recess
   B. Raise a Question of Privilege

   *Answer:* Recess. See RONR (12th ed.) §20:1 — “A recess is a short intermission in the assembly’s proceedings, commonly of only a few minutes, which does not close the meeting and after which business will immediately be resumed at exactly the point where it was interrupted.”

**321.** `[K0345 · Precedence (which is higher)]` Which motion takes precedence — Commit or Refer or Previous Question?
   A. Commit or Refer
   B. Previous Question

   *Answer:* Previous Question. See RONR (12th ed.) §16:1 — “The Previous Question is the motion used to bring the assembly to an immediate vote on one or more pending questions; its adoption does this with certain exceptions.”

**322.** `[K0346 · Precedence (which is higher)]` Which motion takes precedence — Previous Question or Commit or Refer?
   A. Previous Question
   B. Commit or Refer

   *Answer:* Previous Question. See RONR (12th ed.) §16:1 — “The Previous Question is the motion used to bring the assembly to an immediate vote on one or more pending questions; its adoption does this with certain exceptions.”

**323.** `[K0347 · Precedence (which is higher)]` Which motion takes precedence — Fix the Time to Which to Adjourn or Call for the Orders of the Day?
   A. Fix the Time to Which to Adjourn
   B. Call for the Orders of the Day

   *Answer:* Fix the Time to Which to Adjourn. See RONR (12th ed.) §22:1 — “The object of the motion to Fix the Time to Which to Adjourn (also referred to as the motion to “fix the time for an adjourned meeting”) is to set the time, and sometimes the place, for another meeting to continue business of the session, with no effect on when the present meeting will adjourn.”

**324.** `[K0348 · Precedence (which is higher)]` Which motion takes precedence — Limit or Extend Limits of Debate or Fix the Time to Which to Adjourn?
   A. Limit or Extend Limits of Debate
   B. Fix the Time to Which to Adjourn

   *Answer:* Fix the Time to Which to Adjourn. See RONR (12th ed.) §22:1 — “The object of the motion to Fix the Time to Which to Adjourn (also referred to as the motion to “fix the time for an adjourned meeting”) is to set the time, and sometimes the place, for another meeting to continue business of the session, with no effect on when the present meeting will adjourn.”

**325.** `[K0349 · Precedence (which is higher)]` Which motion takes precedence — Call for the Orders of the Day or Postpone Indefinitely?
   A. Call for the Orders of the Day
   B. Postpone Indefinitely

   *Answer:* Call for the Orders of the Day. See RONR (12th ed.) §18:1 — “A Call for the Orders of the Day is a privileged motion by which a member can require the assembly to conform to its agenda, program, or order of business, or to take up a general or special order that is due to come up at the time (14, 41), unless two thirds of those voting wish to do otherwise.”

**326.** `[K0350 · Precedence (which is higher)]` Which motion takes precedence — Amend or Recess?
   A. Amend
   B. Recess

   *Answer:* Recess. See RONR (12th ed.) §20:1 — “A recess is a short intermission in the assembly’s proceedings, commonly of only a few minutes, which does not close the meeting and after which business will immediately be resumed at exactly the point where it was interrupted.”

**327.** `[K0351 · Precedence (which is higher)]` Which motion takes precedence — Previous Question or Amend?
   A. Previous Question
   B. Amend

   *Answer:* Previous Question. See RONR (12th ed.) §16:1 — “The Previous Question is the motion used to bring the assembly to an immediate vote on one or more pending questions; its adoption does this with certain exceptions.”

**328.** `[K0352 · Precedence (which is higher)]` Which motion takes precedence — Amend or Postpone to a Certain Time?
   A. Amend
   B. Postpone to a Certain Time

   *Answer:* Postpone to a Certain Time. See RONR (12th ed.) §14:1 — “The subsidiary motion to Postpone to a Certain Time (or Postpone Definitely, or Postpone) is the motion by which action on a pending question can be put off, within limits, to a definite session, day, meeting, or hour, or until after a certain event.”

**329.** `[K0353 · Precedence (which is higher)]` Which motion takes precedence — Postpone Indefinitely or Commit or Refer?
   A. Postpone Indefinitely
   B. Commit or Refer

   *Answer:* Commit or Refer. See RONR (12th ed.) §13:1 — “The subsidiary motion to Commit or Refer is generally used to send a pending question to a relatively small group of selected persons—a committee—so that the question may be carefully investigated and put into better condition for the assembly to consider.”

**330.** `[K0354 · Precedence (which is higher)]` Which motion takes precedence — Previous Question or Amend?
   A. Previous Question
   B. Amend

   *Answer:* Previous Question. See RONR (12th ed.) §16:1 — “The Previous Question is the motion used to bring the assembly to an immediate vote on one or more pending questions; its adoption does this with certain exceptions.”

See RONR (12th ed.) §22:1 — “The object of the motion to Fix the Time to Which to Adjourn (also referred to as the motion to “fix the time for an adjourned meeting”) is to set the time, and sometimes the place, for another meeting to continue business of the session, with no effect on when the present meeting will adjourn.”

**339.** `[K0363 · Precedence (which is higher)]` Which motion takes precedence — Recess or Postpone to a Certain Time?
   A. Recess
   B. Postpone to a Certain Time

   *Answer:* Recess. See RONR (12th ed.) §20:1 — “A recess is a short intermission in the assembly’s proceedings, commonly of only a few minutes, which does not close the meeting and after which business will immediately be resumed at exactly the point where it was interrupted.”

**340.** `[K0364 · Precedence (select all)]` Select ALL of these motions that rank lower than (yield to) the motion to Raise a Question of Privilege.
   A. Adjourn
   B. Postpone Indefinitely
   C. Commit or Refer
   D. Call for the Orders of the Day

   *Answer:* Postpone Indefinitely, Commit or Refer, Call for the Orders of the Day. See RONR (12th ed.) §19:1 — “To Raise a Question of Privilege is a device that permits a request or main motion relating to the rights and privileges of the assembly or any of its members to be brought up for possible immediate consideration because of its urgency, while business is pending and the request or motion would…”

**341.** `[K0365 · Precedence (select all)]` Select ALL of these motions that rank higher than (take precedence over) the motion to Postpone to a Certain Time.
   A. Commit or Refer
   B. Call for the Orders of the Day
   C. Postpone Indefinitely
   D. Amend

   *Answer:* Call for the Orders of the Day. See RONR (12th ed.) §14:1 — “The subsidiary motion to Postpone to a Certain Time (or Postpone Definitely, or Postpone) is the motion by which action on a pending question can be put off, within limits, to a definite session, day, meeting, or hour, or until after a certain event.”

**342.** `[K0366 · Precedence (select all)]` Select ALL of these motions that rank lower than (yield to) the motion to Previous Question.
   A. Amend
   B. Commit or Refer
   C. Recess
   D. Raise a Question of Privilege

   *Answer:* Amend, Commit or Refer. See RONR (12th ed.) §16:1 — “The Previous Question is the motion used to bring the assembly to an immediate vote on one or more pending questions; its adoption does this with certain exceptions.”

**343.** `[K0367 · Precedence (select all)]` Select ALL of these motions that rank lower than (yield to) the motion to Limit or Extend Limits of Debate.
   A. Recess
   B. Postpone Indefinitely
   C. Commit or Refer
   D. Adjourn

   *Answer:* Postpone Indefinitely, Commit or Refer. See RONR (12th ed.) §15:1 — “The subsidiary motion to Limit or Extend Limits of Debate is one of the two motions by means of which an assembly can exercise special control over debate on a pending question or on a series of pending questions.”

**344.** `[K0368 · Precedence (select all)]` Select ALL of these motions that rank lower than (yield to) the motion to Call for the Orders of the Day.
   A. Raise a Question of Privilege
   B. Commit or Refer
   C. Previous Question
   D. Adjourn

   *Answer:* Commit or Refer, Previous Question, Amend. See RONR (12th ed.) §18:1 — “A Call for the Orders of the Day is a privileged motion by which a member can require the assembly to conform to its agenda, program, or order of business, or to take up a general or special order that is due to come up at the time (14, 41), unless two thirds of those voting wish to do otherwise.”

   A. Lay on the Table
   B. Recess
   C. Amend
   D. Fix the Time to Which to Adjourn

   *Answer:* Amend. See RONR (12th ed.) §14:1 — “The subsidiary motion to Postpone to a Certain Time (or Postpone Definitely, or Postpone) is the motion by which action on a pending question can be put off, within limits, to a definite session, day, meeting, or hour, or until after a certain event.”

   D. Call for the Orders of the Day

   D. Amend

   *Answer:* Adjourn. See RONR (12th ed.) §18:1 — “A Call for the Orders of the Day is a privileged motion by which a member can require the assembly to conform to its agenda, program, or order of business, or to take up a general or special order that is due to come up at the time (14, 41), unless two thirds of those voting wish to do otherwise.”

**365.** `[K0389 · Precedence (select all)]` Select ALL of these motions that rank lower than (yield to) the motion to Raise a Question of Privilege.
   A. Call for the Orders of the Day
   B. Lay on the Table
   C. Amend
   D. Limit or Extend Limits of Debate

   *Answer:* Call for the Orders of the Day, Lay on the Table, Amend, Limit or Extend Limits of Debate. See RONR (12th ed.) §19:1 — “To Raise a Question of Privilege is a device that permits a request or main motion relating to the rights and privileges of the assembly or any of its members to be brought up for possible immediate consideration because of its urgency, while business is pending and the request or motion would…”

**366.** `[K0390 · Precedence (select all)]` Select ALL of these motions that rank lower than (yield to) the motion to Call for the Orders of the Day.
   A. Fix the Time to Which to Adjourn
   B. Limit or Extend Limits of Debate
   C. Postpone to a Certain Time
   D. Previous Question

   *Answer:* Limit or Extend Limits of Debate, Postpone to a Certain Time, Previous Question, Lay on the Table. See RONR (12th ed.) §18:1 — “A Call for the Orders of the Day is a privileged motion by which a member can require the assembly to conform to its agenda, program, or order of business, or to take up a general or special order that is due to come up at the time (14, 41), unless two thirds of those voting wish to do otherwise.”

**367.** `[K0391 · Precedence (select all)]` Select ALL of these motions that rank lower than (yield to) the motion to Call for the Orders of the Day.
   A. Fix the Time to Which to Adjourn
   B. Previous Question
   C. Lay on the Table
   D. Limit or Extend Limits of Debate

   *Answer:* Previous Question, Lay on the Table, Limit or Extend Limits of Debate. See RONR (12th ed.) §18:1 — “A Call for the Orders of the Day is a privileged motion by which a member can require the assembly to conform to its agenda, program, or order of business, or to take up a general or special order that is due to come up at the time (14, 41), unless two thirds of those voting wish to do otherwise.”

**368.** `[K0392 · Precedence (select all)]` Select ALL of these motions that rank lower than (yield to) the motion to Commit or Refer.
   A. Postpone Indefinitely
   B. Call for the Orders of the Day
   C. Adjourn
   D. Previous Question

   *Answer:* Postpone Indefinitely. See RONR (12th ed.) §13:1 — “The subsidiary motion to Commit or Refer is generally used to send a pending question to a relatively small group of selected persons—a committee—so that the question may be carefully investigated and put into better condition for the assembly to consider.”

**369.** `[K0393 · Precedence (select all)]` Select ALL of these motions that rank lower than (yield to) the motion to Commit or Refer.
   A. Call for the Orders of the Day
   B. Postpone Indefinitely
   C. Previous Question
   D. Lay on the Table

   *Answer:* Postpone Indefinitely. See RONR (12th ed.) §13:1 — “The subsidiary motion to Commit or Refer is generally used to send a pending question to a relatively small group of selected persons—a committee—so that the question may be carefully investigated and put into better condition for the assembly to consider.”

**370.** `[K0394 · Precedence (select all)]` Select ALL of these motions that rank higher than (take precedence over) the motion to Call for the Orders of the Day.
   A. Limit or Extend Limits of Debate
   B. Raise a Question of Privilege
   C. Adjourn
   D. Lay on the Table

   *Answer:* Raise a Question of Privilege, Adjourn, Recess. See RONR (12th ed.) §18:1 — “A Call for the Orders of the Day is a privileged motion by which a member can require the assembly to conform to its agenda, program, or order of business, or to take up a general or special order that is due to come up at the time (14, 41), unless two thirds of those voting wish to do otherwise.”

**371.** `[K0395 · Precedence (select all)]` Select ALL of these motions that rank higher than (take precedence over) the motion to Call for the Orders of the Day.
   A. Adjourn
   B. Commit or Refer
   C. Previous Question
   D. Recess

   *Answer:* Adjourn, Recess. See RONR (12th ed.) §18:1 — “A Call for the Orders of the Day is a privileged motion by which a member can require the assembly to conform to its agenda, program, or order of business, or to take up a general or special order that is due to come up at the time (14, 41), unless two thirds of those voting wish to do otherwise.”

**372.** `[K0396 · Precedence (select all)]` Select ALL of these motions that rank lower than (yield to) the motion to Limit or Extend Limits of Debate.
   A. Amend
   B. Lay on the Table
   C. Previous Question
   D. Recess

   *Answer:* Amend, Postpone Indefinitely. See RONR (12th ed.) §15:1 — “The subsidiary motion to Limit or Extend Limits of Debate is one of the two motions by means of which an assembly can exercise special control over debate on a pending question or on a series of pending questions.”

**373.** `[K0397 · Precedence (select all)]` Select ALL of these motions that rank lower than (yield to) the motion to Lay on the Table.
   A. Postpone Indefinitely
   B. Amend
   C. Previous Question
   D. Raise a Question of Privilege

   *Answer:* Postpone Indefinitely, Amend, Previous Question. See RONR (12th ed.) §17:1 — “The motion to Lay on the Table enables the assembly to lay the pending question aside temporarily when something else of immediate urgency has arisen or when something else needs to be addressed before consideration of the pending question is resumed, in such a way that:”

**374.** `[K0398 · Precedence (select all)]` Select ALL of these motions that rank lower than (yield to) the motion to Limit or Extend Limits of Debate.
   A. Fix the Time to Which to Adjourn
   B. Amend
   C. Call for the Orders of the Day
   D. Postpone to a Certain Time

   *Answer:* Amend, Postpone to a Certain Time, Commit or Refer. See RONR (12th ed.) §15:1 — “The subsidiary motion to Limit or Extend Limits of Debate is one of the two motions by means of which an assembly can exercise special control over debate on a pending question or on a series of pending questions.”

**375.** `[K0399 · Precedence (select all)]` Select ALL of these motions that rank lower than (yield to) the motion to Previous Question.
   A. Postpone Indefinitely
   B. Fix the Time to Which to Adjourn
   C. Call for the Orders of the Day
   D. Limit or Extend Limits of Debate

   *Answer:* Postpone Indefinitely, Limit or Extend Limits of Debate. See RONR (12th ed.) §16:1 — “The Previous Question is the motion used to bring the assembly to an immediate vote on one or more pending questions; its adoption does this with certain exceptions.”

**376.** `[K0400 · Precedence (select all)]` Select ALL of these motions that rank lower than (yield to) the motion to Limit or Extend Limits of Debate.
   A. Amend
   B. Fix the Time to Which to Adjourn
   C. Recess
   D. Postpone Indefinitely

   *Answer:* Amend, Postpone Indefinitely. See RONR (12th ed.) §15:1 — “The subsidiary motion to Limit or Extend Limits of Debate is one of the two motions by means of which an assembly can exercise special control over debate on a pending question or on a series of pending questions.”

**377.** `[K0401 · Precedence (select all)]` Select ALL of these motions that rank lower than (yield to) the motion to Previous Question.
   A. Limit or Extend Limits of Debate
   B. Postpone Indefinitely
   C. Raise a Question of Privilege
   D. Postpone to a Certain Time

   *Answer:* Limit or Extend Limits of Debate, Postpone Indefinitely, Postpone to a Certain Time. See RONR (12th ed.) §16:1 — “The Previous Question is the motion used to bring the assembly to an immediate vote on one or more pending questions; its adoption does this with certain exceptions.”

**378.** `[K0402 · Precedence (select all)]` Select ALL of these motions that rank lower than (yield to) the motion to Lay on the Table.
   A. Recess
   B. Postpone to a Certain Time
   C. Raise a Question of Privilege
   D. Call for the Orders of the Day

   *Answer:* Postpone to a Certain Time. See RONR (12th ed.) §17:1 — “The motion to Lay on the Table enables the assembly to lay the pending question aside temporarily when something else of immediate urgency has arisen or when something else needs to be addressed before consideration of the pending question is resumed, in such a way that:”

**379.** `[K0403 · Precedence (select all)]` Select ALL of these motions that rank lower than (yield to) the motion to Recess.
   A. Postpone to a Certain Time
   B. Fix the Time to Which to Adjourn
   C. Lay on the Table
   D. Previous Question

   *Answer:* Postpone to a Certain Time, Lay on the Table, Previous Question. See RONR (12th ed.) §20:1 — “A recess is a short intermission in the assembly’s proceedings, commonly of only a few minutes, which does not close the meeting and after which business will immediately be resumed at exactly the point where it was interrupted.”

**380.** `[K0404 · Precedence (select all)]` Select ALL of these motions that rank higher than (take precedence over) the motion to Recess.
   A. Postpone Indefinitely
   B. Amend
   C. Previous Question
   D. Postpone to a Certain Time

   *Answer:* Fix the Time to Which to Adjourn. See RONR (12th ed.) §20:1 — “A recess is a short intermission in the assembly’s proceedings, commonly of only a few minutes, which does not close the meeting and after which business will immediately be resumed at exactly the point where it was interrupted.”

**381.** `[K0405 · Precedence (select all)]` Select ALL of these motions that rank lower than (yield to) the motion to Lay on the Table.
   A. Postpone to a Certain Time
   B. Recess
   C. Raise a Question of Privilege
   D. Amend

   *Answer:* Postpone to a Certain Time, Amend, Previous Question. See RONR (12th ed.) §17:1 — “The motion to Lay on the Table enables the assembly to lay the pending question aside temporarily when something else of immediate urgency has arisen or when something else needs to be addressed before consideration of the pending question is resumed, in such a way that:”

**382.** `[K0406 · Precedence (select all)]` Select ALL of these motions that rank higher than (take precedence over) the motion to Lay on the Table.
   A. Call for the Orders of the Day
   B. Postpone to a Certain Time
   C. Amend
   D. Previous Question

   *Answer:* Call for the Orders of the Day. See RONR (12th ed.) §17:1 — “The motion to Lay on the Table enables the assembly to lay the pending question aside temporarily when something else of immediate urgency has arisen or when something else needs to be addressed before consideration of the pending question is resumed, in such a way that:”

**383.** `[K0407 · Precedence (select all)]` Select ALL of these motions that rank lower than (yield to) the motion to Call for the Orders of the Day.
   A. Commit or Refer
   B. Postpone to a Certain Time
   C. Postpone Indefinitely
   D. Adjourn

   *Answer:* Commit or Refer, Postpone to a Certain Time, Postpone Indefinitely. See RONR (12th ed.) §18:1 — “A Call for the Orders of the Day is a privileged motion by which a member can require the assembly to conform to its agenda, program, or order of business, or to take up a general or special order that is due to come up at the time (14, 41), unless two thirds of those voting wish to do otherwise.”

**384.** `[K0408 · Precedence (select all)]` Select ALL of these motions that rank lower than (yield to) the motion to Adjourn.
   A. Commit or Refer
   B. Amend
   C. Previous Question
   D. Fix the Time to Which to Adjourn

   *Answer:* Commit or Refer, Amend, Previous Question. See RONR (12th ed.) §21:1 — “A motion to adjourn may be a privileged or a main motion depending on a number of conditions.”

**385.** `[K0409 · Precedence (select all)]` Select ALL of these motions that rank higher than (take precedence over) the motion to Lay on the Table.
   A. Call for the Orders of the Day
   B. Previous Question
   C. Postpone to a Certain Time
   D. Amend

   *Answer:* Call for the Orders of the Day, Fix the Time to Which to Adjourn. See RONR (12th ed.) §17:1 — “The motion to Lay on the Table enables the assembly to lay the pending question aside temporarily when something else of immediate urgency has arisen or when something else needs to be addressed before consideration of the pending question is resumed, in such a way that:”

**386.** `[K0410 · Precedence (select all)]` Select ALL of these motions that rank lower than (yield to) the motion to Postpone to a Certain Time.
   A. Postpone Indefinitely
   B. Previous Question
   C. Recess
   D. Adjourn

   *Answer:* Postpone Indefinitely. See RONR (12th ed.) §14:1 — “The subsidiary motion to Postpone to a Certain Time (or Postpone Definitely, or Postpone) is the motion by which action on a pending question can be put off, within limits, to a definite session, day, meeting, or hour, or until after a certain event.”

**387.** `[K0411 · Precedence (select all)]` Select ALL of these motions that rank higher than (take precedence over) the motion to Postpone to a Certain Time.
   A. Adjourn
   B. Previous Question
   C. Recess
   D. Commit or Refer

   *Answer:* Adjourn, Previous Question, Recess. See RONR (12th ed.) §14:1 — “The subsidiary motion to Postpone to a Certain Time (or Postpone Definitely, or Postpone) is the motion by which action on a pending question can be put off, within limits, to a definite session, day, meeting, or hour, or until after a certain event.”

**388.** `[K0412 · Precedence (select all)]` Select ALL of these motions that rank lower than (yield to) the motion to Limit or Extend Limits of Debate.
   A. Postpone Indefinitely
   B. Recess
   C. Adjourn
   D. Lay on the Table

   *Answer:* Postpone Indefinitely, Commit or Refer. See RONR (12th ed.) §15:1 — “The subsidiary motion to Limit or Extend Limits of Debate is one of the two motions by means of which an assembly can exercise special control over debate on a pending question or on a series of pending questions.”

**389.** `[K0413 · Precedence (select all)]` Select ALL of these motions that rank higher than (take precedence over) the motion to Limit or Extend Limits of Debate.
   A. Lay on the Table
   B. Postpone Indefinitely
   C. Adjourn
   D. Amend

   *Answer:* Lay on the Table, Adjourn. See RONR (12th ed.) §15:1 — “The subsidiary motion to Limit or Extend Limits of Debate is one of the two motions by means of which an assembly can exercise special control over debate on a pending question or on a series of pending questions.”

**390.** `[K0414 · Precedence (select all)]` Select ALL of these motions that rank higher than (take precedence over) the motion to Postpone to a Certain Time.
   A. Limit or Extend Limits of Debate
   B. Postpone Indefinitely
   C. Recess
   D. Commit or Refer

   *Answer:* Limit or Extend Limits of Debate, Recess, Raise a Question of Privilege. See RONR (12th ed.) §14:1 — “The subsidiary motion to Postpone to a Certain Time (or Postpone Definitely, or Postpone) is the motion by which action on a pending question can be put off, within limits, to a definite session, day, meeting, or hour, or until after a certain event.”

**391.** `[K0415 · Precedence (select all)]` Select ALL of these motions that rank lower than (yield to) the motion to Lay on the Table.
   A. Amend
   B. Recess
   C. Limit or Extend Limits of Debate
   D. Previous Question

   *Answer:* Amend, Limit or Extend Limits of Debate, Previous Question. See RONR (12th ed.) §17:1 — “The motion to Lay on the Table enables the assembly to lay the pending question aside temporarily when something else of immediate urgency has arisen or when something else needs to be addressed before consideration of the pending question is resumed, in such a way that:”

**392.** `[K0416 · Precedence (select all)]` Select ALL of these motions that rank lower than (yield to) the motion to Limit or Extend Limits of Debate.
   A. Call for the Orders of the Day
   B. Previous Question
   C. Recess
   D. Postpone Indefinitely

   *Answer:* Postpone Indefinitely. See RONR (12th ed.) §15:1 — “The subsidiary motion to Limit or Extend Limits of Debate is one of the two motions by means of which an assembly can exercise special control over debate on a pending question or on a series of pending questions.”

**393.** `[K0417 · Precedence (select all)]` Select ALL of these motions that rank higher than (take precedence over) the motion to Adjourn.
   A. Limit or Extend Limits of Debate
   B. Postpone to a Certain Time
   C. Fix the Time to Which to Adjourn
   D. Recess

   *Answer:* Fix the Time to Which to Adjourn. See RONR (12th ed.) §21:1 — “A motion to adjourn may be a privileged or a main motion depending on a number of conditions.”

**394.** `[K0418 · Precedence (select all)]` Select ALL of these motions that rank lower than (yield to) the motion to Amend.
   A. Postpone Indefinitely
   B. Fix the Time to Which to Adjourn
   C. Postpone to a Certain Time
   D. Lay on the Table

   *Answer:* Postpone Indefinitely. See RONR (12th ed.) §12:1 — “The subsidiary motion to Amend is a motion to modify the wording—and within certain limits the meaning—of a pending motion before the pending motion itself is acted upon.”

**395.** `[K0419 · Precedence (select all)]` Select ALL of these motions that rank lower than (yield to) the motion to Call for the Orders of the Day.
   A. Lay on the Table
   B. Amend
   C. Raise a Question of Privilege
   D. Limit or Extend Limits of Debate

   *Answer:* Lay on the Table, Amend, Limit or Extend Limits of Debate. See RONR (12th ed.) §18:1 — “A Call for the Orders of the Day is a privileged motion by which a member can require the assembly to conform to its agenda, program, or order of business, or to take up a general or special order that is due to come up at the time (14, 41), unless two thirds of those voting wish to do otherwise.”

   *Answer:* Adjourn. See RONR (12th ed.) §19:1 — “To Raise a Question of Privilege is a device that permits a request or main motion relating to the rights and privileges of the assembly or any of its members to be brought up for possible immediate consideration because of its urgency, while business is pending and the request or motion would…”

**397.** `[K0421 · Precedence (select all)]` Select ALL of these motions that rank higher than (take precedence over) the motion to Previous Question.
   A. Postpone Indefinitely
   B. Recess
   C. Adjourn
   D. Raise a Question of Privilege

   *Answer:* Recess, Adjourn, Raise a Question of Privilege. See RONR (12th ed.) §16:1 — “The Previous Question is the motion used to bring the assembly to an immediate vote on one or more pending questions; its adoption does this with certain exceptions.”

**398.** `[K0422 · Precedence (select all)]` Select ALL of these motions that rank higher than (take precedence over) the motion to Amend.
   A. Postpone Indefinitely
   B. Postpone to a Certain Time

   D. Commit or Refer

   *Answer:* Postpone to a Certain Time, Call for the Orders of the Day, Commit or Refer, Adjourn. See RONR (12th ed.) §12:1 — “The subsidiary motion to Amend is a motion to modify the wording—and within certain limits the meaning—of a pending motion before the pending motion itself is acted upon.”

   D. Raise a Question of Privilege

   *Answer:* Adjourn. See RONR (12th ed.) §20:1 — “A recess is a short intermission in the assembly’s proceedings, commonly of only a few minutes, which does not close the meeting and after which business will immediately be resumed at exactly the point where it was interrupted.”

**414.** `[C0288 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: _____ is a motion that the assembly decline to take a position on the main question.

   *Answer:* Postpone Indefinitely — “Postpone Indefinitely is a motion that the assembly decline to take a position on the main question.”. See RONR (12th ed.) §11:1 — “Postpone Indefinitely is a motion that the assembly decline to take a position on the main question.”

**415.** `[C0288 · Applied multiple-choice]` Fill in the blank: _____ is a motion that the assembly decline to take a position on the main question.
   A. Postpone Indefinitely
   B. Repeal
   C. in some way
   D. four

   *Answer:* A) Postpone Indefinitely. See RONR (12th ed.) §11:1 — “Postpone Indefinitely is a motion that the assembly decline to take a position on the main question.”

**416.** `[C0289 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: Consequently, the adoption of the motion to Postpone _____ is in effect an indirect rejection of the main motion.

See RONR (12th ed.) §11:3 — “Consequently, the adoption of the motion to Postpone Indefinitely is in effect an indirect rejection of the main motion.”

   C. whether to order
   D. Indefinitely

   *Answer:* Postpone — “Consequently, the adoption of the motion to Postpone Indefinitely is in effect an indirect rejection of the main motion.”. See RONR (12th ed.) §11:3 — “Consequently, the adoption of the motion to Postpone Indefinitely is in effect an indirect rejection of the main motion.”

   A. other than the reporting officer

   D. Postpone

   *Answer:* D) Postpone. See RONR (12th ed.) §11:3 — “Consequently, the adoption of the motion to Postpone Indefinitely is in effect an indirect rejection of the main motion.”

   A. executive secretary,
   B. Postpone Indefinitely

   D. beyond

   *Answer:* B) Postpone Indefinitely. See RONR (12th ed.) §11:4 — “Effect of Referral (of the Main Motion) on a Pending Motion to Postpone Indefinitely.”

**422.** `[C0290 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: If a main motion is referred to a committee while Postpone Indefinitely is pending, the latter motion is ignored and does not go to the committee, since the adoption of the motion to _____ indicates that the assembly is not in favor of postponing indefinitely.

   *Answer:* Commit — “If a main motion is referred to a committee while Postpone Indefinitely is pending, the latter motion is ignored and does not go to the committee, since the adoption of the motion to Commit indicates that the assembly is not in favor of postponing indefinitely.”. See RONR (12th ed.) §11:4 — “If a main motion is referred to a committee while Postpone Indefinitely is pending, the latter motion is ignored and does not go to the committee, since the adoption of the motion to Commit indicates that the assembly is not in favor of postponing indefinitely.”

**423.** `[C0290 · Applied multiple-choice]` Fill in the blank: If a main motion is referred to a committee while Postpone Indefinitely is pending, the latter motion is ignored and does not go to the committee, since the adoption of the motion to _____ indicates that the assembly is not in favor of postponing indefinitely.
   A. the assembly or any of its members
   B. Consideration of the Question
   C. a Question Taken from the Table
   D. Commit

   *Answer:* D) Commit. See RONR (12th ed.) §11:4 — “If a main motion is referred to a committee while Postpone Indefinitely is pending, the latter motion is ignored and does not go to the committee, since the adoption of the motion to Commit indicates that the assembly is not in favor of postponing indefinitely.”

**428.** `[C0292 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: Assume that the following resolution is pending in a meeting of a local unit of a state professional society: “_____, That the Ferndale Unit endorse the State President of the Society, James Thornton, for the office of United States Senator.” Debate creates a delicate situation.

   *Answer:* Resolved — “Assume that the following resolution is pending in a meeting of a local unit of a state professional society: “Resolved, That the Ferndale Unit endorse the State President of the Society, James Thornton, for the office of United States Senator.” Debate creates a delicate situation.”. **475.** `[C0309 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: To be germane, an amendment must _____ involve the same question that is raised by the motion to which it is applied.

   *Answer:* in some way — “To be germane, an amendment must in some way involve the same question that is raised by the motion to which it is applied.”. See RONR (12th ed.) §12:16 — “To be germane, an amendment must in some way involve the same question that is raised by the motion to which it is applied.”

**476.** `[C0309 · Applied multiple-choice]` Fill in the blank: To be germane, an amendment must _____ involve the same question that is raised by the motion to which it is applied.
   A. floor
   B. the Day
   C. presents
   D. in some way

   *Answer:* D) in some way. See RONR (12th ed.) §12:16 — “To be germane, an amendment must in some way involve the same question that is raised by the motion to which it is applied.”

**477.** `[C0309 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: When a secondary amendment proposes a change in the _____, it must be germane to that primary amendment—not just to the motion the primary amendment would change.

   *Answer:* primary amendment — “When a secondary amendment proposes a change in the primary amendment, it must be germane to that primary amendment—not just to the motion the primary amendment would change.”. See RONR (12th ed.) §12:16 — “When a secondary amendment proposes a change in the primary amendment, it must be germane to that primary amendment—not just to the motion the primary amendment would change.”

**478.** `[C0309 · Applied multiple-choice]` Fill in the blank: When a secondary amendment proposes a change in the _____, it must be germane to that primary amendment—not just to the motion the primary amendment would change.
   A. renewed
   B. postponed
   C. hearing
   D. primary amendment

   *Answer:* D) primary amendment. See RONR (12th ed.) §12:16 — “When a secondary amendment proposes a change in the primary amendment, it must be germane to that primary amendment—not just to the motion the primary amendment would change.”

**479.** `[C0309 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: As already stated, an amendment must be _____ to be in order.

   *Answer:* germane — “As already stated, an amendment must be germane to be in order.”. See RONR (12th ed.) §12:16 — “As already stated, an amendment must be germane to be in order.”

**480.** `[C0311 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: By these rules, if a proposed amendment is related to the main motion in such a way that, after the adoption, rejection, or temporary disposal of the present main motion, the essential idea of the amendment could _____ be introduced as an independent resolution during the same session, the amendment is germane and must be admitted, since there will not, or may not, be any opportunity to present it later.

   *Answer:* not — “By these rules, if a proposed amendment is related to the main motion in such a way that, after the adoption, rejection, or temporary disposal of the present main motion, the essential idea of the amendment could not be introduced as an independent resolution during the same session, the amendment is germane and must be admitted, since there will not, or may not, be any opportunity to present it later.”. See RONR (12th ed.) §12:18 — “By these rules, if a proposed amendment is related to the main motion in such a way that, after the adoption, rejection, or temporary disposal of the present main motion, the essential idea of the amendment could not be introduced as an independent resolution during the same session, the amendment is germane and must be admitted, since there will not, or may not, be any opportunity to present it later.”

**481.** `[C0311 · Applied multiple-choice]` Fill in the blank: By these rules, if a proposed amendment is related to the main motion in such a way that, after the adoption, rejection, or temporary disposal of the present main motion, the essential idea of the amendment could _____ be introduced as an independent resolution during the same session, the amendment is germane and must be admitted, since there will not, or may not, be any opportunity to present it later.
   A. Objection to the Consideration
   B. not
   C. Voting by Ballot
   D. adopt,

   *Answer:* B) not. See RONR (12th ed.) §12:18 — “By these rules, if a proposed amendment is related to the main motion in such a way that, after the adoption, rejection, or temporary disposal of the present main motion, the essential idea of the amendment could not be introduced as an independent resolution during the same session, the amendment is germane and must be admitted, since there will not, or may not, be any opportunity to present it later.”

**482.** `[C0313 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: Or assume that the following is the pending motion: “that the City Council commend Officer George for his action in…” An amendment to strike out “commend” and insert “_____,” although antagonistic to the original intent, is germane and in order because both ideas deal with the council’s opinion of the officer’s action.

   *Answer:* censure — “Or assume that the following is the pending motion: “that the City Council commend Officer George for his action in…” An amendment to strike out “commend” and insert “censure,” although antagonistic to the original intent, is germane and in order because both ideas deal with the council’s opinion of the officer’s action.”. See RONR (12th ed.) §12:20 — “Or assume that the following is the pending motion: “that the City Council commend Officer George for his action in…” An amendment to strike out “commend” and insert “censure,” although antagonistic to the original intent, is germane and in order because both ideas deal with the council’s opinion of the officer’s action.”

**483.** `[C0313 · Applied multiple-choice]` Fill in the blank: Or assume that the following is the pending motion: “that the City Council commend Officer George for his action in…” An amendment to strike out “commend” and insert “_____,” although antagonistic to the original intent, is germane and in order because both ideas deal with the council’s opinion of the officer’s action.
   A. Raise a Question of
   B. closing
   C. Fix the Time to Which to Adjourn
   D. censure

   *Answer:* D) censure. See RONR (12th ed.) §12:20 — “Or assume that the following is the pending motion: “that the City Council commend Officer George for his action in…” An amendment to strike out “commend” and insert “censure,” although antagonistic to the original intent, is germane and in order because both ideas deal with the council’s opinion of the officer’s action.”

**484.** `[C0313 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: It should be noted that censure is different from _____ (see Improper Amendments, below).

   *Answer:* not commend — “It should be noted that censure is different from not commend (see Improper Amendments, below).”. See RONR (12th ed.) §12:20 — “It should be noted that censure is different from not commend (see Improper Amendments, below).”

**485.** `[C0313 · Applied multiple-choice]` Fill in the blank: It should be noted that censure is different from _____ (see Improper Amendments, below).
   A. not commend
   B. rights
   C. Resolved,
   D. It should be emphasized that neither

   *Answer:* A) not commend. See RONR (12th ed.) §12:20 — “It should be noted that censure is different from not commend (see Improper Amendments, below).”

**486.** `[C0313 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: Or assume that the following is the pending motion: “that the City Council commend Officer George for his action in…” An amendment to strike out “commend” and insert “censure,” although antagonistic to the original intent, is germane and in _____ because both ideas deal with the council’s opinion of the officer’s action.

   *Answer:* order — “Or assume that the following is the pending motion: “that the City Council commend Officer George for his action in…” An amendment to strike out “commend” and insert “censure,” although antagonistic to the original intent, is germane and in order because both ideas deal with the council’s opinion of the officer’s action.”. See RONR (12th ed.) §12:20 — “Or assume that the following is the pending motion: “that the City Council commend Officer George for his action in…” An amendment to strike out “commend” and insert “censure,” although antagonistic to the original intent, is germane and in order because both ideas deal with the council’s opinion of the officer’s action.”

**487.** `[C0314 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: Those of the opinion that the amendment is germane, say _____.

   *Answer:* aye — “Those of the opinion that the amendment is germane, say aye.”. See RONR (12th ed.) §12:21 — “Those of the opinion that the amendment is germane, say aye.”

**488.** `[C0314 · Applied multiple-choice]` Fill in the blank: Those of the opinion that the amendment is germane, say _____.
   A. recording secretary
   B. aye
   C. bylaws
   D. After the completion of new business

   *Answer:* B) aye. See RONR (12th ed.) §12:21 — “Those of the opinion that the amendment is germane, say aye.”

**489.** `[C0316 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: Rules for the Different _____s of Amendment.

   *Answer:* Form — “Rules for the Different Forms of Amendment.”. See RONR (12th ed.) §12:24 — “Rules for the Different Forms of Amendment.”

**490.** `[C0316 · Applied multiple-choice]` Fill in the blank: Rules for the Different _____s of Amendment.
   A. original main motions
   B. at the
   C. the assembly or any of its members
   D. Form

   *Answer:* D) Form. See RONR (12th ed.) §12:24 — “Rules for the Different Forms of Amendment.”

**491.** `[C0316 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: Rules for the Different Forms of _____ment.

   *Answer:* Amend — “Rules for the Different Forms of Amendment.”. See RONR (12th ed.) §12:24 — “Rules for the Different Forms of Amendment.”

**492.** `[C0316 · Applied multiple-choice]` Fill in the blank: Rules for the Different Forms of _____ment.
   A. special order
   B. to a Certain Time
   C. constitution,
   D. Amend

   *Answer:* D) Amend. See RONR (12th ed.) §12:24 — “Rules for the Different Forms of Amendment.”

**493.** `[C0317 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: It should be noted that many of the rules governing the different forms of amendment are particular applications of the following principle: After the assembly has voted that certain words (or a certain paragraph) shall, or shall not, form part of a pending resolution, it is not in order, during the same session at which that vote was taken, to make another motion to _____ that raises the same question of content and effect.

   *Answer:* Amend — “It should be noted that many of the rules governing the different forms of amendment are particular applications of the following principle: After the assembly has voted that certain words (or a certain paragraph) shall, or shall not, form part of a pending resolution, it is not in order, during the same session at which that vote was taken, to make another motion to Amend that raises the same question of content and effect.”. See RONR (12th ed.) §12:25 — “It should be noted that many of the rules governing the different forms of amendment are particular applications of the following principle: After the assembly has voted that certain words (or a certain paragraph) shall, or shall not, form part of a pending resolution, it is not in order, during the same session at which that vote was taken, to make another motion to Amend that raises the same question of content and effect.”

**494.** `[C0317 · Applied multiple-choice]` Fill in the blank: It should be noted that many of the rules governing the different forms of amendment are particular applications of the following principle: After the assembly has voted that certain words (or a certain paragraph) shall, or shall not, form part of a pending resolution, it is not in order, during the same session at which that vote was taken, to make another motion to _____ that raises the same question of content and effect.
   A. immediately pending question
   B. committee reports
   C. To Read Papers
   D. Amend

   *Answer:* D) Amend.
