**976.** `[C0571 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: The chair then puts to vote the motion to _____.

   *Answer:* Fix the Time to Which to Adjourn — “The chair then puts to vote the motion to Fix the Time to Which to Adjourn.”. See RONR (12th ed.) §22:15 — “The chair then puts to vote the motion to Fix the Time to Which to Adjourn.”

**977.** `[C0573 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: As a second example, assume that the motion to Fix the _____ is to be made with a view to immediate adjournment to a specified time, when this purpose cannot be reached by a main motion:

   *Answer:* Time to Which to Adjourn — “As a second example, assume that the motion to Fix the Time to Which to Adjourn is to be made with a view to immediate adjournment to a specified time, when this purpose cannot be reached by a main motion:”. See RONR (12th ed.) §22:17 — “As a second example, assume that the motion to Fix the Time to Which to Adjourn is to be made with a view to immediate adjournment to a specified time, when this purpose cannot be reached by a main motion:”

**978.** `[C0573 · Applied multiple-choice]` Fill in the blank: As a second example, assume that the motion to Fix the _____ is to be made with a view to immediate adjournment to a specified time, when this purpose cannot be reached by a main motion:
   A. Postpone to a Certain
   B. of a Question
   C. Time to Which to Adjourn
   D. Something Previously Adopted

   *Answer:* C) Time to Which to Adjourn. See RONR (12th ed.) §22:17 — “As a second example, assume that the motion to Fix the Time to Which to Adjourn is to be made with a view to immediate adjournment to a specified time, when this purpose cannot be reached by a main motion:”

**979.** `[C0573 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: As a second example, assume that the motion to _____ Time to Which to Adjourn is to be made with a view to immediate adjournment to a specified time, when this purpose cannot be reached by a main motion:

   *Answer:* Fix the — “As a second example, assume that the motion to Fix the Time to Which to Adjourn is to be made with a view to immediate adjournment to a specified time, when this purpose cannot be reached by a main motion:”. See RONR (12th ed.) §22:17 — “As a second example, assume that the motion to Fix the Time to Which to Adjourn is to be made with a view to immediate adjournment to a specified time, when this purpose cannot be reached by a main motion:”

**980.** `[C0573 · Applied multiple-choice]` Fill in the blank: As a second example, assume that the motion to _____ Time to Which to Adjourn is to be made with a view to immediate adjournment to a specified time, when this purpose cannot be reached by a main motion:
   A. not commend
   B. Roll-Call Vote

**981.** `[C0576 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: The chair states the question on the motion to _____ and immediately puts it to vote.

   *Answer:* Adjourn — “The chair states the question on the motion to Adjourn and immediately puts it to vote.”. See RONR (12th ed.) §22:20 — “The chair states the question on the motion to Adjourn and immediately puts it to vote.”

**982.** `[C0576 · Applied multiple-choice]` Fill in the blank: The chair states the question on the motion to _____ and immediately puts it to vote.
   A. decision
   B. secretary pro tem
   C. Adjourn
   D. main motion

   *Answer:* C) Adjourn. See RONR (12th ed.) §22:20 — “The chair states the question on the motion to Adjourn and immediately puts it to vote.”

---

## Domain 3: Incidental Motions and Motions that Bring a Question Again Before the Assembly

**1.** `[K0053 · Vote required]` What vote does the motion to Point of Order require to be adopted?
   A. Majority vote
   B. Two-thirds vote
   C. No vote (chair rules / demand)
   D. Unanimous consent

   *Answer:* No vote (chair rules / demand). See RONR (12th ed.) §23:1 — “When a member thinks that the rules of the assembly are being violated, he can make a Point of Order (or “raise a question of order,” as it is sometimes expressed), thereby calling upon the chair for a ruling and an enforcement of the regular rules.”

**2.** `[K0054 · Debatability]` Is the motion to Point of Order debatable?
   A. Debatable
   B. Not debatable

   *Answer:* Not debatable. See RONR (12th ed.) §23:1 — “When a member thinks that the rules of the assembly are being violated, he can make a Point of Order (or “raise a question of order,” as it is sometimes expressed), thereby calling upon the chair for a ruling and an enforcement of the regular rules.”

**3.** `[K0055 · Second required]` Does the motion to Point of Order require a second?
   A. Requires a second
   B. No second required

   *Answer:* No second required. See RONR (12th ed.) §23:1 — “When a member thinks that the rules of the assembly are being violated, he can make a Point of Order (or “raise a question of order,” as it is sometimes expressed), thereby calling upon the chair for a ruling and an enforcement of the regular rules.”

**4.** `[K0056 · Amendability]` Can the motion to Point of Order be amended?
   A. Amendable
   B. Not amendable

   *Answer:* Not amendable. See RONR (12th ed.) §23:1 — “When a member thinks that the rules of the assembly are being violated, he can make a Point of Order (or “raise a question of order,” as it is sometimes expressed), thereby calling upon the chair for a ruling and an enforcement of the regular rules.”

**5.** `[K0057 · Vote required]` What vote does the motion to Appeal require to be adopted?
   A. Majority vote
   B. Two-thirds vote
   C. No vote (chair rules / demand)
   D. Unanimous consent

   *Answer:* Majority vote. See RONR (12th ed.) §24:1 — “By electing a presiding officer, the assembly delegates to him the authority and duty to make necessary rulings on questions of parliamentary law.”

**6.** `[K0058 · Debatability]` Is the motion to Appeal debatable?
   A. Debatable
   B. Not debatable

   *Answer:* Debatable. See RONR (12th ed.) §24:1 — “By electing a presiding officer, the assembly delegates to him the authority and duty to make necessary rulings on questions of parliamentary law.”

**7.** `[K0059 · Second required]` Does the motion to Appeal require a second?
   A. Requires a second
   B. No second required

   *Answer:* Requires a second. See RONR (12th ed.) §24:1 — “By electing a presiding officer, the assembly delegates to him the authority and duty to make necessary rulings on questions of parliamentary law.”

**8.** `[K0060 · Amendability]` Can the motion to Appeal be amended?
   A. Amendable
   B. Not amendable

   *Answer:* Not amendable. See RONR (12th ed.) §24:1 — “By electing a presiding officer, the assembly delegates to him the authority and duty to make necessary rulings on questions of parliamentary law.”

**9.** `[K0061 · Vote required]` What vote does the motion to Suspend the Rules require to be adopted?
   A. Majority vote
   B. Two-thirds vote
   C. No vote (chair rules / demand)
   D. Unanimous consent

   *Answer:* Two-thirds vote. See RONR (12th ed.) §25:1 — “When an assembly wishes to do something during a meeting that it cannot do without violating one or more of its regular rules, it can adopt a motion to Suspend the Rules interfering with the proposed action—provided that the proposal is not in conflict with the organization’s bylaws (or…”

**10.** `[K0062 · Debatability]` Is the motion to Suspend the Rules debatable?
   A. Debatable
   B. Not debatable

   *Answer:* Not debatable. See RONR (12th ed.) §25:1 — “When an assembly wishes to do something during a meeting that it cannot do without violating one or more of its regular rules, it can adopt a motion to Suspend the Rules interfering with the proposed action—provided that the proposal is not in conflict with the organization’s bylaws (or…”

**11.** `[K0063 · Second required]` Does the motion to Suspend the Rules require a second?
   A. Requires a second
   B. No second required

   *Answer:* Requires a second. See RONR (12th ed.) §25:1 — “When an assembly wishes to do something during a meeting that it cannot do without violating one or more of its regular rules, it can adopt a motion to Suspend the Rules interfering with the proposed action—provided that the proposal is not in conflict with the organization’s bylaws (or…”

**12.** `[K0064 · Amendability]` Can the motion to Suspend the Rules be amended?
   A. Amendable
   B. Not amendable

   *Answer:* Not amendable. See RONR (12th ed.) §25:1 — “When an assembly wishes to do something during a meeting that it cannot do without violating one or more of its regular rules, it can adopt a motion to Suspend the Rules interfering with the proposed action—provided that the proposal is not in conflict with the organization’s bylaws (or…”

**13.** `[K0065 · Vote required]` What vote does the motion to Object to the Consideration of a Question require to be adopted?
   A. Majority vote
   B. Two-thirds vote
   C. No vote (chair rules / demand)
   D. Unanimous consent

   *Answer:* Two-thirds vote. See RONR (12th ed.) §26:1 — “The purpose of an Objection to the Consideration of a Question is to enable the assembly to avoid a particular original main motion altogether when it believes it would be strongly undesirable for the motion even to come before the assembly.”

**14.** `[K0066 · Debatability]` Is the motion to Object to the Consideration of a Question debatable?
   A. Debatable
   B. Not debatable

   *Answer:* Not debatable. See RONR (12th ed.) §26:1 — “The purpose of an Objection to the Consideration of a Question is to enable the assembly to avoid a particular original main motion altogether when it believes it would be strongly undesirable for the motion even to come before the assembly.”

**15.** `[K0067 · Second required]` Does the motion to Object to the Consideration of a Question require a second?
   A. Requires a second
   B. No second required

   *Answer:* No second required. See RONR (12th ed.) §26:1 — “The purpose of an Objection to the Consideration of a Question is to enable the assembly to avoid a particular original main motion altogether when it believes it would be strongly undesirable for the motion even to come before the assembly.”

**16.** `[K0068 · Amendability]` Can the motion to Object to the Consideration of a Question be amended?
   A. Amendable
   B. Not amendable

   *Answer:* Not amendable. See RONR (12th ed.) §26:1 — “The purpose of an Objection to the Consideration of a Question is to enable the assembly to avoid a particular original main motion altogether when it believes it would be strongly undesirable for the motion even to come before the assembly.”

**17.** `[K0069 · Vote required]` What vote does the motion to Division of the Assembly require to be adopted?
   A. Majority vote
   B. Two-thirds vote
   C. No vote (chair rules / demand)
   D. Unanimous consent

   *Answer:* No vote (chair rules / demand). See RONR (12th ed.) §29:1 — “Whenever a member doubts the result of a voice (vivavoce) vote or a vote by show of hands—either because the result appears close or because he doubts that a representative number of the members present have voted— he can call for a Division of the Assembly, thereby requiring the vote to be taken…”

**18.** `[K0070 · Debatability]` Is the motion to Division of the Assembly debatable?
   A. Debatable
   B. Not debatable

   *Answer:* Not debatable. See RONR (12th ed.) §29:1 — “Whenever a member doubts the result of a voice (vivavoce) vote or a vote by show of hands—either because the result appears close or because he doubts that a representative number of the members present have voted— he can call for a Division of the Assembly, thereby requiring the vote to be taken…”

**19.** `[K0071 · Second required]` Does the motion to Division of the Assembly require a second?
   A. Requires a second
   B. No second required

   *Answer:* No second required. See RONR (12th ed.) §29:1 — “Whenever a member doubts the result of a voice (vivavoce) vote or a vote by show of hands—either because the result appears close or because he doubts that a representative number of the members present have voted— he can call for a Division of the Assembly, thereby requiring the vote to be taken…”

**20.** `[K0072 · Amendability]` Can the motion to Division of the Assembly be amended?
   A. Amendable
   B. Not amendable

   *Answer:* Not amendable. See RONR (12th ed.) §29:1 — “Whenever a member doubts the result of a voice (vivavoce) vote or a vote by show of hands—either because the result appears close or because he doubts that a representative number of the members present have voted— he can call for a Division of the Assembly, thereby requiring the vote to be taken…”

**21.** `[C0577 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: When a member thinks that the rules of the assembly are being violated, he can make a _____ (or “raise a question of order,” as it is sometimes expressed), thereby calling upon the chair for a ruling and an enforcement of the regular rules.

   *Answer:* Point of Order — “When a member thinks that the rules of the assembly are being violated, he can make a Point of Order (or “raise a question of order,” as it is sometimes expressed), thereby calling upon the chair for a ruling and an enforcement of the regular rules.”. See RONR (12th ed.) §23:1 — “When a member thinks that the rules of the assembly are being violated, he can make a Point of Order (or “raise a question of order,” as it is sometimes expressed), thereby calling upon the chair for a ruling and an enforcement of the regular rules.”

**22.** `[C0577 · Applied multiple-choice]` Fill in the blank: When a member thinks that the rules of the assembly are being violated, he can make a _____ (or “raise a question of order,” as it is sometimes expressed), thereby calling upon the chair for a ruling and an enforcement of the regular rules.
   A. previous notice
   B. Point of Order
   C. lost
   D. first paragraph

   *Answer:* B) Point of Order. See RONR (12th ed.) §23:1 — “When a member thinks that the rules of the assembly are being violated, he can make a Point of Order (or “raise a question of order,” as it is sometimes expressed), thereby calling upon the chair for a ruling and an enforcement of the regular rules.”

**23.** `[C0578 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: Grounds for a Point of _____.

   *Answer:* Order — “Grounds for a Point of Order.”. See RONR (12th ed.) §23:3 — “Grounds for a Point of Order.”

**24.** `[C0578 · Applied multiple-choice]` Fill in the blank: Grounds for a Point of _____.
   A. the Time to Which to Adjourn
   B. general
   C. bring business
   D. Order

   *Answer:* D) Order. See RONR (12th ed.) §23:3 — “Grounds for a Point of Order.”

**25.** `[C0578 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: Grounds for a _____ Order.

   *Answer:* Point of — “Grounds for a Point of Order.”. See RONR (12th ed.) §23:3 — “Grounds for a Point of Order.”

**26.** `[C0578 · Applied multiple-choice]` Fill in the blank: Grounds for a _____ Order.
   A. adopts
   B. Resolved
   C. Resolved by the
   D. Point of

   *Answer:* D) Point of. See RONR (12th ed.) §23:3 — “Grounds for a Point of Order.”

**27.** `[C0582 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: If the vote was such that the number of members excluded from participating would not have affected the outcome, a member may wish, in the appropriate circumstances, to move to Rescind or _____ Something Previously Adopted (35), to move to Reconsider (37), or to renew a motion (38), arguing that comments in debate by the excluded members could have led to a different result;

   *Answer:* Amend — “If the vote was such that the number of members excluded from participating would not have affected the outcome, a member may wish, in the appropriate circumstances, to move to Rescind or Amend Something Previously Adopted (35), to move to Reconsider (37), or to renew a motion (38), arguing that comments in debate by the excluded members could have led to a different result;”. See RONR (12th ed.) §23:7 — “If the vote was such that the number of members excluded from participating would not have affected the outcome, a member may wish, in the appropriate circumstances, to move to Rescind or Amend Something Previously Adopted (35), to move to Reconsider (37), or to renew a motion (38), arguing that comments in debate by the excluded members could have led to a different result;”

**28.** `[C0582 · Applied multiple-choice]` Fill in the blank: If the vote was such that the number of members excluded from participating would not have affected the outcome, a member may wish, in the appropriate circumstances, to move to Rescind or _____ Something Previously Adopted (35), to move to Reconsider (37), or to renew a motion (38), arguing that comments in debate by the excluded members could have led to a different result;
   A. charge
   B. exhausted his right to debate
   C. substitute
   D. Amend

   *Answer:* D) Amend. See RONR (12th ed.) §23:7 — “If the vote was such that the number of members excluded from participating would not have affected the outcome, a member may wish, in the appropriate circumstances, to move to Rescind or Amend Something Previously Adopted (35), to move to Reconsider (37), or to renew a motion (38), arguing that comments in debate by the excluded members could have led to a different result;”

**29.** `[C0582 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: If the vote was such that the number of members excluded from participating would not have affected the outcome, a member may wish, in the appropriate circumstances, to move to Rescind or Amend _____ (35), to move to Reconsider (37), or to renew a motion (38), arguing that comments in debate by the excluded members could have led to a different result;

   *Answer:* Something Previously Adopted — “If the vote was such that the number of members excluded from participating would not have affected the outcome, a member may wish, in the appropriate circumstances, to move to Rescind or Amend Something Previously Adopted (35), to move to Reconsider (37), or to renew a motion (38), arguing that comments in debate by the excluded members could have led to a different result;”. See RONR (12th ed.) §23:7 — “If the vote was such that the number of members excluded from participating would not have affected the outcome, a member may wish, in the appropriate circumstances, to move to Rescind or Amend Something Previously Adopted (35), to move to Reconsider (37), or to renew a motion (38), arguing that comments in debate by the excluded members could have led to a different result;”

**30.** `[C0582 · Applied multiple-choice]` Fill in the blank: If the vote was such that the number of members excluded from participating would not have affected the outcome, a member may wish, in the appropriate circumstances, to move to Rescind or Amend _____ (35), to move to Reconsider (37), or to renew a motion (38), arguing that comments in debate by the excluded members could have led to a different result;
   A. corresponding secretary
   B. Pattern of Formality
   C. Something Previously Adopted
   D. after the floor was yielded

   *Answer:* C) Something Previously Adopted. See RONR (12th ed.) §23:7 — “If the vote was such that the number of members excluded from participating would not have affected the outcome, a member may wish, in the appropriate circumstances, to move to Rescind or Amend Something Previously Adopted (35), to move to Reconsider (37), or to renew a motion (38), arguing that comments in debate by the excluded members could have led to a different result;”

**31.** `[C0582 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: If the vote was such that the number of members excluded from participating would not have affected the outcome, a member may wish, in the appropriate circumstances, to move to _____ or Amend Something Previously Adopted (35), to move to Reconsider (37), or to renew a motion (38), arguing that comments in debate by the excluded members could have led to a different result;

   *Answer:* Rescind — “If the vote was such that the number of members excluded from participating would not have affected the outcome, a member may wish, in the appropriate circumstances, to move to Rescind or Amend Something Previously Adopted (35), to move to Reconsider (37), or to renew a motion (38), arguing that comments in debate by the excluded members could have led to a different result;”. See RONR (12th ed.) §23:7 — “If the vote was such that the number of members excluded from participating would not have affected the outcome, a member may wish, in the appropriate circumstances, to move to Rescind or Amend Something Previously Adopted (35), to move to Reconsider (37), or to renew a motion (38), arguing that comments in debate by the excluded members could have led to a different result;”

**32.** `[C0585 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: When similar issues arise in the future, such precedents are _____ in resolving them—that is, they carry weight in the absence of overriding reasons for following a different course—but they are not binding on the chair or the assembly.

   *Answer:* persuasive — “When similar issues arise in the future, such precedents are persuasive in resolving them—that is, they carry weight in the absence of overriding reasons for following a different course—but they are not binding on the chair or the assembly.”. See RONR (12th ed.) §23:10 — “When similar issues arise in the future, such precedents are persuasive in resolving them—that is, they carry weight in the absence of overriding reasons for following a different course—but they are not binding on the chair or the assembly.”

**33.** `[C0585 · Applied multiple-choice]` Fill in the blank: When similar issues arise in the future, such precedents are _____ in resolving them—that is, they carry weight in the absence of overriding reasons for following a different course—but they are not binding on the chair or the assembly.
   A. making
   B. viva voce
   C. persuasive
   D. who should be engaged well in

   *Answer:* C) persuasive. See RONR (12th ed.) §23:10 — “When similar issues arise in the future, such precedents are persuasive in resolving them—that is, they carry weight in the absence of overriding reasons for following a different course—but they are not binding on the chair or the assembly.”

**34.** `[C0595 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: Thus, if a point is made that the chair is admitting a motion which is not in order, the question should be put so that an affirmative result of the vote will mean that the motion is in order—as in the example above, or as follows: “… Those of the opinion that the motion is in order, say _____.… ;

   *Answer:* aye — “Thus, if a point is made that the chair is admitting a motion which is not in order, the question should be put so that an affirmative result of the vote will mean that the motion is in order—as in the example above, or as follows: “… Those of the opinion that the motion is in order, say aye.… ;”. See RONR (12th ed.) §23:21 — “Thus, if a point is made that the chair is admitting a motion which is not in order, the question should be put so that an affirmative result of the vote will mean that the motion is in order—as in the example above, or as follows: “… Those of the opinion that the motion is in order, say aye.… ;”

**35.** `[C0595 · Applied multiple-choice]` Fill in the blank: Thus, if a point is made that the chair is admitting a motion which is not in order, the question should be put so that an affirmative result of the vote will mean that the motion is in order—as in the example above, or as follows: “… Those of the opinion that the motion is in order, say _____.… ;
   A. accept
   B. one fifth of
   C. Postpone to a Certain Time
   D. aye

   *Answer:* D) aye. See RONR (12th ed.) §23:21 — “Thus, if a point is made that the chair is admitting a motion which is not in order, the question should be put so that an affirmative result of the vote will mean that the motion is in order—as in the example above, or as follows: “… Those of the opinion that the motion is in order, say aye.… ;”

**36.** `[C0595 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: Thus, if a point is made that the chair is admitting a motion which is not in order, the question should be put so that an affirmative result of the vote will mean that the motion is in order—as in the example above, or as follows: “… Those of the opinion that the motion is in order, say _____… ;

   *Answer:* aye. — “Thus, if a point is made that the chair is admitting a motion which is not in order, the question should be put so that an affirmative result of the vote will mean that the motion is in order—as in the example above, or as follows: “… Those of the opinion that the motion is in order, say aye.… ;”. See RONR (12th ed.) §23:21 — “Thus, if a point is made that the chair is admitting a motion which is not in order, the question should be put so that an affirmative result of the vote will mean that the motion is in order—as in the example above, or as follows: “… Those of the opinion that the motion is in order, say aye.… ;”

**37.** `[C0595 · Applied multiple-choice]` Fill in the blank: Thus, if a point is made that the chair is admitting a motion which is not in order, the question should be put so that an affirmative result of the vote will mean that the motion is in order—as in the example above, or as follows: “… Those of the opinion that the motion is in order, say _____… ;
   A. main
   B. aye.
   C. Annul
   D. standing

   *Answer:* B) aye.. See RONR (12th ed.) §23:21 — “Thus, if a point is made that the chair is admitting a motion which is not in order, the question should be put so that an affirmative result of the vote will mean that the motion is in order—as in the example above, or as follows: “… Those of the opinion that the motion is in order, say aye.… ;”

**38.** `[C0595 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: Thus, if a point is made that the chair is admitting a motion which is not in order, the question should be put so that an affirmative result of the vote will mean that the motion is in order—as in the example above, or as follows: “… Those of the opinion that the motion is in order, say _____.… ;

   *Answer:* aye — “Thus, if a point is made that the chair is admitting a motion which is not in order, the question should be put so that an affirmative result of the vote will mean that the motion is in order—as in the example above, or as follows: “… Those of the opinion that the motion is in order, say aye.… ;”. See RONR (12th ed.) §23:21 — “Thus, if a point is made that the chair is admitting a motion which is not in order, the question should be put so that an affirmative result of the vote will mean that the motion is in order—as in the example above, or as follows: “… Those of the opinion that the motion is in order, say aye.… ;”

**39.** `[C0596 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: But any two members have the right to _____ from his decision on such a question.

   *Answer:* Appeal — “But any two members have the right to Appeal from his decision on such a question.”. See RONR (12th ed.) §24:1 — “But any two members have the right to Appeal from his decision on such a question.”

**40.** `[C0596 · Applied multiple-choice]` Fill in the blank: But any two members have the right to _____ from his decision on such a question.
   A. Appeal
   B. Robert’s Rules
   C. request
   D. Division of the Assembly

   *Answer:* A) Appeal. See RONR (12th ed.) §24:1 — “But any two members have the right to Appeal from his decision on such a question.”

**41.** `[C0599 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: As explained in Standard Characteristic 2, an appeal is applicable only to a _____ by the chair.

   *Answer:* ruling — “As explained in Standard Characteristic 2, an appeal is applicable only to a ruling by the chair.”. See RONR (12th ed.) §24:5 — “As explained in Standard Characteristic 2, an appeal is applicable only to a ruling by the chair.”

**42.** `[C0599 · Applied multiple-choice]` Fill in the blank: As explained in Standard Characteristic 2, an appeal is applicable only to a _____ by the chair.
   A. incidental motions
   B. in the bylaws
   C. Fix
   D. ruling

   *Answer:* D) ruling. See RONR (12th ed.) §24:5 — “As explained in Standard Characteristic 2, an appeal is applicable only to a ruling by the chair.”

**43.** `[C0600 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: No appeal can be made from the chair’s response to a parliamentary inquiry or other query, since such a reply is an _____ rendered by the chair, not a ruling on a question that has actually arisen.

   *Answer:* opinion — “No appeal can be made from the chair’s response to a parliamentary inquiry or other query, since such a reply is an opinion rendered by the chair, not a ruling on a question that has actually arisen.”. See RONR (12th ed.) §24:6 — “No appeal can be made from the chair’s response to a parliamentary inquiry or other query, since such a reply is an opinion rendered by the chair, not a ruling on a question that has actually arisen.”

**44.** `[C0600 · Applied multiple-choice]` Fill in the blank: No appeal can be made from the chair’s response to a parliamentary inquiry or other query, since such a reply is an _____ rendered by the chair, not a ruling on a question that has actually arisen.
   A. extending
   B. Descriptive Characteristics
   C. opinion
   D. secondary amendment by inserting

   *Answer:* C) opinion. See RONR (12th ed.) §24:6 — “No appeal can be made from the chair’s response to a parliamentary inquiry or other query, since such a reply is an opinion rendered by the chair, not a ruling on a question that has actually arisen.”

**45.** `[C0600 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: But the point can be put at issue before the assembly by making the motion despite the chair’s opinion and, when he _____ that the motion is not in order, appealing from the chair’s decision.

   *Answer:* rules — “But the point can be put at issue before the assembly by making the motion despite the chair’s opinion and, when he rules that the motion is not in order, appealing from the chair’s decision.”. See RONR (12th ed.) §24:6 — “But the point can be put at issue before the assembly by making the motion despite the chair’s opinion and, when he rules that the motion is not in order, appealing from the chair’s decision.”

**46.** `[C0600 · Applied multiple-choice]` Fill in the blank: But the point can be put at issue before the assembly by making the motion despite the chair’s opinion and, when he _____ that the motion is not in order, appealing from the chair’s decision.
   A. that are pending
   B. Reconsider and Enter on the Minutes
   C. rules
   D. in the brief interval

   *Answer:* C) rules. See RONR (12th ed.) §24:6 — “But the point can be put at issue before the assembly by making the motion despite the chair’s opinion and, when he rules that the motion is not in order, appealing from the chair’s decision.”

**47.** `[C0601 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: If a member doubts the correctness of such an announced result, however, he should call for a _____ (see 29) or move that the vote be counted.

   *Answer:* Division — “If a member doubts the correctness of such an announced result, however, he should call for a Division (see 29) or move that the vote be counted.”. See RONR (12th ed.) §24:7 — “If a member doubts the correctness of such an announced result, however, he should call for a Division (see 29) or move that the vote be counted.”

**48.** `[C0601 · Applied multiple-choice]` Fill in the blank: If a member doubts the correctness of such an announced result, however, he should call for a _____ (see 29) or move that the vote be counted.
   A. house of delegates,
   B. Division
   C. recognized
   D. Insert, or Add

   *Answer:* B) Division. See RONR (12th ed.) §24:7 — “If a member doubts the correctness of such an announced result, however, he should call for a Division (see 29) or move that the vote be counted.”

**49.** `[C0605 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: The question is not on “sustaining the chair,” because the _____, not the presiding officer, is in question.

   *Answer:* decision — “The question is not on “sustaining the chair,” because the decision, not the presiding officer, is in question.”. See RONR (12th ed.) §24:11 — “The question is not on “sustaining the chair,” because the decision, not the presiding officer, is in question.”

**50.** `[C0605 · Applied multiple-choice]` Fill in the blank: The question is not on “sustaining the chair,” because the _____, not the presiding officer, is in question.
   A. decision
   B. Order of Precedence of Motions
   C. whether to order
   D. that are pending

   *Answer:* A) decision. See RONR (12th ed.) §24:11 — “The question is not on “sustaining the chair,” because the decision, not the presiding officer, is in question.”

**51.** `[C0608 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: When an assembly wishes to do something during a meeting that it cannot do without violating one or more of its regular rules, it can adopt a motion to _____ interfering with the proposed action—provided that the proposal is not in conflict with the organization’s bylaws (or constitution), with local, state, or national law prescribing procedural rules applicable to the organization or assembly, or with a fundamental principle of parliamentary law.

   *Answer:* Suspend the Rules — “When an assembly wishes to do something during a meeting that it cannot do without violating one or more of its regular rules, it can adopt a motion to Suspend the Rules interfering with the proposed action—provided that the proposal is not in conflict with the organization’s bylaws (or constitution), with local, state, or national law prescribing procedural rules applicable to the organization or assembly, or with a fundamental principle of parliamentary law.”. See RONR (12th ed.) §25:1 — “When an assembly wishes to do something during a meeting that it cannot do without violating one or more of its regular rules, it can adopt a motion to Suspend the Rules interfering with the proposed action—provided that the proposal is not in conflict with the organization’s bylaws (or constitution), with local, state, or national law prescribing procedural rules applicable to the organization or assembly, or with a fundamental principle of parliamentary law.”

**52.** `[C0608 · Applied multiple-choice]` Fill in the blank: When an assembly wishes to do something during a meeting that it cannot do without violating one or more of its regular rules, it can adopt a motion to _____ interfering with the proposed action—provided that the proposal is not in conflict with the organization’s bylaws (or constitution), with local, state, or national law prescribing procedural rules applicable to the organization or assembly, or with a fundamental principle of parliamentary law.
   A. Suspend the Rules
   B. ceases to be
   C. Debate,
   D. of the House

   *Answer:* A) Suspend the Rules. See RONR (12th ed.) §25:1 — “When an assembly wishes to do something during a meeting that it cannot do without violating one or more of its regular rules, it can adopt a motion to Suspend the Rules interfering with the proposed action—provided that the proposal is not in conflict with the organization’s bylaws (or constitution), with local, state, or national law prescribing procedural rules applicable to the organization or assembly, or with a fundamental principle of parliamentary law.”

**53.** `[C0609 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: A motion to “take up a question out of its proper order,” or to consider one before a time to which it has been postponed, is an application of the motion to _____ (see 14, 41).

   *Answer:* Suspend the Rules — “A motion to “take up a question out of its proper order,” or to consider one before a time to which it has been postponed, is an application of the motion to Suspend the Rules (see 14, 41).”. See RONR (12th ed.) §25:3 — “A motion to “take up a question out of its proper order,” or to consider one before a time to which it has been postponed, is an application of the motion to Suspend the Rules (see 14, 41).”

**54.** `[C0609 · Applied multiple-choice]` Fill in the blank: A motion to “take up a question out of its proper order,” or to consider one before a time to which it has been postponed, is an application of the motion to _____ (see 14, 41).
   A. Suspend the Rules
   B. previous notice
   C. in the brief interval
   D. of Order Newly Revised

   *Answer:* A) Suspend the Rules. See RONR (12th ed.) §25:3 — “A motion to “take up a question out of its proper order,” or to consider one before a time to which it has been postponed, is an application of the motion to Suspend the Rules (see 14, 41).”

**55.** `[C0610 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: In making the incidental motion to _____, the particular rule or rules to be suspended are not mentioned;

   *Answer:* Suspend the Rules — “In making the incidental motion to Suspend the Rules, the particular rule or rules to be suspended are not mentioned;”. See RONR (12th ed.) §25:4 — “In making the incidental motion to Suspend the Rules, the particular rule or rules to be suspended are not mentioned;”

**56.** `[C0610 · Applied multiple-choice]` Fill in the blank: In making the incidental motion to _____, the particular rule or rules to be suspended are not mentioned;
   A. Suspend the Rules
   B. the Rules
   C. Main Motions,
   D. one fifth of

   *Answer:* A) Suspend the Rules. See RONR (12th ed.) §25:4 — “In making the incidental motion to Suspend the Rules, the particular rule or rules to be suspended are not mentioned;”

**57.** `[C0610 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: In making the incidental motion to _____ Rules, the particular rule or rules to be suspended are not mentioned;

   *Answer:* Suspend the — “In making the incidental motion to Suspend the Rules, the particular rule or rules to be suspended are not mentioned;”. See RONR (12th ed.) §25:4 — “In making the incidental motion to Suspend the Rules, the particular rule or rules to be suspended are not mentioned;”

**58.** `[C0610 · Applied multiple-choice]` Fill in the blank: In making the incidental motion to _____ Rules, the particular rule or rules to be suspended are not mentioned;
   A. Commit
   B. exhausted
   C. Suspend the
   D. order of the day,

   *Answer:* C) Suspend the. See RONR (12th ed.) §25:4 — “In making the incidental motion to Suspend the Rules, the particular rule or rules to be suspended are not mentioned;”

**59.** `[C0610 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: In making the incidental motion to Suspend the _____, the particular rule or rules to be suspended are not mentioned;

   *Answer:* Rules — “In making the incidental motion to Suspend the Rules, the particular rule or rules to be suspended are not mentioned;”. See RONR (12th ed.) §25:4 — “In making the incidental motion to Suspend the Rules, the particular rule or rules to be suspended are not mentioned;”

**60.** `[C0611 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: If a motion to _____ is adopted and its object is to allow consideration of business that could not otherwise have been considered at the time, the chair immediately recognizes the member who moved the suspension of the rules, to make the appropriate motion that will bring up the desired business.

   *Answer:* Suspend the Rules — “If a motion to Suspend the Rules is adopted and its object is to allow consideration of business that could not otherwise have been considered at the time, the chair immediately recognizes the member who moved the suspension of the rules, to make the appropriate motion that will bring up the desired business.”. See RONR (12th ed.) §25:5 — “If a motion to Suspend the Rules is adopted and its object is to allow consideration of business that could not otherwise have been considered at the time, the chair immediately recognizes the member who moved the suspension of the rules, to make the appropriate motion that will bring up the desired business.”

**61.** `[C0611 · Applied multiple-choice]` Fill in the blank: If a motion to _____ is adopted and its object is to allow consideration of business that could not otherwise have been considered at the time, the chair immediately recognizes the member who moved the suspension of the rules, to make the appropriate motion that will bring up the desired business.
   A. Suspend the Rules
   B. Making a Motion
   C. Something Previously Adopted
   D. no more than a quarterly time interval

   *Answer:* A) Suspend the Rules. See RONR (12th ed.) §25:5 — “If a motion to Suspend the Rules is adopted and its object is to allow consideration of business that could not otherwise have been considered at the time, the chair immediately recognizes the member who moved the suspension of the rules, to make the appropriate motion that will bring up the desired business.”

**62.** `[C0614 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: No applicable procedural rule prescribed by federal, state, _____ can be suspended unless the rule specifically provides for its own suspension.

   *Answer:* or local law — “No applicable procedural rule prescribed by federal, state, or local law can be suspended unless the rule specifically provides for its own suspension.”. See RONR (12th ed.) §25:8 — “No applicable procedural rule prescribed by federal, state, or local law can be suspended unless the rule specifically provides for its own suspension.”

**63.** `[C0614 · Applied multiple-choice]` Fill in the blank: No applicable procedural rule prescribed by federal, state, _____ can be suspended unless the rule specifically provides for its own suspension.
   A. Consider Seriatim
   B. or local law
   C. Indefinitely
   D. calling the meeting to order

   *Answer:* B) or local law. See RONR (12th ed.) §25:8 — “No applicable procedural rule prescribed by federal, state, or local law can be suspended unless the rule specifically provides for its own suspension.”

**64.** `[C0616 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: Rules _____ cannot be suspended, even by unanimous consent or an actual unanimous vote, because the absentees do not consent to such suspension.

   *Answer:* protecting absentees — “Rules protecting absentees cannot be suspended, even by unanimous consent or an actual unanimous vote, because the absentees do not consent to such suspension.”. See RONR (12th ed.) §25:10 — “Rules protecting absentees cannot be suspended, even by unanimous consent or an actual unanimous vote, because the absentees do not consent to such suspension.”

**65.** `[C0616 · Applied multiple-choice]` Fill in the blank: Rules _____ cannot be suspended, even by unanimous consent or an actual unanimous vote, because the absentees do not consent to such suspension.
   A. applied
   B. entitled to vote
   C. Adopted
   D. protecting absentees

   *Answer:* D) protecting absentees. See RONR (12th ed.) §25:10 — “Rules protecting absentees cannot be suspended, even by unanimous consent or an actual unanimous vote, because the absentees do not consent to such suspension.”

**66.** `[C0617 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: Thus, while generally applicable limits on debate and the making of motions may be imposed by motions such as the _____, the rules may not be suspended so as to deny any particular member the right to attend meetings, make motions or nominations, speak in debate, give previous notice, or vote.

   *Answer:* Previous Question — “Thus, while generally applicable limits on debate and the making of motions may be imposed by motions such as the Previous Question, the rules may not be suspended so as to deny any particular member the right to attend meetings, make motions or nominations, speak in debate, give previous notice, or vote.”. See RONR (12th ed.) §25:11 — “Thus, while generally applicable limits on debate and the making of motions may be imposed by motions such as the Previous Question, the rules may not be suspended so as to deny any particular member the right to attend meetings, make motions or nominations, speak in debate, give previous notice, or vote.”

**67.** `[C0617 · Applied multiple-choice]` Fill in the blank: Thus, while generally applicable limits on debate and the making of motions may be imposed by motions such as the _____, the rules may not be suspended so as to deny any particular member the right to attend meetings, make motions or nominations, speak in debate, give previous notice, or vote.
   A. Previous Question
   B. society,
   C. aye
   D. recording secretary

   *Answer:* A) Previous Question. See RONR (12th ed.) §25:11 — “Thus, while generally applicable limits on debate and the making of motions may be imposed by motions such as the Previous Question, the rules may not be suspended so as to deny any particular member the right to attend meetings, make motions or nominations, speak in debate, give previous notice, or vote.”

**68.** `[C0617 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: Rules protecting a _____ cannot be suspended.

   *Answer:* basic right of the individual member — “Rules protecting a basic right of the individual member cannot be suspended.”. See RONR (12th ed.) §25:11 — “Rules protecting a basic right of the individual member cannot be suspended.”

**69.** `[C0617 · Applied multiple-choice]` Fill in the blank: Rules protecting a _____ cannot be suspended.
   A. chairman pro tem
   B. basic right of the individual member
   C. constitution and bylaws
   D. Taking

   *Answer:* B) basic right of the individual member. See RONR (12th ed.) §25:11 — “Rules protecting a basic right of the individual member cannot be suspended.”

**70.** `[C0618 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: At a regular meeting of an organization that has an established order of business, the assembly cannot, even unanimously, vote to _____ that order of business (in the sense of voting, in advance of the time when it adjourns, that the order of business shall not be gone through at all at that meeting).

   *Answer:* dispense with — “At a regular meeting of an organization that has an established order of business, the assembly cannot, even unanimously, vote to dispense with that order of business (in the sense of voting, in advance of the time when it adjourns, that the order of business shall not be gone through at all at that meeting).”. See RONR (12th ed.) §25:12 — “At a regular meeting of an organization that has an established order of business, the assembly cannot, even unanimously, vote to dispense with that order of business (in the sense of voting, in advance of the time when it adjourns, that the order of business shall not be gone through at all at that meeting).”

**71.** `[C0618 · Applied multiple-choice]` Fill in the blank: At a regular meeting of an organization that has an established order of business, the assembly cannot, even unanimously, vote to _____ that order of business (in the sense of voting, in advance of the time when it adjourns, that the order of business shall not be gone through at all at that meeting).
   A. in good standing
   B. dispense with
   C. Debatability of Motions,
   D. the

   *Answer:* B) dispense with. See RONR (12th ed.) §25:12 — “At a regular meeting of an organization that has an established order of business, the assembly cannot, even unanimously, vote to dispense with that order of business (in the sense of voting, in advance of the time when it adjourns, that the order of business shall not be gone through at all at that meeting).”

**72.** `[C0620 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: The _____ of a society, as contained in the manual established by the bylaws as the parliamentary authority, or as included in any special rules of order adopted by the organization (see 2), are rules of parliamentary procedure, the suspension of which requires a two-thirds vote.

   *Answer:* rules of order — “The rules of order of a society, as contained in the manual established by the bylaws as the parliamentary authority, or as included in any special rules of order adopted by the organization (see 2), are rules of parliamentary procedure, the suspension of which requires a two-thirds vote.”. See RONR (12th ed.) §25:14 — “The rules of order of a society, as contained in the manual established by the bylaws as the parliamentary authority, or as included in any special rules of order adopted by the organization (see 2), are rules of parliamentary procedure, the suspension of which requires a two-thirds vote.”

**73.** `[C0620 · Applied multiple-choice]` Fill in the blank: The _____ of a society, as contained in the manual established by the bylaws as the parliamentary authority, or as included in any special rules of order adopted by the organization (see 2), are rules of parliamentary procedure, the suspension of which requires a two-thirds vote.
   A. aye.
   B. rules of order
   C. withdraw
   D. Division of the Assembly

   *Answer:* B) rules of order. See RONR (12th ed.) §25:14 — “The rules of order of a society, as contained in the manual established by the bylaws as the parliamentary authority, or as included in any special rules of order adopted by the organization (see 2), are rules of parliamentary procedure, the suspension of which requires a two-thirds vote.”

**74.** `[C0625 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: The purpose of an Objection to the Consideration of a _____ is to enable the assembly to avoid a particular original main motion altogether when it believes it would be strongly undesirable for the motion even to come before the assembly.

   *Answer:* Question — “The purpose of an Objection to the Consideration of a Question is to enable the assembly to avoid a particular original main motion altogether when it believes it would be strongly undesirable for the motion even to come before the assembly.”. See RONR (12th ed.) §26:1 — “The purpose of an Objection to the Consideration of a Question is to enable the assembly to avoid a particular original main motion altogether when it believes it would be strongly undesirable for the motion even to come before the assembly.”

**75.** `[C0625 · Applied multiple-choice]` Fill in the blank: The purpose of an Objection to the Consideration of a _____ is to enable the assembly to avoid a particular original main motion altogether when it believes it would be strongly undesirable for the motion even to come before the assembly.
   A. aye,

   C. Question

   *Answer:* C) Question. See RONR (12th ed.) §26:1 — “The purpose of an Objection to the Consideration of a Question is to enable the assembly to avoid a particular original main motion altogether when it believes it would be strongly undesirable for the motion even to come before the assembly.”

   D. Point

   *Answer:* D) Point. See RONR (12th ed.) §26:3 — “Resemblance to Point of Order.”

**80.** `[C0626 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: An _____ Consideration of a Question is similar in some ways to a Point of Order.

   *Answer:* Objection to the — “An Objection to the Consideration of a Question is similar in some ways to a Point of Order.”. See RONR (12th ed.) §26:3 — “An Objection to the Consideration of a Question is similar in some ways to a Point of Order.”

**81.** `[C0626 · Applied multiple-choice]` Fill in the blank: An _____ Consideration of a Question is similar in some ways to a Point of Order.
   A. Repeal
   B. unique
   C. extending limits
   D. Objection to the

   *Answer:* D) Objection to the. See RONR (12th ed.) §26:3 — “An Objection to the Consideration of a Question is similar in some ways to a Point of Order.”

**82.** `[C0626 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: An Objection to the _____ is similar in some ways to a Point of Order.

**83.** `[C0629 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: As noted in Standard Characteristic 8, a vote _____ an objection to consideration can be reconsidered.

   *Answer:* sustaining — “As noted in Standard Characteristic 8, a vote sustaining an objection to consideration can be reconsidered.”. See RONR (12th ed.) §26:6 — “As noted in Standard Characteristic 8, a vote sustaining an objection to consideration can be reconsidered.”

**84.** `[C0629 · Applied multiple-choice]` Fill in the blank: As noted in Standard Characteristic 8, a vote _____ an objection to consideration can be reconsidered.

   *Answer:* A) sustaining. See RONR (12th ed.) §26:6 — “As noted in Standard Characteristic 8, a vote sustaining an objection to consideration can be reconsidered.”

**86.** `[C0629 · Applied multiple-choice]` Fill in the blank: _____ation of a Vote That Has Sustained the Objection.

**87.** `[C0630 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: Therefore, those who wish to _____ of the question vote in the negative.

   *Answer:* prevent consideration — “Therefore, those who wish to prevent consideration of the question vote in the negative.”. See RONR (12th ed.) §26:7 — “Therefore, those who wish to prevent consideration of the question vote in the negative.”

**88.** `[C0630 · Applied multiple-choice]` Fill in the blank: Therefore, those who wish to _____ of the question vote in the negative.

**89.** `[C0630 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: When the objection is put to a vote in its correct form (see _____, below), members are asked to vote for or against consideration of the question objected to (not for or against sustaining the objection).

See RONR (12th ed.) §26:7 — “When the objection is put to a vote in its correct form (see Form and Example, below), members are asked to vote for or against consideration of the question objected to (not for or against sustaining the objection).”

**90.** `[C0630 · Applied multiple-choice]` Fill in the blank: When the objection is put to a vote in its correct form (see _____, below), members are asked to vote for or against consideration of the question objected to (not for or against sustaining the objection).
   A. privileged motions
   B. bylaws
   C. are
   D. Form and Example

**91.** `[C0630 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: Therefore, those who wish to prevent consideration of the question _____.

   *Answer:* vote in the negative — “Therefore, those who wish to prevent consideration of the question vote in the negative.”. See RONR (12th ed.) §26:7 — “Therefore, those who wish to prevent consideration of the question vote in the negative.”

**92.** `[C0632 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: In putting the objection to vote, the chair must be careful _____ to say, “Shall the objection be sustained?” This would reverse the effect of affirmative and negative votes and might cause confusion.

   *Answer:* not — “In putting the objection to vote, the chair must be careful not to say, “Shall the objection be sustained?” This would reverse the effect of affirmative and negative votes and might cause confusion.”. See RONR (12th ed.) §26:9 — “In putting the objection to vote, the chair must be careful not to say, “Shall the objection be sustained?” This would reverse the effect of affirmative and negative votes and might cause confusion.”

**93.** `[C0632 · Applied multiple-choice]` Fill in the blank: In putting the objection to vote, the chair must be careful _____ to say, “Shall the objection be sustained?” This would reverse the effect of affirmative and negative votes and might cause confusion.
   A. committee reports
   B. not
   C. division
   D. the board

   *Answer:* B) not. See RONR (12th ed.) §26:9 — “In putting the objection to vote, the chair must be careful not to say, “Shall the objection be sustained?” This would reverse the effect of affirmative and negative votes and might cause confusion.”

**94.** `[C0633 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: When a motion relating to a single subject contains several parts, each of which is capable of standing as a complete proposition if the others are removed, the parts can be separated to be considered and voted on as if they were distinct questions—by adoption of the motion for Division of a _____ (or “to divide the question”).

   *Answer:* Question — “When a motion relating to a single subject contains several parts, each of which is capable of standing as a complete proposition if the others are removed, the parts can be separated to be considered and voted on as if they were distinct questions—by adoption of the motion for Division of a Question (or “to divide the question”).”. See RONR (12th ed.) §27:1 — “When a motion relating to a single subject contains several parts, each of which is capable of standing as a complete proposition if the others are removed, the parts can be separated to be considered and voted on as if they were distinct questions—by adoption of the motion for Division of a Question (or “to divide the question”).”

**95.** `[C0633 · Applied multiple-choice]` Fill in the blank: When a motion relating to a single subject contains several parts, each of which is capable of standing as a complete proposition if the others are removed, the parts can be separated to be considered and voted on as if they were distinct questions—by adoption of the motion for Division of a _____ (or “to divide the question”).
   A. Parliamentary Inquiry
   B. Suspend the Rules
   C. assuming
   D. Question

   *Answer:* D) Question. See RONR (12th ed.) §27:1 — “When a motion relating to a single subject contains several parts, each of which is capable of standing as a complete proposition if the others are removed, the parts can be separated to be considered and voted on as if they were distinct questions—by adoption of the motion for Division of a Question (or “to divide the question”).”

   *Answer:* Division of a — “When a motion relating to a single subject contains several parts, each of which is capable of standing as a complete proposition if the others are removed, the parts can be separated to be considered and voted on as if they were distinct questions—by adoption of the motion for Division of a Question (or “to divide the question”).”. **102.** `[C0637 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: If possible, the division should be carried out by no more than a renumbering of phrases or clauses, prefacing each part with the formal word(s), “That,” “Resolved, That,” or “_____, That,” dropping conjunctions where necessary, or replacing pronouns with the nouns for which they stand, with or without the definite article “the,” as required.

   *Answer:* Ordered — “If possible, the division should be carried out by no more than a renumbering of phrases or clauses, prefacing each part with the formal word(s), “That,” “Resolved, That,” or “Ordered, That,” dropping conjunctions where necessary, or replacing pronouns with the nouns for which they stand, with or without the definite article “the,” as required.”. See RONR (12th ed.) §27:6 — “If possible, the division should be carried out by no more than a renumbering of phrases or clauses, prefacing each part with the formal word(s), “That,” “Resolved, That,” or “Ordered, That,” dropping conjunctions where necessary, or replacing pronouns with the nouns for which they stand, with or without the definite article “the,” as required.”

**103.** `[C0637 · Applied multiple-choice]` Fill in the blank: If possible, the division should be carried out by no more than a renumbering of phrases or clauses, prefacing each part with the formal word(s), “That,” “Resolved, That,” or “_____, That,” dropping conjunctions where necessary, or replacing pronouns with the nouns for which they stand, with or without the definite article “the,” as required.
   A. of Order
   B. Executive Committee
   C. Ordered
   D. incidental

   *Answer:* C) Ordered. See RONR (12th ed.) §27:6 — “If possible, the division should be carried out by no more than a renumbering of phrases or clauses, prefacing each part with the formal word(s), “That,” “Resolved, That,” or “Ordered, That,” dropping conjunctions where necessary, or replacing pronouns with the nouns for which they stand, with or without the definite article “the,” as required.”

**104.** `[C0637 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: If possible, the division should be carried out by no more than a renumbering of phrases or clauses, prefacing each part with the formal word(s), “That,” “_____, That,” or “Ordered, That,” dropping conjunctions where necessary, or replacing pronouns with the nouns for which they stand, with or without the definite article “the,” as required.

   *Answer:* Resolved — “If possible, the division should be carried out by no more than a renumbering of phrases or clauses, prefacing each part with the formal word(s), “That,” “Resolved, That,” or “Ordered, That,” dropping conjunctions where necessary, or replacing pronouns with the nouns for which they stand, with or without the definite article “the,” as required.”. See RONR (12th ed.) §27:6 — “If possible, the division should be carried out by no more than a renumbering of phrases or clauses, prefacing each part with the formal word(s), “That,” “Resolved, That,” or “Ordered, That,” dropping conjunctions where necessary, or replacing pronouns with the nouns for which they stand, with or without the definite article “the,” as required.”

   A. standing rule of
   B. Resolved

   D. entitled to vote

**106.** `[C0638 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: For example, suppose that the following resolution is pending: “Resolved, That the Society congratulate its member Ernest Dunn on his novel _____, and that three copies be purchased for the Society’s library.” Suppose also that a member wishes to divide the question so as to consider the purchase of the books separately.

   *Answer:* Crestwood — “For example, suppose that the following resolution is pending: “Resolved, That the Society congratulate its member Ernest Dunn on his novel Crestwood, and that three copies be purchased for the Society’s library.” Suppose also that a member wishes to divide the question so as to consider the purchase of the books separately.”. See RONR (12th ed.) §27:7 — “For example, suppose that the following resolution is pending: “Resolved, That the Society congratulate its member Ernest Dunn on his novel Crestwood, and that three copies be purchased for the Society’s library.” Suppose also that a member wishes to divide the question so as to consider the purchase of the books separately.”

**107.** `[C0638 · Applied multiple-choice]` Fill in the blank: For example, suppose that the following resolution is pending: “Resolved, That the Society congratulate its member Ernest Dunn on his novel _____, and that three copies be purchased for the Society’s library.” Suppose also that a member wishes to divide the question so as to consider the purchase of the books separately.
   A. within the
   B. amendment to the amendment
   C. in the office of president
   D. Crestwood

   *Answer:* D) Crestwood. See RONR (12th ed.) §27:7 — “For example, suppose that the following resolution is pending: “Resolved, That the Society congratulate its member Ernest Dunn on his novel Crestwood, and that three copies be purchased for the Society’s library.” Suppose also that a member wishes to divide the question so as to consider the purchase of the books separately.”

   *Answer:* Resolved — “For example, suppose that the following resolution is pending: “Resolved, That the Society congratulate its member Ernest Dunn on his novel Crestwood, and that three copies be purchased for the Society’s library.” Suppose also that a member wishes to divide the question so as to consider the purchase of the books separately.”. See RONR (12th ed.) §27:7 — “For example, suppose that the following resolution is pending: “Resolved, That the Society congratulate its member Ernest Dunn on his novel Crestwood, and that three copies be purchased for the Society’s library.” Suppose also that a member wishes to divide the question so as to consider the purchase of the books separately.”

**109.** `[C0638 · Applied multiple-choice]` Fill in the blank: For example, suppose that the following resolution is pending: “_____, That the Society congratulate its member Ernest Dunn on his novel Crestwood, and that three copies be purchased for the Society’s library.” Suppose also that a member wishes to divide the question so as to consider the purchase of the books separately.
   A. not commend
