   A. five
   B. Reconsider
   C. viva voce
   D. first paragraph

   *Answer:* B) Reconsider. See RONR (12th ed.) §37:16 — “Although any member can call up the motion to Reconsider as just described, usually no one but the mover of the reconsideration calls it up on the day the motion is made—at least in cases where the session is to last beyond that day and there is no need for immediate action.”

**280.** `[C0752 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: If it has been moved to adjourn under these circumstances, the motion to _____ can be withdrawn or voted down—or the time can be fixed for an adjourned meeting, which can be done either before or after the vote on adjournment has been taken (see Fix the Time to Which to Adjourn, 22).

   *Answer:* Adjourn — “If it has been moved to adjourn under these circumstances, the motion to Adjourn can be withdrawn or voted down—or the time can be fixed for an adjourned meeting, which can be done either before or after the vote on adjournment has been taken (see Fix the Time to Which to Adjourn, 22).”. See RONR (12th ed.) §37:17 — “If it has been moved to adjourn under these circumstances, the motion to Adjourn can be withdrawn or voted down—or the time can be fixed for an adjourned meeting, which can be done either before or after the vote on adjournment has been taken (see Fix the Time to Which to Adjourn, 22).”

   B. president-elect
   C. Adjourn
   D. all

   *Answer:* C) Adjourn. See RONR (12th ed.) §37:17 — “If it has been moved to adjourn under these circumstances, the motion to Adjourn can be withdrawn or voted down—or the time can be fixed for an adjourned meeting, which can be done either before or after the vote on adjournment has been taken (see Fix the Time to Which to Adjourn, 22).”

**297.** `[C0757 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: If a vote on one of a series of motions is taken under an order limiting debate or for the _____, and then is reconsidered before such an order is exhausted (as explained in the sections on those motions, 15 and 16), the same restrictions continue to apply to debate both on the motion to Reconsider and on the reconsideration.

   *Answer:* Previous Question — “If a vote on one of a series of motions is taken under an order limiting debate or for the Previous Question, and then is reconsidered before such an order is exhausted (as explained in the sections on those motions, 15 and 16), the same restrictions continue to apply to debate both on the motion to Reconsider and on the reconsideration.”. See RONR (12th ed.) §37:23 — “If a vote on one of a series of motions is taken under an order limiting debate or for the Previous Question, and then is reconsidered before such an order is exhausted (as explained in the sections on those motions, 15 and 16), the same restrictions continue to apply to debate both on the motion to Reconsider and on the reconsideration.”

   *Answer:* B) Previous Question. **304.** `[C0760 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: If the main question is now pending alone, or if no other questions are pending except motions to Postpone Indefinitely or to Amend (which rank below the motion to _____), the chair at once states the question on the motion to Reconsider as immediately pending.

   *Answer:* Commit — “If the main question is now pending alone, or if no other questions are pending except motions to Postpone Indefinitely or to Amend (which rank below the motion to Commit), the chair at once states the question on the motion to Reconsider as immediately pending.”. See RONR (12th ed.) §37:26 — “If the main question is now pending alone, or if no other questions are pending except motions to Postpone Indefinitely or to Amend (which rank below the motion to Commit), the chair at once states the question on the motion to Reconsider as immediately pending.”

   *Answer:* A) Commit. **314.** `[C0763 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: When it is moved to reconsider a debatable subsidiary or incidental motion which relates to a pending main question or a series of pending questions (in which case the motion to _____ is debatable, as noted in Standard Characteristic 5), debate on the motion to Reconsider can go into the merits of the motion proposed to be reconsidered, but not into the merits of any other pending question.

   *Answer:* Reconsider — “When it is moved to reconsider a debatable subsidiary or incidental motion which relates to a pending main question or a series of pending questions (in which case the motion to Reconsider is debatable, as noted in Standard Characteristic 5), debate on the motion to Reconsider can go into the merits of the motion proposed to be reconsidered, but not into the merits of any other pending question.”. See RONR (12th ed.) §37:29 — “When it is moved to reconsider a debatable subsidiary or incidental motion which relates to a pending main question or a series of pending questions (in which case the motion to Reconsider is debatable, as noted in Standard Characteristic 5), debate on the motion to Reconsider can go into the merits of the motion proposed to be reconsidered, but not into the merits of any other pending question.”

**315.** `[C0763 · Applied multiple-choice]` Fill in the blank: When it is moved to reconsider a debatable subsidiary or incidental motion which relates to a pending main question or a series of pending questions (in which case the motion to _____ is debatable, as noted in Standard Characteristic 5), debate on the motion to Reconsider can go into the merits of the motion proposed to be reconsidered, but not into the merits of any other pending question.
   A. incidental main motion
   B. Form and Example
   C. made while no question is pending
   D. Reconsider

   *Answer:* D) Reconsider. See RONR (12th ed.) §37:29 — “When it is moved to reconsider a debatable subsidiary or incidental motion which relates to a pending main question or a series of pending questions (in which case the motion to Reconsider is debatable, as noted in Standard Characteristic 5), debate on the motion to Reconsider can go into the merits of the motion proposed to be reconsidered, but not into the merits of any other pending question.”

   *Answer:* A) Reconsider and Enter on. See RONR (12th ed.) §37:49 — “If the motion to Reconsider and Enter on the Minutes is seconded, all action required by the vote proposed to be reconsidered is suspended, and there is time to notify absent members of the proposed action.”

**367.** `[C0782 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: At this next meeting, provided that such notice has been given, the motion to _____ can then be adopted by a majority vote.

   *Answer:* Rescind — “At this next meeting, provided that such notice has been given, the motion to Rescind can then be adopted by a majority vote.”. See RONR (12th ed.) §37:50 — “At this next meeting, provided that such notice has been given, the motion to Rescind can then be adopted by a majority vote.”

**368.** `[C0782 · Applied multiple-choice]` Fill in the blank: At this next meeting, provided that such notice has been given, the motion to _____ can then be adopted by a majority vote.
   A. recognized
   B. approve
   C. Rescind
   D. Up Business out of Its Proper Order

   *Answer:* C) Rescind. See RONR (12th ed.) §37:50 — “At this next meeting, provided that such notice has been given, the motion to Rescind can then be adopted by a majority vote.”

**369.** `[C0783 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: The motion to _____ may occasionally be subject to attempted abuse, particularly in ordinary societies with single-meeting sessions, since it gives any two members power to hold up action taken by a meeting.

   *Answer:* Reconsider and Enter on the Minutes — “The motion to Reconsider and Enter on the Minutes may occasionally be subject to attempted abuse, particularly in ordinary societies with single-meeting sessions, since it gives any two members power to hold up action taken by a meeting.”. See RONR (12th ed.) §37:51 — “The motion to Reconsider and Enter on the Minutes may occasionally be subject to attempted abuse, particularly in ordinary societies with single-meeting sessions, since it gives any two members power to hold up action taken by a meeting.”

**370.** `[C0783 · Applied multiple-choice]` Fill in the blank: The motion to _____ may occasionally be subject to attempted abuse, particularly in ordinary societies with single-meeting sessions, since it gives any two members power to hold up action taken by a meeting.
   A. Strike Out
   B. at a meeting
   C. Reconsider and Enter on the Minutes
   D. receive

   *Answer:* C) Reconsider and Enter on the Minutes. See RONR (12th ed.) §37:51 — “The motion to Reconsider and Enter on the Minutes may occasionally be subject to attempted abuse, particularly in ordinary societies with single-meeting sessions, since it gives any two members power to hold up action taken by a meeting.”

**371.** `[C0784 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: In such a case, the mere making of a motion to set an adjourned meeting would likely cause withdrawal of the motion to _____, since its object would be defeated.

   *Answer:* Reconsider and Enter on the Minutes — “In such a case, the mere making of a motion to set an adjourned meeting would likely cause withdrawal of the motion to Reconsider and Enter on the Minutes, since its object would be defeated.”. See RONR (12th ed.) §37:52 — “In such a case, the mere making of a motion to set an adjourned meeting would likely cause withdrawal of the motion to Reconsider and Enter on the Minutes, since its object would be defeated.”

**372.** `[C0784 · Applied multiple-choice]` Fill in the blank: In such a case, the mere making of a motion to set an adjourned meeting would likely cause withdrawal of the motion to _____, since its object would be defeated.
   A. Reconsider and Enter on the Minutes
   B. Order of Business
   C. does
   D. Debate,

   *Answer:* A) Reconsider and Enter on the Minutes. See RONR (12th ed.) §37:52 — “In such a case, the mere making of a motion to set an adjourned meeting would likely cause withdrawal of the motion to Reconsider and Enter on the Minutes, since its object would be defeated.”

## Domain 4: Organization and Conduct of Meetings

**1.** `[C0001 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: A _____—the kind of gathering to which parliamentary law is generally understood to apply—has the following distinguishing characteristics:

   *Answer:* deliberative assembly — “A deliberative assembly—the kind of gathering to which parliamentary law is generally understood to apply—has the following distinguishing characteristics:”. See RONR (12th ed.) §1:1 — “A deliberative assembly—the kind of gathering to which parliamentary law is generally understood to apply—has the following distinguishing characteristics:”

**2.** `[C0001 · Applied multiple-choice]` Fill in the blank: A _____—the kind of gathering to which parliamentary law is generally understood to apply—has the following distinguishing characteristics:
   A. deliberative assembly
   B. Reconsider,
   C. from the primary amendment
   D. privileged motions

   *Answer:* A) deliberative assembly. See RONR (12th ed.) §1:1 — “A deliberative assembly—the kind of gathering to which parliamentary law is generally understood to apply—has the following distinguishing characteristics:”

**3.** `[C0003 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: The distinction should be noted between the assembly (that is, the _____ who assemble) and the meeting (which is the event of their being assembled to transact business).

   *Answer:* body of people — “The distinction should be noted between the assembly (that is, the body of people who assemble) and the meeting (which is the event of their being assembled to transact business).”. See RONR (12th ed.) §1:3 — “The distinction should be noted between the assembly (that is, the body of people who assemble) and the meeting (which is the event of their being assembled to transact business).”

**4.** `[C0003 · Applied multiple-choice]` Fill in the blank: The distinction should be noted between the assembly (that is, the _____ who assemble) and the meeting (which is the event of their being assembled to transact business).
   A. body of people
   B. special meeting
   C. vote
   D. general orders

   *Answer:* A) body of people. See RONR (12th ed.) §1:3 — “The distinction should be noted between the assembly (that is, the body of people who assemble) and the meeting (which is the event of their being assembled to transact business).”

**5.** `[C0003 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: The term meeting is also distinguished from _____, according to definitions stated in 8.

   *Answer:* session — “The term meeting is also distinguished from session, according to definitions stated in 8.”. See RONR (12th ed.) §1:3 — “The term meeting is also distinguished from session, according to definitions stated in 8.”

**6.** `[C0003 · Applied multiple-choice]` Fill in the blank: The term meeting is also distinguished from _____, according to definitions stated in 8.
   A. To Read Papers
   B. session
   C. by article or section
   D. rising

   *Answer:* B) session. See RONR (12th ed.) §1:3 — “The term meeting is also distinguished from session, according to definitions stated in 8.”

**7.** `[C0003 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: The distinction should be noted between the assembly (that is, the body of people who assemble) and the meeting (which is the _____ of their being assembled to transact business).

   *Answer:* event — “The distinction should be noted between the assembly (that is, the body of people who assemble) and the meeting (which is the event of their being assembled to transact business).”. See RONR (12th ed.) §1:3 — “The distinction should be noted between the assembly (that is, the body of people who assemble) and the meeting (which is the event of their being assembled to transact business).”

**8.** `[C0004 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: A _____ of an assembly, in the parliamentary sense, as mentioned above, is a person entitled to full participation in its proceedings, that is, as explained in 3 and 4, the right to attend meetings, to make motions, to speak in debate, and to vote.

   *Answer:* member — “A member of an assembly, in the parliamentary sense, as mentioned above, is a person entitled to full participation in its proceedings, that is, as explained in 3 and 4, the right to attend meetings, to make motions, to speak in debate, and to vote.”. See RONR (12th ed.) §1:4 — “A member of an assembly, in the parliamentary sense, as mentioned above, is a person entitled to full participation in its proceedings, that is, as explained in 3 and 4, the right to attend meetings, to make motions, to speak in debate, and to vote.”

See RONR (12th ed.) §1:4 — “A member of an assembly, in the parliamentary sense, as mentioned above, is a person entitled to full participation in its proceedings, that is, as explained in 3 and 4, the right to attend meetings, to make motions, to speak in debate, and to vote.”

   *Answer:* vote — “A member of an assembly, in the parliamentary sense, as mentioned above, is a person entitled to full participation in its proceedings, that is, as explained in 3 and 4, the right to attend meetings, to make motions, to speak in debate, and to vote.”. See RONR (12th ed.) §1:4 — “A member of an assembly, in the parliamentary sense, as mentioned above, is a person entitled to full participation in its proceedings, that is, as explained in 3 and 4, the right to attend meetings, to make motions, to speak in debate, and to vote.”

**13.** `[C0005 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: A deliberative assembly that has not adopted any rules is commonly understood to hold itself bound by the rules and customs of the general _____—or common parliamentary law (as discussed in the Introduction)—to the extent that there is agreement in the meeting body as to what these rules and practices are.

   *Answer:* parliamentary law — “A deliberative assembly that has not adopted any rules is commonly understood to hold itself bound by the rules and customs of the general parliamentary law—or common parliamentary law (as discussed in the Introduction)—to the extent that there is agreement in the meeting body as to what these rules and practices are.”. See RONR (12th ed.) §1:5 — “A deliberative assembly that has not adopted any rules is commonly understood to hold itself bound by the rules and customs of the general parliamentary law—or common parliamentary law (as discussed in the Introduction)—to the extent that there is agreement in the meeting body as to what these rules and practices are.”

**14.** `[C0005 · Applied multiple-choice]` Fill in the blank: A deliberative assembly that has not adopted any rules is commonly understood to hold itself bound by the rules and customs of the general _____—or common parliamentary law (as discussed in the Introduction)—to the extent that there is agreement in the meeting body as to what these rules and practices are.
   A. immediately
   B. carried
   C. parliamentary law
   D. Fix

   *Answer:* C) parliamentary law. See RONR (12th ed.) §1:5 — “A deliberative assembly that has not adopted any rules is commonly understood to hold itself bound by the rules and customs of the general parliamentary law—or common parliamentary law (as discussed in the Introduction)—to the extent that there is agreement in the meeting body as to what these rules and practices are.”

   *Answer:* common — “A deliberative assembly that has not adopted any rules is commonly understood to hold itself bound by the rules and customs of the general parliamentary law—or common parliamentary law (as discussed in the Introduction)—to the extent that there is agreement in the meeting body as to what these rules and practices are.”. See RONR (12th ed.) §1:5 — “A deliberative assembly that has not adopted any rules is commonly understood to hold itself bound by the rules and customs of the general parliamentary law—or common parliamentary law (as discussed in the Introduction)—to the extent that there is agreement in the meeting body as to what these rules and practices are.”

   *Answer:* majority vote — “The basic principle of decision in a deliberative assembly is that, to become the act or choice of the body, a proposition must be adopted by a majority vote;”. See RONR (12th ed.) §1:6 — “The basic principle of decision in a deliberative assembly is that, to become the act or choice of the body, a proposition must be adopted by a majority vote;”

**19.** `[C0006 · Applied multiple-choice]` Fill in the blank: The basic principle of decision in a deliberative assembly is that, to become the act or choice of the body, a proposition must be adopted by a _____;

   C. class
   D. general assembly, house of delegates,

   *Answer:* A) majority vote. See RONR (12th ed.) §1:6 — “The basic principle of decision in a deliberative assembly is that, to become the act or choice of the body, a proposition must be adopted by a majority vote;”

**42.** `[C0027 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: The Corporate Charter (in different states variously called the _____, Articles of Incorporation, Articles of Association, etc.) is a legal instrument that sets forth the name and object of the society and whatever other information is needed for incorporating the society under the laws of the particular state—or under federal law in the case of a few special types of organizations.

   *Answer:* Certificate of Incorporation — “The Corporate Charter (in different states variously called the Certificate of Incorporation, Articles of Incorporation, Articles of Association, etc.) is a legal instrument that sets forth the name and object of the society and whatever other information is needed for incorporating the society under the laws of the particular state—or under federal law in the case of a few special types of organizations.”. See RONR (12th ed.) §2:5 — “The Corporate Charter (in different states variously called the Certificate of Incorporation, Articles of Incorporation, Articles of Association, etc.) is a legal instrument that sets forth the name and object of the society and whatever other information is needed for incorporating the society under the laws of the particular state—or under federal law in the case of a few special types of organizations.”

   A. stand at ease
   B. Certificate of Incorporation

   D. strike out and insert

   *Answer:* Articles of Incorporation — “The Corporate Charter (in different states variously called the Certificate of Incorporation, Articles of Incorporation, Articles of Association, etc.) is a legal instrument that sets forth the name and object of the society and whatever other information is needed for incorporating the society under the laws of the particular state—or under federal law in the case of a few special types of organizations.”. See RONR (12th ed.) §2:5 — “The Corporate Charter (in different states variously called the Certificate of Incorporation, Articles of Incorporation, Articles of Association, etc.) is a legal instrument that sets forth the name and object of the society and whatever other information is needed for incorporating the society under the laws of the particular state—or under federal law in the case of a few special types of organizations.”

**45.** `[C0027 · Applied multiple-choice]` Fill in the blank: The Corporate Charter (in different states variously called the Certificate of Incorporation, _____, Articles of Association, etc.) is a legal instrument that sets forth the name and object of the society and whatever other information is needed for incorporating the society under the laws of the particular state—or under federal law in the case of a few special types of organizations.
   A. Articles of Incorporation
   B. extending limits
   C. Extend Limits of Debate
   D. Parliamentary Inquiry

   *Answer:* A) Articles of Incorporation. See RONR (12th ed.) §2:5 — “The Corporate Charter (in different states variously called the Certificate of Incorporation, Articles of Incorporation, Articles of Association, etc.) is a legal instrument that sets forth the name and object of the society and whatever other information is needed for incorporating the society under the laws of the particular state—or under federal law in the case of a few special types of organizations.”

**46.** `[C0027 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: The Corporate Charter (in different states variously called the Certificate of Incorporation, Articles of Incorporation, _____, etc.) is a legal instrument that sets forth the name and object of the society and whatever other information is needed for incorporating the society under the laws of the particular state—or under federal law in the case of a few special types of organizations.

   *Answer:* Articles of Association — “The Corporate Charter (in different states variously called the Certificate of Incorporation, Articles of Incorporation, Articles of Association, etc.) is a legal instrument that sets forth the name and object of the society and whatever other information is needed for incorporating the society under the laws of the particular state—or under federal law in the case of a few special types of organizations.”. See RONR (12th ed.) §2:5 — “The Corporate Charter (in different states variously called the Certificate of Incorporation, Articles of Incorporation, Articles of Association, etc.) is a legal instrument that sets forth the name and object of the society and whatever other information is needed for incorporating the society under the laws of the particular state—or under federal law in the case of a few special types of organizations.”

**47.** `[C0030 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: In general, the constitution or the _____—or both—of a society are the documents that contain its own basic rules relating principally to itself as an organization, rather than to the parliamentary procedure that it follows.

   *Answer:* bylaws — “In general, the constitution or the bylaws—or both—of a society are the documents that contain its own basic rules relating principally to itself as an organization, rather than to the parliamentary procedure that it follows.”. See RONR (12th ed.) §2:8 — “In general, the constitution or the bylaws—or both—of a society are the documents that contain its own basic rules relating principally to itself as an organization, rather than to the parliamentary procedure that it follows.”

**48.** `[C0030 · Applied multiple-choice]` Fill in the blank: In general, the constitution or the _____—or both—of a society are the documents that contain its own basic rules relating principally to itself as an organization, rather than to the parliamentary procedure that it follows.
   A. Program
   B. event
   C. assuming
   D. bylaws

   *Answer:* D) bylaws. See RONR (12th ed.) §2:8 — “In general, the constitution or the bylaws—or both—of a society are the documents that contain its own basic rules relating principally to itself as an organization, rather than to the parliamentary procedure that it follows.”

**49.** `[C0032 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: It formerly was common practice to divide the basic rules of an organization into two documents, in order that one of them —the constitution—might be made more difficult to amend than the other, to which the name _____ was applied.

   *Answer:* bylaws — “It formerly was common practice to divide the basic rules of an organization into two documents, in order that one of them —the constitution—might be made more difficult to amend than the other, to which the name bylaws was applied.”. See RONR (12th ed.) §2:10 — “It formerly was common practice to divide the basic rules of an organization into two documents, in order that one of them —the constitution—might be made more difficult to amend than the other, to which the name bylaws was applied.”

**51.** `[C0032 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: It formerly was common practice to divide the basic rules of an organization into two documents, in order that one of them —the _____—might be made more difficult to amend than the other, to which the name bylaws was applied.

   *Answer:* constitution — “It formerly was common practice to divide the basic rules of an organization into two documents, in order that one of them —the constitution—might be made more difficult to amend than the other, to which the name bylaws was applied.”. **53.** `[C0036 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: The term _____ refers to written rules of parliamentary procedure formally adopted by an assembly or an organization.

   *Answer:* rules of order — “The term rules of order refers to written rules of parliamentary procedure formally adopted by an assembly or an organization.”. See RONR (12th ed.) §2:14 — “The term rules of order refers to written rules of parliamentary procedure formally adopted by an assembly or an organization.”

**54.** `[C0036 · Applied multiple-choice]` Fill in the blank: The term _____ refers to written rules of parliamentary procedure formally adopted by an assembly or an organization.
   A. of the board
   B. in the brief interval
   C. rules of order
   D. select,

   *Answer:* C) rules of order. See RONR (12th ed.) §2:14 — “The term rules of order refers to written rules of parliamentary procedure formally adopted by an assembly or an organization.”

**55.** `[C0037 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: The usual and preferable method by which an ordinary society now provides itself with suitable rules of order is therefore to place in its bylaws a provision prescribing that the current edition of a specified and generally accepted manual of parliamentary law shall be the organization’s parliamentary authority, and then to adopt only such _____ rules of order as it finds needed to supplement or modify rules contained in that manual.

   *Answer:* special — “The usual and preferable method by which an ordinary society now provides itself with suitable rules of order is therefore to place in its bylaws a provision prescribing that the current edition of a specified and generally accepted manual of parliamentary law shall be the organization’s parliamentary authority, and then to adopt only such special rules of order as it finds needed to supplement or modify rules contained in that manual.”. See RONR (12th ed.) §2:15 — “The usual and preferable method by which an ordinary society now provides itself with suitable rules of order is therefore to place in its bylaws a provision prescribing that the current edition of a specified and generally accepted manual of parliamentary law shall be the organization’s parliamentary authority, and then to adopt only such special rules of order as it finds needed to supplement or modify rules contained in that manual.”

**56.** `[C0037 · Applied multiple-choice]` Fill in the blank: The usual and preferable method by which an ordinary society now provides itself with suitable rules of order is therefore to place in its bylaws a provision prescribing that the current edition of a specified and generally accepted manual of parliamentary law shall be the organization’s parliamentary authority, and then to adopt only such _____ rules of order as it finds needed to supplement or modify rules contained in that manual.
   A. suspects
   B. special
   C. exact
   D. Point of Order

   *Answer:* B) special. See RONR (12th ed.) §2:15 — “The usual and preferable method by which an ordinary society now provides itself with suitable rules of order is therefore to place in its bylaws a provision prescribing that the current edition of a specified and generally accepted manual of parliamentary law shall be the organization’s parliamentary authority, and then to adopt only such special rules of order as it finds needed to supplement or modify rules contained in that manual.”

**57.** `[C0037 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: In contrast to bylaws, _____ derive their proper substance largely from the general nature of the parliamentary process rather than from the circumstances of a particular assembly.

   *Answer:* rules of order — “In contrast to bylaws, rules of order derive their proper substance largely from the general nature of the parliamentary process rather than from the circumstances of a particular assembly.”. See RONR (12th ed.) §2:15 — “In contrast to bylaws, rules of order derive their proper substance largely from the general nature of the parliamentary process rather than from the circumstances of a particular assembly.”

**58.** `[C0037 · Applied multiple-choice]` Fill in the blank: In contrast to bylaws, _____ derive their proper substance largely from the general nature of the parliamentary process rather than from the circumstances of a particular assembly.
   A. prevent consideration
   B. same
   C. Division
   D. rules of order

   *Answer:* D) rules of order. See RONR (12th ed.) §2:15 — “In contrast to bylaws, rules of order derive their proper substance largely from the general nature of the parliamentary process rather than from the circumstances of a particular assembly.”

**59.** `[C0037 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: The usual and preferable method by which an ordinary society now provides itself with suitable rules of order is therefore to place in its bylaws a provision prescribing that the current edition of a specified and generally accepted manual of parliamentary law shall be the organization’s _____, and then to adopt only such special rules of order as it finds needed to supplement or modify rules contained in that manual.

   *Answer:* parliamentary authority — “The usual and preferable method by which an ordinary society now provides itself with suitable rules of order is therefore to place in its bylaws a provision prescribing that the current edition of a specified and generally accepted manual of parliamentary law shall be the organization’s parliamentary authority, and then to adopt only such special rules of order as it finds needed to supplement or modify rules contained in that manual.”. See RONR (12th ed.) §2:15 — “The usual and preferable method by which an ordinary society now provides itself with suitable rules of order is therefore to place in its bylaws a provision prescribing that the current edition of a specified and generally accepted manual of parliamentary law shall be the organization’s parliamentary authority, and then to adopt only such special rules of order as it finds needed to supplement or modify rules contained in that manual.”

**60.** `[C0040 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: In matters on which an organization’s adopted parliamentary authority is silent, provisions found in other works on parliamentary law may be _____—that is, they may carry weight in the absence of overriding reasons for following a different course—but they are not binding on the body.

   *Answer:* persuasive — “In matters on which an organization’s adopted parliamentary authority is silent, provisions found in other works on parliamentary law may be persuasive—that is, they may carry weight in the absence of overriding reasons for following a different course—but they are not binding on the body.”. See RONR (12th ed.) §2:18 — “In matters on which an organization’s adopted parliamentary authority is silent, provisions found in other works on parliamentary law may be persuasive—that is, they may carry weight in the absence of overriding reasons for following a different course—but they are not binding on the body.”

**61.** `[C0040 · Applied multiple-choice]` Fill in the blank: In matters on which an organization’s adopted parliamentary authority is silent, provisions found in other works on parliamentary law may be _____—that is, they may carry weight in the absence of overriding reasons for following a different course—but they are not binding on the body.
   A. finally, disposed of
   B. persuasive
   C. Order
   D. Unfinished Business and General Orders

   *Answer:* B) persuasive. See RONR (12th ed.) §2:18 — “In matters on which an organization’s adopted parliamentary authority is silent, provisions found in other works on parliamentary law may be persuasive—that is, they may carry weight in the absence of overriding reasons for following a different course—but they are not binding on the body.”

**62.** `[C0045 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: As with special rules of order, it is advisable for standing rules to be printed under a separate heading in the booklet containing the bylaws, and in such a case, any enacting words such as “_____, That” are normally dropped.

   *Answer:* Resolved — “As with special rules of order, it is advisable for standing rules to be printed under a separate heading in the booklet containing the bylaws, and in such a case, any enacting words such as “Resolved, That” are normally dropped.”. See RONR (12th ed.) §2:23 — “As with special rules of order, it is advisable for standing rules to be printed under a separate heading in the booklet containing the bylaws, and in such a case, any enacting words such as “Resolved, That” are normally dropped.”

**63.** `[C0045 · Applied multiple-choice]` Fill in the blank: As with special rules of order, it is advisable for standing rules to be printed under a separate heading in the booklet containing the bylaws, and in such a case, any enacting words such as “_____, That” are normally dropped.
   A. Resolved
   B. dispense with
   C. undebatable
   D. adjournment

   *Answer:* A) Resolved. See RONR (12th ed.) §2:23 — “As with special rules of order, it is advisable for standing rules to be printed under a separate heading in the booklet containing the bylaws, and in such a case, any enacting words such as “Resolved, That” are normally dropped.”

**64.** `[C0045 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: _____, as understood in this book except in the case of conventions, are rules (1) which are related to the details of the administration of a society rather than to parliamentary procedure, and (2) which can be adopted or changed upon the same conditions as any ordinary act of the society.

   *Answer:* Standing rules — “Standing rules, as understood in this book except in the case of conventions, are rules (1) which are related to the details of the administration of a society rather than to parliamentary procedure, and (2) which can be adopted or changed upon the same conditions as any ordinary act of the society.”. See RONR (12th ed.) §2:23 — “Standing rules, as understood in this book except in the case of conventions, are rules (1) which are related to the details of the administration of a society rather than to parliamentary procedure, and (2) which can be adopted or changed upon the same conditions as any ordinary act of the society.”

**65.** `[C0045 · Applied multiple-choice]` Fill in the blank: _____, as understood in this book except in the case of conventions, are rules (1) which are related to the details of the administration of a society rather than to parliamentary procedure, and (2) which can be adopted or changed upon the same conditions as any ordinary act of the society.
   A. Disposition of
   B. Request for
   C. motions
   D. Standing rules

   *Answer:* D) Standing rules. See RONR (12th ed.) §2:23 — “Standing rules, as understood in this book except in the case of conventions, are rules (1) which are related to the details of the administration of a society rather than to parliamentary procedure, and (2) which can be adopted or changed upon the same conditions as any ordinary act of the society.”

**66.** `[C0047 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: However, if a customary practice is or becomes in conflict with the parliamentary authority or any written rule, and a _____ (23) citing the conflict is raised at any time, the custom falls to the ground, and the conflicting provision in the parliamentary authority or written rule must thereafter be complied with.

   *Answer:* Point of Order — “However, if a customary practice is or becomes in conflict with the parliamentary authority or any written rule, and a Point of Order (23) citing the conflict is raised at any time, the custom falls to the ground, and the conflicting provision in the parliamentary authority or written rule must thereafter be complied with.”. See RONR (12th ed.) §2:25 — “However, if a customary practice is or becomes in conflict with the parliamentary authority or any written rule, and a Point of Order (23) citing the conflict is raised at any time, the custom falls to the ground, and the conflicting provision in the parliamentary authority or written rule must thereafter be complied with.”

**67.** `[C0047 · Applied multiple-choice]` Fill in the blank: However, if a customary practice is or becomes in conflict with the parliamentary authority or any written rule, and a _____ (23) citing the conflict is raised at any time, the custom falls to the ground, and the conflicting provision in the parliamentary authority or written rule must thereafter be complied with.
   A. substitute
   B. previous notice,
   C. Point of Order
   D. Call of the House,

   *Answer:* C) Point of Order. See RONR (12th ed.) §2:25 — “However, if a customary practice is or becomes in conflict with the parliamentary authority or any written rule, and a Point of Order (23) citing the conflict is raised at any time, the custom falls to the ground, and the conflicting provision in the parliamentary authority or written rule must thereafter be complied with.”

**68.** `[C0050 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: The minimum number of members who must be present at the meetings of a deliberative assembly for business to be validly transacted is the _____ of the assembly.

   *Answer:* quorum — “The minimum number of members who must be present at the meetings of a deliberative assembly for business to be validly transacted is the quorum of the assembly.”. See RONR (12th ed.) §3:3 — “The minimum number of members who must be present at the meetings of a deliberative assembly for business to be validly transacted is the quorum of the assembly.”

**69.** `[C0050 · Applied multiple-choice]` Fill in the blank: The minimum number of members who must be present at the meetings of a deliberative assembly for business to be validly transacted is the _____ of the assembly.
   A. stated meeting
   B. and Example
   C. not
   D. quorum

   *Answer:* D) quorum. See RONR (12th ed.) §3:3 — “The minimum number of members who must be present at the meetings of a deliberative assembly for business to be validly transacted is the quorum of the assembly.”

**70.** `[C0052 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: The minimum essential officers for the conduct of business in a deliberative assembly are a presiding officer, who conducts the meeting and sees that the rules are observed, and a _____, or clerk, who makes a written record of what is done—usually called “the minutes.” If the officers are members of the assembly—as they usually are in ordinary societies—they are counted in determining whether a quorum is present.

   *Answer:* secretary — “The minimum essential officers for the conduct of business in a deliberative assembly are a presiding officer, who conducts the meeting and sees that the rules are observed, and a secretary, or clerk, who makes a written record of what is done—usually called “the minutes.” If the officers are members of the assembly—as they usually are in ordinary societies—they are counted in determining whether a quorum is present.”. See RONR (12th ed.) §3:6 — “The minimum essential officers for the conduct of business in a deliberative assembly are a presiding officer, who conducts the meeting and sees that the rules are observed, and a secretary, or clerk, who makes a written record of what is done—usually called “the minutes.” If the officers are members of the assembly—as they usually are in ordinary societies—they are counted in determining whether a quorum is present.”

**71.** `[C0052 · Applied multiple-choice]` Fill in the blank: The minimum essential officers for the conduct of business in a deliberative assembly are a presiding officer, who conducts the meeting and sees that the rules are observed, and a _____, or clerk, who makes a written record of what is done—usually called “the minutes.” If the officers are members of the assembly—as they usually are in ordinary societies—they are counted in determining whether a quorum is present.
   A. the one
   B. Main Motions,
   C. extend the limits

   *Answer:* D) secretary. **75.** `[C0057 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: Even in a small meeting, the presiding officer of an _____ is not addressed or referred to by name.

   *Answer:* assembly — “Even in a small meeting, the presiding officer of an assembly is not addressed or referred to by name.”. See RONR (12th ed.) §3:11 — “Even in a small meeting, the presiding officer of an assembly is not addressed or referred to by name.”

**77.** `[C0061 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: When the time of a meeting has arrived, the presiding officer opens it, after he has determined that a quorum is present, by _____.

See RONR (12th ed.) §3:15 — “When the time of a meeting has arrived, the presiding officer opens it, after he has determined that a quorum is present, by calling the meeting to order.”

**78.** `[C0061 · Applied multiple-choice]` Fill in the blank: When the time of a meeting has arrived, the presiding officer opens it, after he has determined that a quorum is present, by _____.
   A. Regular
   B. involve
   C. calling the meeting to order
   D. Standing committees

   *Answer:* C) calling the meeting to order. See RONR (12th ed.) §3:15 — “When the time of a meeting has arrived, the presiding officer opens it, after he has determined that a quorum is present, by calling the meeting to order.”

**79.** `[C0062 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: Meetings of permanently organized bodies usually follow an established _____ that specifies the sequence in which certain general types or classes of business are to be brought up or permitted to be introduced.

See RONR (12th ed.) §3:16 — “Meetings of permanently organized bodies usually follow an established order of business that specifies the sequence in which certain general types or classes of business are to be brought up or permitted to be introduced.”

**80.** `[C0062 · Applied multiple-choice]` Fill in the blank: Meetings of permanently organized bodies usually follow an established _____ that specifies the sequence in which certain general types or classes of business are to be brought up or permitted to be introduced.
   A. order of business

   C. floor
   D. within the control of the

   *Answer:* A) order of business. See RONR (12th ed.) §3:16 — “Meetings of permanently organized bodies usually follow an established order of business that specifies the sequence in which certain general types or classes of business are to be brought up or permitted to be introduced.”
