   *Answer:* informal consideration, — “The committee of the whole and its two alternate forms, the quasi committee of the whole (or consideration as if in committee of the whole) and informal consideration, are devices that enable the full assembly to give detailed consideration to a matter under conditions of freedom somewhat like those of a committee.”. See RONR (12th ed.) §52:1 — “The committee of the whole and its two alternate forms, the quasi committee of the whole (or consideration as if in committee of the whole) and informal consideration, are devices that enable the full assembly to give detailed consideration to a matter under conditions of freedom somewhat like those of a committee.”

**78.** `[C1285 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: Amendments to a resolution _____ are in order, however;

   *Answer:* originating in the committee — “Amendments to a resolution originating in the committee are in order, however;”. See RONR (12th ed.) §52:8 — “Amendments to a resolution originating in the committee are in order, however;”

**79.** `[C1285 · Applied multiple-choice]` Fill in the blank: Amendments to a resolution _____ are in order, however;
   A. constitution
   B. originating in the committee
   C. not
   D. primary amendment

   *Answer:* B) originating in the committee. See RONR (12th ed.) §52:8 — “Amendments to a resolution originating in the committee are in order, however;”

**80.** `[C1297 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: Thus, for example, if a motion to refer the main question to an ordinary committee is made in quasi committee of the whole, such a motion to _____ would be equivalent to the following series of motions if the matter were being considered in a real committee of the whole: (1) that the committee of the whole rise;

   *Answer:* Commit — “Thus, for example, if a motion to refer the main question to an ordinary committee is made in quasi committee of the whole, such a motion to Commit would be equivalent to the following series of motions if the matter were being considered in a real committee of the whole: (1) that the committee of the whole rise;”. See RONR (12th ed.) §52:22 — “Thus, for example, if a motion to refer the main question to an ordinary committee is made in quasi committee of the whole, such a motion to Commit would be equivalent to the following series of motions if the matter were being considered in a real committee of the whole: (1) that the committee of the whole rise;”

**81.** `[C1297 · Applied multiple-choice]` Fill in the blank: Thus, for example, if a motion to refer the main question to an ordinary committee is made in quasi committee of the whole, such a motion to _____ would be equivalent to the following series of motions if the matter were being considered in a real committee of the whole: (1) that the committee of the whole rise;
   A. Commit
   B. charge
   C. corresponding secretary
   D. unfinished business,

   *Answer:* A) Commit. See RONR (12th ed.) §52:22 — “Thus, for example, if a motion to refer the main question to an ordinary committee is made in quasi committee of the whole, such a motion to Commit would be equivalent to the following series of motions if the matter were being considered in a real committee of the whole: (1) that the committee of the whole rise;”

**82.** `[C1303 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: The more traditional aids to the crystallization of opinion in societies have been, simply, to take a _____ or to refer the matter to a committee—often a large committee composed of members representing differing views in the society, such as a committee of the whole or one of its alternate forms.

   *Answer:* Recess — “The more traditional aids to the crystallization of opinion in societies have been, simply, to take a Recess or to refer the matter to a committee—often a large committee composed of members representing differing views in the society, such as a committee of the whole or one of its alternate forms.”. See RONR (12th ed.) §52:28 — “The more traditional aids to the crystallization of opinion in societies have been, simply, to take a Recess or to refer the matter to a committee—often a large committee composed of members representing differing views in the society, such as a committee of the whole or one of its alternate forms.”

**83.** `[C1303 · Applied multiple-choice]` Fill in the blank: The more traditional aids to the crystallization of opinion in societies have been, simply, to take a _____ or to refer the matter to a committee—often a large committee composed of members representing differing views in the society, such as a committee of the whole or one of its alternate forms.
   A. from office
   B. yields
   C. Recess
   D. right

   *Answer:* C) Recess. See RONR (12th ed.) §52:28 — “The more traditional aids to the crystallization of opinion in societies have been, simply, to take a Recess or to refer the matter to a committee—often a large committee composed of members representing differing views in the society, such as a committee of the whole or one of its alternate forms.”

**88.** `[C1334 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: If so, the officers elected at the first meeting are designated _____ and secretary pro tem— although the words pro tem are not used in addressing these officers.

**93.** `[C1340 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: _____ That it is the sense of this meeting that a society for… [the object of the proposed society] now be formed [or “shall now be formed”].

   *Answer:* Resolved, — “Resolved, That it is the sense of this meeting that a society for… [the object of the proposed society] now be formed [or “shall now be formed”].”. See RONR (12th ed.) §54:6 — “Resolved, That it is the sense of this meeting that a society for… [the object of the proposed society] now be formed [or “shall now be formed”].”

**94.** `[C1340 · Applied multiple-choice]` Fill in the blank: _____ That it is the sense of this meeting that a society for… [the object of the proposed society] now be formed [or “shall now be formed”].
   A. order of
   B. Resolved,
   C. Lay on the
   D. insert

   *Answer:* B) Resolved,. See RONR (12th ed.) §54:6 — “Resolved, That it is the sense of this meeting that a society for… [the object of the proposed society] now be formed [or “shall now be formed”].”

**95.** `[C1351 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: Since a complete set of bylaws is commonly considered _____ (see 28), the chair states the question as follows:

   *Answer:* by article or section — “Since a complete set of bylaws is commonly considered by article or section (see 28), the chair states the question as follows:”. See RONR (12th ed.) §54:17 — “Since a complete set of bylaws is commonly considered by article or section (see 28), the chair states the question as follows:”

**96.** `[C1351 · Applied multiple-choice]` Fill in the blank: Since a complete set of bylaws is commonly considered _____ (see 28), the chair states the question as follows:
   A. quarterly time interval
   B. by article or section
   C. at a point established in the
   D. quorum

   *Answer:* B) by article or section. See RONR (12th ed.) §54:17 — “Since a complete set of bylaws is commonly considered by article or section (see 28), the chair states the question as follows:”

**97.** `[C1366 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: In the case of an unincorporated society, a resolution should be prepared, such as: “_____ That the X Society be dissolved as of March 31, 20.” This resolution may be preceded by a preamble setting forth the reasons for the dissolution.

   *Answer:* Resolved, — “In the case of an unincorporated society, a resolution should be prepared, such as: “Resolved, That the X Society be dissolved as of March 31, 20.” This resolution may be preceded by a preamble setting forth the reasons for the dissolution.”. See RONR (12th ed.) §55:6 — “In the case of an unincorporated society, a resolution should be prepared, such as: “Resolved, That the X Society be dissolved as of March 31, 20.” This resolution may be preceded by a preamble setting forth the reasons for the dissolution.”

**98.** `[C1366 · Applied multiple-choice]` Fill in the blank: In the case of an unincorporated society, a resolution should be prepared, such as: “_____ That the X Society be dissolved as of March 31, 20.” This resolution may be preceded by a preamble setting forth the reasons for the dissolution.
   A. order of business, agenda,
   B. Reconsider and Enter on the Minutes
   C. Resolved,
   D. rules of order

   *Answer:* C) Resolved,. See RONR (12th ed.) §55:6 — “In the case of an unincorporated society, a resolution should be prepared, such as: “Resolved, That the X Society be dissolved as of March 31, 20.” This resolution may be preceded by a preamble setting forth the reasons for the dissolution.”

**99.** `[C1368 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: The constitution and/or _____ of a society, as explained in 2, contains its own basic rules that relate to itself as an organization, except for what must be included in the corporate charter of an incorporated society.

   *Answer:* bylaws — “The constitution and/or bylaws of a society, as explained in 2, contains its own basic rules that relate to itself as an organization, except for what must be included in the corporate charter of an incorporated society.”. See RONR (12th ed.) §56:1 — “The constitution and/or bylaws of a society, as explained in 2, contains its own basic rules that relate to itself as an organization, except for what must be included in the corporate charter of an incorporated society.”

**100.** `[C1368 · Applied multiple-choice]` Fill in the blank: The constitution and/or _____ of a society, as explained in 2, contains its own basic rules that relate to itself as an organization, except for what must be included in the corporate charter of an incorporated society.
   A. Executive Board
   B. bylaws
   C. by paragraph or
   D. Point

   *Answer:* B) bylaws. See RONR (12th ed.) §56:1 — “The constitution and/or bylaws of a society, as explained in 2, contains its own basic rules that relate to itself as an organization, except for what must be included in the corporate charter of an incorporated society.”

**101.** `[C1368 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: Under the preferred practice for ordinary societies today, the constitution and the bylaws— once usually separate—are now combined in a single instrument, referred to in this book as the bylaws (although in some organizations called the _____ or—even though only one document—the constitution and bylaws).

   *Answer:* constitution, — “Under the preferred practice for ordinary societies today, the constitution and the bylaws— once usually separate—are now combined in a single instrument, referred to in this book as the bylaws (although in some organizations called the constitution, or—even though only one document—the constitution and bylaws).”. See RONR (12th ed.) §56:1 — “Under the preferred practice for ordinary societies today, the constitution and the bylaws— once usually separate—are now combined in a single instrument, referred to in this book as the bylaws (although in some organizations called the constitution, or—even though only one document—the constitution and bylaws).”

**102.** `[C1368 · Applied multiple-choice]` Fill in the blank: Under the preferred practice for ordinary societies today, the constitution and the bylaws— once usually separate—are now combined in a single instrument, referred to in this book as the bylaws (although in some organizations called the _____ or—even though only one document—the constitution and bylaws).
   A. constitution,
   B. house of delegates
   C. question is pending
   D. carried

   *Answer:* A) constitution,. See RONR (12th ed.) §56:1 — “Under the preferred practice for ordinary societies today, the constitution and the bylaws— once usually separate—are now combined in a single instrument, referred to in this book as the bylaws (although in some organizations called the constitution, or—even though only one document—the constitution and bylaws).”

**103.** `[C1368 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: Under the preferred practice for ordinary societies today, the constitution and the bylaws— once usually separate—are now combined in a single instrument, referred to in this book as the bylaws (although in some organizations called the constitution, or—even though only one document—the _____).

   *Answer:* constitution and bylaws — “Under the preferred practice for ordinary societies today, the constitution and the bylaws— once usually separate—are now combined in a single instrument, referred to in this book as the bylaws (although in some organizations called the constitution, or—even though only one document—the constitution and bylaws).”. See RONR (12th ed.) §56:1 — “Under the preferred practice for ordinary societies today, the constitution and the bylaws— once usually separate—are now combined in a single instrument, referred to in this book as the bylaws (although in some organizations called the constitution, or—even though only one document—the constitution and bylaws).”

**104.** `[C1397 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: To accomplish the first alternative, the bylaws may provide that officers “shall hold office for a term of year(s) _____ until their successors are elected.” To accomplish the second alternative, the bylaws may provide that officers “shall hold office for a term of year(s) or until their successors are elected.” (Emphases added.) Because the significant difference in effect between the use of “and” and “or” is unlikely to be clear to most members, it may be desirable (although it is not essential) to add an explanatory sentence, such as:

   *Answer:* and — “To accomplish the first alternative, the bylaws may provide that officers “shall hold office for a term of year(s) and until their successors are elected.” To accomplish the second alternative, the bylaws may provide that officers “shall hold office for a term of year(s) or until their successors are elected.” (Emphases added.) Because the significant difference in effect between the use of “and” and “or” is unlikely to be clear to most members, it may be desirable (although it is not essential) to add an explanatory sentence, such as:”. See RONR (12th ed.) §56:30 — “To accomplish the first alternative, the bylaws may provide that officers “shall hold office for a term of year(s) and until their successors are elected.” To accomplish the second alternative, the bylaws may provide that officers “shall hold office for a term of year(s) or until their successors are elected.” (Emphases added.) Because the significant difference in effect between the use of “and” and “or” is unlikely to be clear to most members, it may be desirable (although it is not essential) to add an explanatory sentence, such as:”

**105.** `[C1397 · Applied multiple-choice]` Fill in the blank: To accomplish the first alternative, the bylaws may provide that officers “shall hold office for a term of year(s) _____ until their successors are elected.” To accomplish the second alternative, the bylaws may provide that officers “shall hold office for a term of year(s) or until their successors are elected.” (Emphases added.) Because the significant difference in effect between the use of “and” and “or” is unlikely to be clear to most members, it may be desirable (although it is not essential) to add an explanatory sentence, such as:
   A. arising out of
   B. and
   C. constitution,
   D. advance

   *Answer:* B) and. See RONR (12th ed.) §56:30 — “To accomplish the first alternative, the bylaws may provide that officers “shall hold office for a term of year(s) and until their successors are elected.” To accomplish the second alternative, the bylaws may provide that officers “shall hold office for a term of year(s) or until their successors are elected.” (Emphases added.) Because the significant difference in effect between the use of “and” and “or” is unlikely to be clear to most members, it may be desirable (although it is not essential) to add an explanatory sentence, such as:”

**106.** `[C1406 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: Organizations may sometimes give varied names to their full boards, such as _____ of Trustees, Board of Governors, Administrative Council, etc.

   *Answer:* Board of Managers, Board — “Organizations may sometimes give varied names to their full boards, such as Board of Managers, Board of Trustees, Board of Governors, Administrative Council, etc.”. See RONR (12th ed.) §56:40 — “Organizations may sometimes give varied names to their full boards, such as Board of Managers, Board of Trustees, Board of Governors, Administrative Council, etc.”

**107.** `[C1406 · Applied multiple-choice]` Fill in the blank: Organizations may sometimes give varied names to their full boards, such as _____ of Trustees, Board of Governors, Administrative Council, etc.
   A. Board of Managers, Board
   B. Call of the House,
   C. Designating the Committee Chairman
   D. who should be engaged well in

   *Answer:* A) Board of Managers, Board. See RONR (12th ed.) §56:40 — “Organizations may sometimes give varied names to their full boards, such as Board of Managers, Board of Trustees, Board of Governors, Administrative Council, etc.”

**108.** `[C1416 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: The bylaws should always prescribe _____ procedure for their amendment, and such provision should always require at least that advance notice be given in a specified manner, and that the amendment be approved by a two-thirds vote.

   *Answer:* the — “The bylaws should always prescribe the procedure for their amendment, and such provision should always require at least that advance notice be given in a specified manner, and that the amendment be approved by a two-thirds vote.”. See RONR (12th ed.) §56:50 — “The bylaws should always prescribe the procedure for their amendment, and such provision should always require at least that advance notice be given in a specified manner, and that the amendment be approved by a two-thirds vote.”

**109.** `[C1416 · Applied multiple-choice]` Fill in the blank: The bylaws should always prescribe _____ procedure for their amendment, and such provision should always require at least that advance notice be given in a specified manner, and that the amendment be approved by a two-thirds vote.
   A. class
   B. renewed
   C. the
   D. debate

   *Answer:* C) the. See RONR (12th ed.) §56:50 — “The bylaws should always prescribe the procedure for their amendment, and such provision should always require at least that advance notice be given in a specified manner, and that the amendment be approved by a two-thirds vote.”

**110.** `[C1422 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: The wording of this article should avoid redundant phraseology such as “_____, alter, add to, or repeal,” or “alter or amend,” or “amend or in any way change.” The word amend covers any change, whether a word or a paragraph is to be added, struck out, or replaced, or whether a new set of articles is to be substituted for the old one.

   *Answer:* amend — “The wording of this article should avoid redundant phraseology such as “amend, alter, add to, or repeal,” or “alter or amend,” or “amend or in any way change.” The word amend covers any change, whether a word or a paragraph is to be added, struck out, or replaced, or whether a new set of articles is to be substituted for the old one.”. See RONR (12th ed.) §56:56 — “The wording of this article should avoid redundant phraseology such as “amend, alter, add to, or repeal,” or “alter or amend,” or “amend or in any way change.” The word amend covers any change, whether a word or a paragraph is to be added, struck out, or replaced, or whether a new set of articles is to be substituted for the old one.”

**111.** `[C1422 · Applied multiple-choice]` Fill in the blank: The wording of this article should avoid redundant phraseology such as “_____, alter, add to, or repeal,” or “alter or amend,” or “amend or in any way change.” The word amend covers any change, whether a word or a paragraph is to be added, struck out, or replaced, or whether a new set of articles is to be substituted for the old one.
   A. adhere
   B. Special Orders
   C. amend
   D. Rescind

   *Answer:* C) amend. See RONR (12th ed.) §56:56 — “The wording of this article should avoid redundant phraseology such as “amend, alter, add to, or repeal,” or “alter or amend,” or “amend or in any way change.” The word amend covers any change, whether a word or a paragraph is to be added, struck out, or replaced, or whether a new set of articles is to be substituted for the old one.”

**112.** `[C1430 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: ARTICLE VIII: PARLIAMENTARY AUTHORITY The rules contained in the current edition of _____ of Order Newly Revised shall govern the Society in all cases to which they are applicable and in which they are not inconsistent with these bylaws and any special rules of order the Society may adopt.

   *Answer:* Robert’s Rules — “ARTICLE VIII: PARLIAMENTARY AUTHORITY The rules contained in the current edition of Robert’s Rules of Order Newly Revised shall govern the Society in all cases to which they are applicable and in which they are not inconsistent with these bylaws and any special rules of order the Society may adopt.”. See RONR (12th ed.) §56:66 — “ARTICLE VIII: PARLIAMENTARY AUTHORITY The rules contained in the current edition of Robert’s Rules of Order Newly Revised shall govern the Society in all cases to which they are applicable and in which they are not inconsistent with these bylaws and any special rules of order the Society may adopt.”

   A. Call
   B. Robert’s Rules
   C. motion
   D. exhausted

   *Answer:* B) Robert’s Rules. See RONR (12th ed.) §56:66 — “ARTICLE VIII: PARLIAMENTARY AUTHORITY The rules contained in the current edition of Robert’s Rules of Order Newly Revised shall govern the Society in all cases to which they are applicable and in which they are not inconsistent with these bylaws and any special rules of order the Society may adopt.”

**114.** `[C1430 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: ARTICLE VIII: PARLIAMENTARY AUTHORITY The rules contained in the current edition of Robert’s Rules _____ shall govern the Society in all cases to which they are applicable and in which they are not inconsistent with these bylaws and any special rules of order the Society may adopt.

   *Answer:* of Order Newly Revised — “ARTICLE VIII: PARLIAMENTARY AUTHORITY The rules contained in the current edition of Robert’s Rules of Order Newly Revised shall govern the Society in all cases to which they are applicable and in which they are not inconsistent with these bylaws and any special rules of order the Society may adopt.”. See RONR (12th ed.) §56:66 — “ARTICLE VIII: PARLIAMENTARY AUTHORITY The rules contained in the current edition of Robert’s Rules of Order Newly Revised shall govern the Society in all cases to which they are applicable and in which they are not inconsistent with these bylaws and any special rules of order the Society may adopt.”

**115.** `[C1430 · Applied multiple-choice]` Fill in the blank: ARTICLE VIII: PARLIAMENTARY AUTHORITY The rules contained in the current edition of Robert’s Rules _____ shall govern the Society in all cases to which they are applicable and in which they are not inconsistent with these bylaws and any special rules of order the Society may adopt.

**116.** `[C1433 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: A motion to amend the bylaws is a particular case of the motion to _____ (35);

   *Answer:* Amend Something Previously Adopted — “A motion to amend the bylaws is a particular case of the motion to Amend Something Previously Adopted (35);”. See RONR (12th ed.) §57:1 — “A motion to amend the bylaws is a particular case of the motion to Amend Something Previously Adopted (35);”

**117.** `[C1433 · Applied multiple-choice]` Fill in the blank: A motion to amend the bylaws is a particular case of the motion to _____ (35);
   A. Amend Something Previously Adopted
   B. refrain
   C. from office
   D. Order Newly Revised

   *Answer:* A) Amend Something Previously Adopted. See RONR (12th ed.) §57:1 — “A motion to amend the bylaws is a particular case of the motion to Amend Something Previously Adopted (35);”

**118.** `[C1435 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: If only an isolated change is to be made in the bylaws, it can be treated as any motion to _____ Something Previously Adopted (35), subject to the particular rules indicated immediately above.

See RONR (12th ed.) §57:3 — “If only an isolated change is to be made in the bylaws, it can be treated as any motion to Amend Something Previously Adopted (35), subject to the particular rules indicated immediately above.”

   *Answer:* D) Amend. See RONR (12th ed.) §57:3 — “If only an isolated change is to be made in the bylaws, it can be treated as any motion to Amend Something Previously Adopted (35), subject to the particular rules indicated immediately above.”

**120.** `[C1435 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: If only an isolated change is to be made in the bylaws, it can be treated as any motion to Amend _____ (35), subject to the particular rules indicated immediately above.

**122.** `[C1437 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: Changes of the bylaws that are so extensive and general that they are scattered throughout the bylaws should be effected through the substitution of an entirely new set of bylaws, called a _____.

   *Answer:* revision — “Changes of the bylaws that are so extensive and general that they are scattered throughout the bylaws should be effected through the substitution of an entirely new set of bylaws, called a revision.”. See RONR (12th ed.) §57:5 — “Changes of the bylaws that are so extensive and general that they are scattered throughout the bylaws should be effected through the substitution of an entirely new set of bylaws, called a revision.”

**123.** `[C1437 · Applied multiple-choice]` Fill in the blank: Changes of the bylaws that are so extensive and general that they are scattered throughout the bylaws should be effected through the substitution of an entirely new set of bylaws, called a _____.
   A. parliamentary law
   B. revision
   C. takes precedence of
   D. two-thirds vote

   *Answer:* B) revision. See RONR (12th ed.) §57:5 — “Changes of the bylaws that are so extensive and general that they are scattered throughout the bylaws should be effected through the substitution of an entirely new set of bylaws, called a revision.”

**124.** `[C1438 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: An affirmative vote adopting such an arrangement is not subject to a motion to _____ nor may a later, separate amendment be offered as a substitute for a pending one.

   *Answer:* Reconsider, — “An affirmative vote adopting such an arrangement is not subject to a motion to Reconsider, nor may a later, separate amendment be offered as a substitute for a pending one.”. See RONR (12th ed.) §57:6 — “An affirmative vote adopting such an arrangement is not subject to a motion to Reconsider, nor may a later, separate amendment be offered as a substitute for a pending one.”

**125.** `[C1438 · Applied multiple-choice]` Fill in the blank: An affirmative vote adopting such an arrangement is not subject to a motion to _____ nor may a later, separate amendment be offered as a substitute for a pending one.
   A. receives
   B. show of hands
   C. Reconsider,
   D. house of delegates

   *Answer:* C) Reconsider,. See RONR (12th ed.) §57:6 — “An affirmative vote adopting such an arrangement is not subject to a motion to Reconsider, nor may a later, separate amendment be offered as a substitute for a pending one.”

**126.** `[C1439 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: and it is the bylaw language _____ rather than the previous language, which any bylaw amendments subsequently considered would now propose to modify.

   *Answer:* as thus amended, — “and it is the bylaw language as thus amended, rather than the previous language, which any bylaw amendments subsequently considered would now propose to modify.”. See RONR (12th ed.) §57:7 — “and it is the bylaw language as thus amended, rather than the previous language, which any bylaw amendments subsequently considered would now propose to modify.”

**127.** `[C1439 · Applied multiple-choice]` Fill in the blank: and it is the bylaw language _____ rather than the previous language, which any bylaw amendments subsequently considered would now propose to modify.

   B. Rules
   C. as thus amended,
   D. the one

   *Answer:* C) as thus amended,. See RONR (12th ed.) §57:7 — “and it is the bylaw language as thus amended, rather than the previous language, which any bylaw amendments subsequently considered would now propose to modify.”

**128.** `[C1449 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: It is important to note that, although the time when a bylaw amendment _____ can be delayed by the assembly, the amendment becomes part of the bylaws immediately upon adoption.

   *Answer:* takes effect — “It is important to note that, although the time when a bylaw amendment takes effect can be delayed by the assembly, the amendment becomes part of the bylaws immediately upon adoption.”. See RONR (12th ed.) §57:17 — “It is important to note that, although the time when a bylaw amendment takes effect can be delayed by the assembly, the amendment becomes part of the bylaws immediately upon adoption.”

**129.** `[C1449 · Applied multiple-choice]` Fill in the blank: It is important to note that, although the time when a bylaw amendment _____ can be delayed by the assembly, the amendment becomes part of the bylaws immediately upon adoption.
   A. takes effect
   B. exact
   C. Reference Committee
   D. said

   *Answer:* A) takes effect. See RONR (12th ed.) §57:17 — “It is important to note that, although the time when a bylaw amendment takes effect can be delayed by the assembly, the amendment becomes part of the bylaws immediately upon adoption.”

**130.** `[C1452 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: As commonly understood in parliamentary law and as used in this book, the word convention refers to an assembly of _____ (other than a permanently constituted public lawmaking body), who are usually chosen specially for each session as representatives of the constituent units or subdivisions within a larger group of people, to sit as a single deliberative body acting in the name of the entire group.

   *Answer:* delegates — “As commonly understood in parliamentary law and as used in this book, the word convention refers to an assembly of delegates (other than a permanently constituted public lawmaking body), who are usually chosen specially for each session as representatives of the constituent units or subdivisions within a larger group of people, to sit as a single deliberative body acting in the name of the entire group.”. See RONR (12th ed.) §58:1 — “As commonly understood in parliamentary law and as used in this book, the word convention refers to an assembly of delegates (other than a permanently constituted public lawmaking body), who are usually chosen specially for each session as representatives of the constituent units or subdivisions within a larger group of people, to sit as a single deliberative body acting in the name of the entire group.”

**131.** `[C1452 · Applied multiple-choice]` Fill in the blank: As commonly understood in parliamentary law and as used in this book, the word convention refers to an assembly of _____ (other than a permanently constituted public lawmaking body), who are usually chosen specially for each session as representatives of the constituent units or subdivisions within a larger group of people, to sit as a single deliberative body acting in the name of the entire group.
   A. delegates
   B. Consider Seriatim
   C. are
   D. after the floor

   *Answer:* A) delegates. See RONR (12th ed.) §58:1 — “As commonly understood in parliamentary law and as used in this book, the word convention refers to an assembly of delegates (other than a permanently constituted public lawmaking body), who are usually chosen specially for each session as representatives of the constituent units or subdivisions within a larger group of people, to sit as a single deliberative body acting in the name of the entire group.”

**135.** `[C1453 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: The term house of delegates, or _____ is often applied particularly in the case of learned or professional associations, to distinguish the voting body of delegates from large numbers of other members of the constituent units, who come to the convention to attend seminars, workshops, educational or social activities, or the like.

   *Answer:* house of representatives, — “The term house of delegates, or house of representatives, is often applied particularly in the case of learned or professional associations, to distinguish the voting body of delegates from large numbers of other members of the constituent units, who come to the convention to attend seminars, workshops, educational or social activities, or the like.”. See RONR (12th ed.) §58:2 — “The term house of delegates, or house of representatives, is often applied particularly in the case of learned or professional associations, to distinguish the voting body of delegates from large numbers of other members of the constituent units, who come to the convention to attend seminars, workshops, educational or social activities, or the like.”

**141.** `[C1469 · Applied multiple-choice]` Fill in the blank: a meeting of this kind is usually called a _____.

**150.** `[C1482 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: Before a convention can transact any other _____, it must officially form itself into a single voting body—which is done at the first business meeting.

**151.** `[C1482 · Applied multiple-choice]` Fill in the blank: Before a convention can transact any other _____, it must officially form itself into a single voting body—which is done at the first business meeting.

   *Answer:* C) business. See RONR (12th ed.) §59:10 — “Before a convention can transact any other business, it must officially form itself into a single voting body—which is done at the first business meeting.”

**152.** `[C1519 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: The rules contained in the current edition of Robert’s Rules of _____ shall govern the convention in all cases to which they are applicable and in which they are not inconsistent with the bylaws of the Society [or “Federation,” “Association,” etc.] and these standing rules.

   *Answer:* Order Newly Revised — “The rules contained in the current edition of Robert’s Rules of Order Newly Revised shall govern the convention in all cases to which they are applicable and in which they are not inconsistent with the bylaws of the Society [or “Federation,” “Association,” etc.] and these standing rules.”. See RONR (12th ed.) §59:47 — “The rules contained in the current edition of Robert’s Rules of Order Newly Revised shall govern the convention in all cases to which they are applicable and in which they are not inconsistent with the bylaws of the Society [or “Federation,” “Association,” etc.] and these standing rules.”

**153.** `[C1519 · Applied multiple-choice]` Fill in the blank: The rules contained in the current edition of Robert’s Rules of _____ shall govern the convention in all cases to which they are applicable and in which they are not inconsistent with the bylaws of the Society [or “Federation,” “Association,” etc.] and these standing rules.

   B. Subsidiary motions
   C. Order Newly Revised
   D. representatives

   *Answer:* C) Order Newly Revised. See RONR (12th ed.) §59:47 — “The rules contained in the current edition of Robert’s Rules of Order Newly Revised shall govern the convention in all cases to which they are applicable and in which they are not inconsistent with the bylaws of the Society [or “Federation,” “Association,” etc.] and these standing rules.”

**154.** `[C1519 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: The rules contained in the current edition of _____ Order Newly Revised shall govern the convention in all cases to which they are applicable and in which they are not inconsistent with the bylaws of the Society [or “Federation,” “Association,” etc.] and these standing rules.

   *Answer:* Robert’s Rules of — “The rules contained in the current edition of Robert’s Rules of Order Newly Revised shall govern the convention in all cases to which they are applicable and in which they are not inconsistent with the bylaws of the Society [or “Federation,” “Association,” etc.] and these standing rules.”. See RONR (12th ed.) §59:47 — “The rules contained in the current edition of Robert’s Rules of Order Newly Revised shall govern the convention in all cases to which they are applicable and in which they are not inconsistent with the bylaws of the Society [or “Federation,” “Association,” etc.] and these standing rules.”

**155.** `[C1519 · Applied multiple-choice]` Fill in the blank: The rules contained in the current edition of _____ Order Newly Revised shall govern the convention in all cases to which they are applicable and in which they are not inconsistent with the bylaws of the Society [or “Federation,” “Association,” etc.] and these standing rules.
   A. secondary amendment
   B. Form and
   C. when no question is
   D. Robert’s Rules of

   *Answer:* D) Robert’s Rules of. See RONR (12th ed.) §59:47 — “The rules contained in the current edition of Robert’s Rules of Order Newly Revised shall govern the convention in all cases to which they are applicable and in which they are not inconsistent with the bylaws of the Society [or “Federation,” “Association,” etc.] and these standing rules.”

**156.** `[C1520 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: When the program is adopted by the voting body, with or without amendment, it becomes the _____ of the entire convention session (41).

   *Answer:* order of business — “When the program is adopted by the voting body, with or without amendment, it becomes the order of business of the entire convention session (41).”. See RONR (12th ed.) §59:48 — “When the program is adopted by the voting body, with or without amendment, it becomes the order of business of the entire convention session (41).”

**157.** `[C1520 · Applied multiple-choice]` Fill in the blank: When the program is adopted by the voting body, with or without amendment, it becomes the _____ of the entire convention session (41).
   A. order of business
   B. standing rule of
   C. Debate,
   D. seem

   *Answer:* A) order of business. See RONR (12th ed.) §59:48 — “When the program is adopted by the voting body, with or without amendment, it becomes the order of business of the entire convention session (41).”

**158.** `[C1526 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: The second part, listing the items or classes of business set for each meeting, is then known as the _____ (41).

   *Answer:* agenda — “The second part, listing the items or classes of business set for each meeting, is then known as the agenda (41).”. See RONR (12th ed.) §59:54 — “The second part, listing the items or classes of business set for each meeting, is then known as the agenda (41).”

**159.** `[C1526 · Applied multiple-choice]` Fill in the blank: The second part, listing the items or classes of business set for each meeting, is then known as the _____ (41).
   A. assemblies
   B. agenda
   C. general order
   D. brought before the assembly

   *Answer:* B) agenda. See RONR (12th ed.) §59:54 — “The second part, listing the items or classes of business set for each meeting, is then known as the agenda (41).”

**160.** `[C1539 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: The Resolutions Committee—also sometimes called the Reference Committee or, in certain cases described below, the _____—has as its basic purpose the screening of all original main motions (10) that have not been screened by another committee and that come—or are to come—before the convention.

   *Answer:* Platform Committee — “The Resolutions Committee—also sometimes called the Reference Committee or, in certain cases described below, the Platform Committee—has as its basic purpose the screening of all original main motions (10) that have not been screened by another committee and that come—or are to come—before the convention.”. See RONR (12th ed.) §59:67 — “The Resolutions Committee—also sometimes called the Reference Committee or, in certain cases described below, the Platform Committee—has as its basic purpose the screening of all original main motions (10) that have not been screened by another committee and that come—or are to come—before the convention.”

**161.** `[C1539 · Applied multiple-choice]` Fill in the blank: The Resolutions Committee—also sometimes called the Reference Committee or, in certain cases described below, the _____—has as its basic purpose the screening of all original main motions (10) that have not been screened by another committee and that come—or are to come—before the convention.
   A. different subjects
   B. Platform Committee
   C. refrain
   D. rising

   *Answer:* B) Platform Committee. See RONR (12th ed.) §59:67 — “The Resolutions Committee—also sometimes called the Reference Committee or, in certain cases described below, the Platform Committee—has as its basic purpose the screening of all original main motions (10) that have not been screened by another committee and that come—or are to come—before the convention.”

**162.** `[C1539 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: The Resolutions Committee—also sometimes called the _____ or, in certain cases described below, the Platform Committee—has as its basic purpose the screening of all original main motions (10) that have not been screened by another committee and that come—or are to come—before the convention.

   *Answer:* Reference Committee — “The Resolutions Committee—also sometimes called the Reference Committee or, in certain cases described below, the Platform Committee—has as its basic purpose the screening of all original main motions (10) that have not been screened by another committee and that come—or are to come—before the convention.”. See RONR (12th ed.) §59:67 — “The Resolutions Committee—also sometimes called the Reference Committee or, in certain cases described below, the Platform Committee—has as its basic purpose the screening of all original main motions (10) that have not been screened by another committee and that come—or are to come—before the convention.”

**163.** `[C1539 · Applied multiple-choice]` Fill in the blank: The Resolutions Committee—also sometimes called the _____ or, in certain cases described below, the Platform Committee—has as its basic purpose the screening of all original main motions (10) that have not been screened by another committee and that come—or are to come—before the convention.
   A. renewed
   B. congress, conference, convocation,
   C. Reference Committee
   D. financial secretary

   *Answer:* C) Reference Committee. See RONR (12th ed.) §59:67 — “The Resolutions Committee—also sometimes called the Reference Committee or, in certain cases described below, the Platform Committee—has as its basic purpose the screening of all original main motions (10) that have not been screened by another committee and that come—or are to come—before the convention.”

**164.** `[C1549 · Cloze recall]` Fill the blank: In the body of the paper, each paragraph, instead of opening with the enacting words “_____ That,” begins with a verb denoting an attitude or position—for example, “Affirms…,” “Assures…,” “Condemns…,” “Calls upon…,” and the like.

   *Answer:* Resolved, — “In the body of the paper, each paragraph, instead of opening with the enacting words “Resolved, That,” begins with a verb denoting an attitude or position—for example, “Affirms…,” “Assures…,” “Condemns…,” “Calls upon…,” and the like.”. See RONR (12th ed.) §59:77 — “In the body of the paper, each paragraph, instead of opening with the enacting words “Resolved, That,” begins with a verb denoting an attitude or position—for example, “Affirms…,” “Assures…,” “Condemns…,” “Calls upon…,” and the like.”

**165.** `[C1549 · Applied multiple-choice]` Fill in the blank: In the body of the paper, each paragraph, instead of opening with the enacting words “_____ That,” begins with a verb denoting an attitude or position—for example, “Affirms…,” “Assures…,” “Condemns…,” “Calls upon…,” and the like.
   A. executive committee
   B. Substitute
   C. the
   D. Resolved,

   *Answer:* D) Resolved,. See RONR (12th ed.) §59:77 — “In the body of the paper, each paragraph, instead of opening with the enacting words “Resolved, That,” begins with a verb denoting an attitude or position—for example, “Affirms…,” “Assures…,” “Condemns…,” “Calls upon…,” and the like.”
